chapters.summary: Council staff instructs attendees to take seats, silence devices, and reminds them this is a government proceeding with decorum rules.
chapters.title: Staff opens hearing with housekeeping instructions

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Folks in the balcony, please have a seat.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find a seat.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Health.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: As this time, please sign all cell phone and electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please keep the quorum in here.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: This government proceeding.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Lynn Schulman opens the hearing on Intro 943, sponsored by Councilmember Marte, to prohibit horse-drawn cabs. She recognizes Speaker Menin and multiple council members in attendance.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman opens hearing on Intro 943 to ban horse-drawn carriages

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Lynn Schulman, Chair of the New York City Council's Committee on Health.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Today, the Committee on Health will hear Introduction 943, sponsored by Councilmember Marte, in relation to prohibiting the operation of horse-drawn cabs.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I would like to recognize that we have been joined by Speaker Menon and by Councilmembers

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Marte, Gennaro, Wong, Epstein, De La Rosa, Wilson, and Areola.

chapters.summary: Schulman recounts the tragic death of 18-year-old Romanch Mahajan, who fell from a horse carriage in Central Park when the driver stepped away to take a photo and the horse bolted.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman describes Romanch Mahajan's death in Central Park carriage accident

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Last month, Ramanj Mahajan and his family were visiting New York City from India when they took a horse carriage ride in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: During the ride, the carriage driver stepped away from the carriage to take a photograph of the family.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The horse bolted, causing...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Romance's mother, Priya, to fall out of the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Romance then either jumped or fell from the carriage, suffering a severe head injury.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He tragically died later that day.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: My heart goes out to the Mahajan family for the

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: unimaginable loss they have endured.

chapters.summary: The committee observes a moment of silence in memory of Romanch Mahajan.
chapters.title: Moment of silence for Romanch Mahajan

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please join me in a moment of silence in memory of Ramach.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I will now turn it over to Speaker Menon for her opening remarks.

chapters.summary: Speaker Julie Menin delivers opening remarks, thanking Chair Schulman and Councilmember Marte, and addressing the Mahajan family directly with condolences for Romanch's death.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin offers condolences to Mahajan family

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm Julie Menon, Speaker of the New York City Council, and I want to welcome all of you to this important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I first of all want to thank my colleague, our Health Chair, Councilmember Shulman, for convening today's hearing, and I want to thank my colleague, Councilmember Marche, the sponsor of Ramach's law.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So to the Mahajan family, there are no words for Ramansha's death.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: When I spoke to you this morning and I heard the pain in your voice, there is nothing we can do to bring him back.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: But what we can do is honor his legacy, which is what we're doing today at this hearing to make sure that this type of tragedy never, ever happens again.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so I just want to thank you for your courage in being here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know this is not easy, and we're going to hear from you in a few minutes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And our condolences extend to your whole family.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know when a young person is taken from us, cut off in their prime.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: of life as Ramach was, with the world of potential going to college ahead of him.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It is, and I say this as a mom, it's like really deeply heart-wrenching.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: When that death was avoidable, where he could be alive if only we had acted sooner, it is a call to action for all people of good conscience.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: For your family, for all the families, and for our city, we are here to ensure that this never happens again.

chapters.summary: Menin discusses the 40-year history of the horse carriage debate, acknowledges the industry's cultural significance and immigrant workforce, and commits to protecting workers and horses through the transition.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on history of horse carriage debate and worker protections

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This issue is not new to our city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It's certainly not new to city government.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: There are many people who are in these chambers who've been working on this issue for many, many years, but it has never come to fruition.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The matter of horse carriages or maintenance or safety and operation has come up under previous mayors and previous city councils every year for almost four decades.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The time to act is now.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This is not a simple issue because this industry is historically and culturally significant.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: For years, horse carriages have been synonymous with our iconic world-class tourism.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The rides they provide have given visitors an opportunity to slow down and take in the sights of our city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And they have given hundreds of hardworking immigrant New Yorkers the dignity of a good-paying job.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These are drivers who care deeply for their horses and who take the safety of their passengers and passengers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: pedestrians extremely seriously.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I do not take these considerations lightly.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Anytime there is possibility of ending an industry, the possibility of job losses for small business owners and employees, we obviously need to think carefully about that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm a former small business owner myself.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I understand the challenges and burdens of operating and working for one.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm committing here today that we are going to chart a path forward to protect the workers and ensure that they have good paying jobs moving forward,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: whether it be in the hospitality or tourism or other industry.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know that you are a vital thread of the fabric of this city and we will not exclude you from this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm also very committed to ensuring that horses involved are treated fairly and compassionately.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And we will ensure through this bill that we will guarantee their safety and well-being as well.

chapters.summary: Menin cites nearly 70% of New Yorkers supporting a phase-out, references scores of accidents, and frames the issue as both public safety and animal welfare, calling for a civil and respectful hearing.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on public safety concerns and growing support for ban

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that brings us to the pivotal crossroads where we stand today.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As public servants, we must respond to the concerns of our constituents, and we must recognize that the appetite towards banning horse carriages has grown significantly over the past four decades.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: New Yorkers have long been concerned about public safety.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: With scores of accidents and close calls involving horses, passengers,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Commuters, maintaining large and naturally skittish animals in our chaotic city is a dangerous combination.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And sadly, with Ramach's death, we have now reached a turning point.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We have also become a city that is overwhelmingly supportive of the humane treatment of animals.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And there have been many incidents involving horses collapsing in the hot, congested environment of our streets.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Accordingly, nearly 70% of New Yorkers are supportive of phasing out this practice.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And we want to be clear, the Council's focus is on ending this tradition while protecting the workers whose livelihoods depend on it, and we will ensure strong worker protections in this bill.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We can and we should have this conversation today with respect and with empathy, no matter where we stand on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I hope and I know that this hearing will maintain a tone of somberness, of sensitivity, and civility for everyone who has come here today.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to hearing from the administration, from the industry, from advocates, and most of all, from Ramach's family.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm also so incredibly sorry, as I said from the beginning, for your loss.

chapters.summary: Menin describes Romanch's heroic act of trying to protect his mother, says the law named in his honor will protect residents, tourists, and horses. She turns the hearing back to Chair Schulman.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin honors Romanch's heroism and calls for passage

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And for those of you that don't know this aspect, Ramant died in an act of heroism as he tried to protect

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: his mother.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Now a law named in his honor will protect countless others, residents, tourists, humans, and horses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: While there's nothing we can do to sadly change the tragedy of his death, passing his law is one way to seek justice for him and his family, to keep his blessed memory alive, and to carry his brave and compassionate spirit

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: forward.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I now want to turn it over to Councilmember Shulman again.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman calls the driver's conduct reckless and preventable, references prior horse deaths including Denise and Ryder, and outlines Intro 943's key provisions: no new licenses after June 2028, humane horse disposition, and workforce development programs.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman details Intro 943 provisions

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The circumstances surrounding Romantia's death are deeply disturbing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The driver's conduct was reckless, unacceptable, and this young man's death was entirely preventable.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This recent tragedy comes on the heels of other high-profile incidents in which carriage horses have broken free, collapsed, or died.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These include Denise, a carriage horse who died in June after eating a plant toxic to horses, and Ryder, a horse who collapsed in Hell's Kitchen in 2022 and was later euthanized.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These incidents have intensified a debate that has been taking place for years in our city about the welfare of carriage horses, the safety of our residents and visitors, the livelihoods of carriage drivers, and the future of the horse carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Today, we are discussing legislation that, if enacted, would wind down the horse carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Introduction 943 would prohibit the issuance of new horse carriage operator licenses after June 1, 2028, and would also prohibit the operation of horse-drawn cabs after that date.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The bill would also require the humane disposition of former carriage horses, including prohibiting their sale or transfer for slaughter or for use in another horse-drawn cab business.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Finally, the bill would require the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection and the Mayor's Office of Talent and Workforce Development to develop a job training program for former drivers and other workers involved in the industry,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: with the goal of facilitating their transition to other fields of employment.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This is an industry with a long history in New York City, and for generations, carriage drivers have built their livelihoods around this work.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But times are changing, and we cannot ignore these tragic incidents or the serious public safety concerns they have raised.

chapters.summary: Schulman discusses how Central Park has become more congested with pedestrians, bikes, e-bikes, and pedicabs, making horse carriage operation increasingly dangerous for all street users.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on changing city landscape and public safety risks

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The landscape of our city has changed dramatically.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Central Park and the surrounding streets are busier and more congested with pedestrians, bicycles, e-bikes, pedicabs, scooters, vehicles, and horse-drawn carriages increasingly competing for space.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: When a horse becomes distressed, collapses, or breaks free in this environment, the consequences are devastating, not only for the horse, but for drivers, passengers, and everyone sharing our streets and public spaces.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This is fundamentally an issue that greatly affects the public.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These concerns are not based on a single incident.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: For years, New Yorkers, advocates, elected officials, and others have raised questions about animal welfare and public safety within the horse carriage industry.

chapters.summary: Schulman emphasizes the need to protect both horses and workers, calls for civil proceedings, and thanks committee staff and her team for their extensive preparation.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on transition responsibilities and decorum

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Last month's devastating tragedy has forced us to confront these concerns in a new light and consider whether it is time for a new path forward.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: As we consider the future of this industry, we must carefully examine what a responsible transition would require, both for the horses and for the people whose livelihoods depend on this work.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We must consider how to protect the horses by placing them in safe and caring homes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We must also recognize the drivers and workers who have devoted years, and in some cases generations, to this industry, as well as the significant investments they have made in their carriage licenses.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We must consider how to provide drivers with meaningful opportunities for new employment.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: My hope is that we can bring people together around these shared responsibilities.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I know emotions are running high today on all sides of this issue, and I want to conclude by reminding everyone that although there may be strong opinions on this legislation and on the future of this industry, we must maintain a civil and respectful proceeding.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The purpose of this hearing is to ensure that all voices and all perspectives can be heard today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We must maintain decorum throughout the proceedings.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I would also like to thank the representatives for the administration for being here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank the committee staff, and I want to make mention the committee staff has worked an enormous amount of hours and an enormous amount of days and worked really hard on this, and so I really want to thank them from the bottom of my heart.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Chris Pepe, Josh Newman, Elizabeth Art for their hard work in preparing for this hearing, as well as my team, Jonathan Boucher, Kevin McAleer, and Sammy New.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Before I hand it over to Councilmember Marte for a brief statement on his legislation, I want to...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to let people know that you can request translation in the following languages, Spanish, Hindi, Tajik, Turkish, and Russian.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So if anybody needs anything, please let the sergeants know.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now I will turn it over to Councilmember Marte for a brief statement on his legislation.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Marte, the bill sponsor, thanks leadership and advocates, cites 128 documented carriage accidents in 34 years, multiple horse deaths, and argues the industry does not work in modern NYC.
chapters.title: Councilmember Marte sponsors Intro 943, cites 128 carriage accidents in 34 years

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I'd like to give a huge thank you to Speaker Menon and Chair Shulman for holding today's historic hearing, and to the thousands of advocates and everyday New Yorkers who for years have been demanding we take action on horse carriages in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: For decades, we've seen horse carriages live in cramped stables.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We've seen them walk through midtown traffic, breathing in exhaust.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We've seen them pull thousands of pounds in the heat and in the cold.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We've seen them get scared, run off, and die in the streets.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Every time an incident occurs, we are given an excuse.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We are told it's just an isolated incident and the industry is completely safe.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: But the reality is that in the past 34 years, there have been 128 documented horse carriage accidents.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Dirty horses have died.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Dozens of horses and people have been injured and hospitalized.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Vehicles have been damaged and destroyed.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And most recently, we lost the life of a young tourist, Ramash Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: As legislators, now is the time for serious accountability.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: While the City Council

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: has dragged its feet through endless debates, we now have the loss of a boy on our hand.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: These are not unrelated accidents, but a pattern of failure that proves this industry does not work in modern-day New York City.

chapters.summary: Marte systematically refutes industry arguments about training, hitching posts, and government oversight, noting the industry refused independent vet inspections and that drivers were documented leaving horses unattended even after Romanch's death.
chapters.title: Marte refutes industry safety reform arguments

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: During this hearing, we're going to hear a lot of more excuses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We'll be told that the industry is safe, yet somehow also needs safety reforms.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: If the drivers are just trained better, this will never happen again.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: That if we just add additional infrastructure to Central Park, such as hitching posts, that will solve the problem.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We'll be told that the issue is just the lack of government oversight.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And we'll be threatened that if we pass this bill, all the horses will be sent to the slaughterhouse.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: However, these excuses don't hold up.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: The Department of Health had tried to provide oversight over the carriage industry, but when the agency ordered independent veterinarians to inspect the stables and the horses, the workers refused to allow them to test their horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: That veterinarian found horses stuck in stables too small, covered in scarring, and exhibiting signs of many medical issues that could disqualify them from working.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: It was not the city who did not enforce.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: It was the industry that refused to comply.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: After 128 horse carriage accidents, it was only when someone was killed that additional training was provided to the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Legislation has never been required.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: to make better training.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Either the owners have ignored training their drivers or the training has not been working.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: For days after Romash's death, we were promised that drivers were going to be trained to never leave their horses unattended.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Drivers were documented doing exactly that.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: No amount of driver training will change the nature of horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: They can and will get scared and run off.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Even hitching posts, which we are told are the magic bullets to stop horse accidents, are not a real solution.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: A 1,500 pound horse will still get scared while tied to a hitching post.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Their halters can snap under extreme pressures and the horses can run off.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: They can even pull the post onto the ground.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: If the horse pulls back violently, he may fall with the carriage and capsize.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And even if the harness does not snap, the extreme pressures on the top of the head can result in serious and lasting injuries.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: It can even break their neck.

chapters.summary: Marte addresses the slaughter concern by noting a broad coalition of sanctuaries willing to pay full price for horses, and calls for passage of Romanch's Law to end the industry, find safe homes for horses, and transition workers.
chapters.title: Marte refutes slaughter argument and calls for passage of Romanch's Law

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Lastly, we must put to rest the idea that banning horse carriages will lead to the death of horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We are going to hear today from a broad coalition of sanctuaries and farms willing to pay owners full prices for their horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Sending the horses to the slaughterhouse is completely unnecessary.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: If we all work together, we can pass this bill, compensate the owners, let the horses live their lives on open pastures.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: The preventable death of Romash has proved

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: than that we can't reform our way out of this.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: But there's a way forward, and that is Ramash Law, Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: With this bill, we can end this inhumane industry, find safe homes for the horses, and transition workers to other jobs.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: My office has already been in discussion with City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And they're in the process of developing proposals for workforce development programs for workers.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Ramash Law is how we can truly honor Ramash, so we can offer his family more than just words, by action.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We can protect the horses, take care of the workers, and make sure that this tragedy never happens again, ever.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I look forward to today's discussion and urge my colleagues to sponsor this legislation so we can finally put an end to this form of legalized animal cruelty.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman recognizes newly arrived council members and introduces the Mahajan family testimony segment, beginning with Priya and Deepak Mahajan.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman recognizes new members and introduces family testimony

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member Marte.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I would now like to recognize we've been joined by Council Members Abreu and Narcisse and Felder.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Did I miss anybody?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Before we hear testimony from the administration, we will hear directly from Romancha's family who have joined us here in chambers and online to provide testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: First, we will hear from...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Priya Mahajan and Deepak Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Do we have?

chapters.summary: Deepak Mahajan, Romanch's father, delivers emotional testimony about the family's trip from India, the carriage ride, the horse bolting, his son's fatal head injury, and the devastating impact on the family.
chapters.title: Deepak Mahajan testifies about son Romanch's death

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Members of the council, we are romance mother and father Priya and Deepak Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We brought our son to the New York from India to celebrate romance finishing school.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: The day we landed, he found out that he had been accepted to one of the most prestigious universities in India.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Two days later, we took a carriage ride in Central Park and near the end of the ride,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: The driver stepped away from the horse to take our family picture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: The horse ran away out of control.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: I cannot properly describe to you the real truth here inside that carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: It was shaking, it was speeding, there was no one holding the reins.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: And we could do nothing but hold on to each other and scream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: My wife fell, Romesh tried to help his mother and he too fell.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Then he hit his hand on the ground and he did not move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Head to the ground.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He never moved again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: I kept shouting his name but he did not move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He was 18, 6 foot 2, obedient, smart, the boy who helped everyone whether he knew them or not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Who was always sharing his ideas with me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He wanted to build something of his own.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He died that day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He took his last breath in his mother's arm while he was in the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: His 11-year-old brother and I stood watching.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: This was the last year of his life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: No one planned for this or expected to happen to their child, but it happened to us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Now we live inside that day over and over again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We are shattered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Our whole family is shattered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: The pain does not go away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Every morning we wake up and think of nothing but the loss of our son.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We came to New York as a family of four.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We are learning hard by hard to cope up with life as we are three now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Our home is no longer the same.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We still have not unpacked romance luggage from that trip.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Every time we enter his room, we break down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: His books, his belongings, and his dreams are exactly where he left them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: His friends are shattered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: They had plans with him for college, for life, for the future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: They still struggle to believe that he's gone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Our younger son, Manik, who is now our only son, is unable to cope with the trauma and horror he witnessed that day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He watched his brother die.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: He lost not only his sibling, but his companion, his guide, and his best friend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: No child should ever have to carry memories like this for the rest of their life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: And for us, every night is a night here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: We close our eyes and relive that character, the screams, the helplessness, and the moment our son's life ended before ours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: I want you to imagine what it is like watching her son die right in front of you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: I would not wish one hour of any of this on any family this worth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Ravanch was wearing a red striped shirt and blue jean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Sometimes when he thinks back to those final moments, it feels as though he was wrapped in the color of American flag.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: A young boy who came to America full of dreams and left us for too soon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: This was not an unpredictable accident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: It was a safety violation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: A system failure to protect the people who visit our city, your city, and everyone responsible has admitted the driver should never have left that carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: And still, the ride went back on the streets while we were planning our son's funeral.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: As if our boy was a temporary inconvenience, but we will not let that be Roman's legacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Now, we are asking you plainly, act now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: A version of this law sat before this council last year, and if it had passed, the carriages would have stopped on June 1st, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Roman died on June 17th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: If the city council has passed the law banning horse-drawn parallel ridges last year, our son will still be alive today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: The only measure that can prevent another death or injury is a full and complete ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Pass Intro 943 from Manchester Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Let our son be the last beating of the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deepak Mahajan
utterances.text: Let's see.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Priya Mahajan
utterances.text: That's not being good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Priya Mahajan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Priya Mahajan
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So sorry for your loss.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next we have Gauravna Hajoon and, sorry, Nuvjeet Manjana.

chapters.summary: Gaurav Mahajan, Romanch's uncle, describes Romanch as a brilliant aspiring entrepreneur, recounts his bravery in trying to save his mother, and references decades of carriage-related injuries. He requests a permanent memorial at the site.
chapters.title: Gaurav Mahajan (uncle) testifies about Romanch's bravery and requests memorial

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Members of the committee, my name is Gaurav Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Romesh was my nephew.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: I'm speaking in front of you, but I want to be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: A part of me died with him that day, and it has not come back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Let me tell you who he was, because he was not a headline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Romacho was a brilliant boy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: He didn't have small dreams.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: He wanted to build something big to be an entrepreneur.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: And he talked about it constantly to his father, to me, and anyone who would listen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: He had a smile that pulled you in and a heart that could still sit still, still if someone needed help, whether he knew them or not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: That is not a story we made up for this room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: That is who he was.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: And that is exactly how he died, reaching for his mother when the horse bolted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: In the most terrifying moment of his life, with no one at the reins, and everything out of control, my 18-year-old nephew showed true bravery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Grief takes the future from you a piece at a time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Romand collected sneakers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: He loved them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: It wrecks me that I will never be able to buy him another pair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I should be watching him build his business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Instead, I catch myself staring at the door, waiting for him to walk through it, knowing he won't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: We did not ask to become advocates.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: A broken system forced us into it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Roman's law is written in our tears, but it's not just written in our tears.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: It's written in the tears of those individuals and their families who were injured in the 1983 West Drive mass casualty incident and the 1985 incident involving the horse named Chester, who left four women in wheelchairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: And one woman blind due to severe head trauma.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: And the list of tragedies and injuries caused by horse-drawn carriages goes on and on for decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Ending with our romance and his mother Priya.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: If this law had already existed, my nephew would be alive today, chasing everything he dreamed about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: You have the power to stop this from happening to another family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: I'm asking you, look at us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Remember his face and pass the law so his soul can finally rest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: In closing, we request a permanent physical memorial at the exact site of the tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Romancia's unmatched bravery defined the very best of humanity on New York soil.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: The ground where he took his final breath must stand forever as a monument to their love, ensuring the city never forgets the boy who gave everything to protect his mother.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gaurav Mahajan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next, we will hear from Anuj Thukrat and Sobhya Thukrat.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: They have one more.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, they have one more.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I didn't realize that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

chapters.summary: Naveed Margaret, Romanch's aunt, testifies about the family's grief, the silence in their home, and urges the council to pass the ban so no other family experiences this nightmare.
chapters.title: Naveed Margaret (aunt) testifies about family's grief

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: My name is Naveed Margaret, and I'm Romance's aunt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: I came here today with a broken heart because our beautiful boy is gone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: And where he used to be, there is now a silence I cannot describe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Before this, Romance was a sunshine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: I know every family says that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: But I need you to hear it about him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Charming, loving, full of life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: 18 years old, celebrating his graduation with every dream still in front of him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: He trusted the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: He got into the carriage believing that if New York allowed these rides on its streets, they must be safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: That trust is what makes this unbearable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: A happy family trip turned into pure terror when the force became wild and uncontrollable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: And in those last moments, my nephew was so brave, braver than any child should ever have to be, facing something like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Never should have been allowed to happen to him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Every day since the quiet in our home is deafening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Some mornings the grief is so heavy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: It is hard to take a breath.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: I look at the empty space where romance should be and the weight of it sits on my chest and does not move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Please do not let him become a case number on a piece of paper.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: If you have children or a niece or a nephew, take off them tonight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Imagine that fear for one second and then follow your heart.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: The horse-drawn carriage ban must come to an end, which is why Romand's death was preventable and why only a ban will do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Pass Romand's law immediately.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Please help us spare the next family from this nightmare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Naveed Margaret
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now I'll call up Anuj Thukrat and Sovia Thukrat.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You're here, right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Come up to the table.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No, he's got to go.

chapters.summary: Sovya and Anuj Tukral, Romanch's aunt and uncle, describe the phone call hearing the family's screams, the history of carriage incidents, and the devastating impact on Romanch's mother Priya, who is numbed and devastated.
chapters.title: Sovya and Anuj Tukral testify about family's trauma

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: Good morning, members of the committee, and thank you for letting us speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: My name is Sovya Tukral.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: I'm along with my husband, Anuj Tukral.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: We are here for our nephew, Romanj Mahajan, who lost his life in Central Park on June 17, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: He arrived in New York just two days prior, on June 15th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: He was so excited to start college.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: He had been accepted to one of the top universities in India.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: And at 18, he had every dream and his entire life still ahead of him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: He was kind, sincere, he loved being around little children, and he carried himself with an honesty and loyalty to his family that you don't see in someone that young.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: I need to tell you about one phone call.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: On June 17th, that afternoon, I called my brother.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: Deepak Mahajan, expecting to hear that they were having a wonderful day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: Instead, I heard him crying, begging me, begging the officers at the scene, telling them, save my son.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: I heard my sister-in-law, Priya Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sovya Tukral
utterances.text: I heard Roman's little brother, Manik Mahajan, crying, horrified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: Those screams, those cries, those sounds of agony and despair, those sounds have not left me and they haunt me every single time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: They come back to me and they will for the rest of my life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: A boy who came here to celebrate the start of his new future in the city he was so excited to see never went home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: The unattended horse spooked and bolted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: And when it did, Romance tried to save his mother and it cost him his life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: Romance is not a tragedy on a page.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: He was a son, a grandson, a brother, a nephew, a young man with a future that belonged to him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: He was a part of a family, just like the elderly woman who was catastrophically injured and the tourist who was dragged by two spooked horses in 1997,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: just like two passengers who were injured by the runaway horse Nikki in 1998, 61st Street and 5th Avenue, two carriage incidents of 2003.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: Years of injured victims, years of life-changing events, years of families getting broken.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: The difference here is that romance is the first death from decades and decades of horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: injuries and catastrophes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: Now, will you finally act and put a stop to this industry?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: I am standing here in support of Roman's law so that his death will not go unremembered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: His passing will not be forgotten.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: That forces society to change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: So that no other family ever has to carry what we carry now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Anuj Tukral
utterances.text: Please remember his name, Ramach Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Remember the life he lived and the love he gave to everyone around him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Make sure he's the last person to die from this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Make sure it's the last person to die from this adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sorry, guys.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: His mother is shattered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: She doesn't want to speak anything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: She's numbed, devastated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Losing her 18-year-old son.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's painful for everybody in the house.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The house is empty there now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There is no sound of him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: His room is dark.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Nobody can enter that room anymore.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We just want to thank you for coming here today.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know how painful this is for you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You have our deepest condolences, and I just want to thank you for your courage and being able to share what the family is going through.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know this is very, very difficult for you, and we really appreciate your whole family coming here and testifying before us today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, I want to reiterate what the speaker said.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you for your courage, and thank you for sharing with us today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I really appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So did you have some words or?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You can speak.

chapters.summary: A family member continues describing Priya's devastation, how she watched Romanch take his last breath, and how their house went from the energy of Disneyland to a graveyard.
chapters.title: Family member describes Priya's devastation and household grief

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I just...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I just want to say that when this whole incident happened, the entire family was helpless.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When the horse bolted, they were helpless.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When Romange fell, they were helpless.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: His mom telling us that he literally took his last breath in front of her, and she said he was breathing in front of me, but he couldn't open his eyes, and they were helpless.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: At least now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: with all of your effort and the efforts of everybody in this room, including the speaker and all of you guys, maybe you can pass the law to be a help to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Their house literally went from

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They have kids everywhere to have an energy of a Disneyland to becoming like an energy of a graveyard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's the energy that they're living with because that's what they are going through every single day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you again.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are there...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Did you or you're...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Good.

chapters.summary: Sagar Chada, the family's lawyer, states the incident was entirely avoidable, that Romanch came as a tourist and died as a son of the city, and vows to hold the city accountable for decades of enforcement failures.
chapters.title: Family attorney Sagar Chada vows to hold city accountable

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: Thank you to the committee here today, to Speaker Menon, to Councilman Marte, to Councilman Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: My name is Sagar Chada.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: I'm a partner at Liakis Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: I represent the estate and the family of Ramach Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: I just want to, first of all, point out what everyone else here has already said, but to highlight the fact that this incident was entirely avoidable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: Ramach's death will not be in vain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: He came to this city as a tourist, and he died as a son of this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: And we will hold the city accountable for the decades of failures in enforcing and preventing this action from taking place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: Hitching posts, tethering points, no safety devices whatsoever to secure a 2,000-pound animal from consistently getting spooked and bolting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: This incident could have been avoided.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: It should have never happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: And if this law had been passed a year ago, on June 1st, 2026, there would be no horses in this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: Ramunsh, unfortunately, passed away on June 17th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: His death will not be in vain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: This is not about the workers and the union members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: They, too, have called for better safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: The Conservancy for decades has called for better safety, and we will hold the city accountable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sagar Chada
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you very much to all of you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now, are there any other family members that are here before I move on?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to also recognize that we've been joined by Councilmember Murano, and now the administration will present their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Will the administration come up?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I will turn it over to the committee council to administer the oath.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel administers the oath to the two administration witnesses from DOHMH and DCWP.
chapters.title: Administration witnesses sworn in

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you could both please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may proceed with your testimony.

chapters.summary: Corinne Schiff, Deputy Commissioner for Environmental Health at DOHMH, testifies about the department's oversight role, licensing process, and states the administration supports the intent of Intro 943 while noting concerns about worker protections.
chapters.title: DOHMH Deputy Commissioner Schiff testifies on administration's position

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Good morning, Madam Speaker, Chair Shulman, members of the Health Committee and other council members.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I am Corinne Schiff, Deputy Commissioner for Environmental Health at the New York City Health Department.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: On behalf of Commissioner Martin, thank you for the opportunity to testify today on Introduction 943.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I am joined by Carlos Ortiz, Chief of Staff and Deputy Commissioner of External Affairs at the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Before discussing the legislation, I want to express my deep condolences and those of the Health Commissioner and all of us at the Health Department to the family of Ramaj Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: His death associated with a horse carriage ride was a terrible tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is unimaginable what his family is going through.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The oversight of the carriage horse industry requires citywide coordination and the health department works closely with many other agencies also involved including DCWP, NYPD, DOT, and New York City Parks.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We also work closely with the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The health department is charged with issuing initial and renewal licenses for the carriage horses under the administrative code.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Our process includes reviewing the horse's health certificates to check the horses are examined by their veterinarian every four to eight months,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: as well as reviewing owner records showing that the horses have had at least five weeks a year of vacation or furlough at a horse stable that allows daily access to a paddock or pasture.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We make periodic observations of the horse's movement and overall body condition at the three Central Park hack stands where carriages must pick up and discharge passengers and monitor weather and issue weather-related suspensions.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We also conduct the training course for aspiring carriage horse drivers that is a prerequisite to obtain a horse carriage driver's license from DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani has been clear that New York City should join the other cities that no longer allow operation of a horse carriage industry, and the administration supports the intent of Introduction 943.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: To promote a good outcome for the horses, the bill would require the health department to provide horse owners with information.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: about individuals and organizations that can receive or acquire horses for humane purposes and require owners to certify that they are not transferring or selling their horse for an inhumane purpose.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The bill provides a two-year phase-out period, providing time for owners and drivers to make this transition and requires a workforce development program.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The administration is, however, critical of the insufficiencies of the worker protections in the bill, and we look forward to working with the council to address those concerns as we move forward on this important issue for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Ortiz and I can now take your questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, did Commissioner Ortiz, did you have a separate?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Gennaro asks whether any carriage driver licenses have been suspended or revoked. Ortiz says not in recent years; Menin notes a suspension did occur when she was DCWP commissioner.
chapters.title: Gennaro questions administration on license suspensions and revocations

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Deputy Commissioner, for your statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It's primarily your agency and also PD that really looks after the drivers.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: By the way, how much time do I have?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: In the last 167 years of the industry, or as far back as you have records,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: has there ever been a situation where a horse carriage driver's license has been suspended or revoked?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: The answer is no, but you can, you know.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I actually don't know if that's true.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: In recent years, there have not been suspensions or revocations, but I believe in prior administrations there were.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: No, there aren't.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But I can pull the...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yeah, you can do it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm sorry, Councilor M.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Gennaro, and we'll give you extra time for this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I was commissioner of DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We actually did do a suspension due to conduct when I was commissioner, so it has happened.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yeah, I think it was due to conduct.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It had to do with financial, not safety.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But in any event, I was offered the opportunity by the speaker to make a statement at the outset of the hearing, but I was told by staff that now tells the speaker what to do that I was like not able to make a statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so that's...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Okay, and so now when it comes to medallions, have medallions been revoked or suspended?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: To your knowledge?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: To my knowledge, in recent years, they have not, sir.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Gennaro presses the administration for enforcement data and facts supporting the ban, noting their testimony lacked specific violation counts. Ortiz gives a general response about regulatory efforts; Gennaro expresses frustration at the lack of specifics.
chapters.title: Gennaro presses administration for enforcement data supporting ban

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: In terms of...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: In terms of what the health department and the mountain unit that's out there,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: what are the levels of tickets and violations for

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: safety and also and I also will note that

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, after, you know, incidents have happened, I noticed that there have not been, I don't think there have been violations issued to drivers, meaning that

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: the people, that would be you, who are in charge of this kind of thing, thought that it was explainable or something.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so I'm trying to find out how you come here today to support the elimination of the industry just because the mayor thinks so.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But what I'm looking for here are actual facts because you are the oversight thing and it was nothing in your testimony that said, I'm sure that if you had actual

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: data, facts, violations, episodes, or whatever, you would have made mention of them in your testimony.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You did not.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Can you explain that?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think I would like to take a step back in a sense and answer the question.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: No, I want you to answer the question.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, I would like to recognize that I think this issue around horse-drawn carriages in Central Park has been an ongoing discourse for many administrations now.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It doesn't matter whether it's a discourse.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: We're based, like we're doing things based on facts.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And if you had information that supports the elimination of the industry, you would have put it in your statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You did not.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: The bell rang.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I yield.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: No, no, no, no.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I would like to...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: No, I am...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I get to ask the questions.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I, you know, close with a statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You don't get to say anything now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member Roll.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I intend to comment that...

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I didn't ask for comments.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I asked for facts.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: To echo my colleague's testimony that the administration supports a winding down this industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We recognize...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: That's your full statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: That's all you have is that the mayor wants it.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I like to operate based on fact and what you guys have done to enforce and recommendations that you might have made to make it safe.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I didn't hear any of that in the testimony, and there's no dancing that's going to get you out of that.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we have done significant efforts in the city over the many years to try to improve regulations around this industry, whether that is limitations around where these carriages can operate, how many carriages can operate.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: How many horses are licensed?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We have all engaged in robust efforts to try and bring additional actionable summonses.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We have worked with industry across multiple administrations to try and address what's happening.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: The reality is that it's not working.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Madam Chair, he's not being responsive, and he's just, you know, he's just.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm not looking for a word salad here.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm looking for answers which I didn't get.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council member, your time is up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And you answered the question, yes?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes, Chair, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilman, I'm sorry, Speakerman and...

chapters.summary: Speaker Menin questions both agencies about the fatal June incident where the driver left the horse unattended, asks about worker protection provisions, and how many workers would be affected. Ortiz confirms 208 licensed drivers, ~170 active.
chapters.title: Menin questions agencies on fatal incident and worker protections

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So a number of questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So questions for DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: During the fatal incident involving Romange in June, the driver reportedly left the horse and carriage unattended to take a photograph of the family members, after which the horse became startled and bolted.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What is the agency's, both agencies'position on this incident?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The administrative code is very clear that drivers may not leave the horse unattended.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The health department, as I noted in my testimony, provides the training course for aspiring carriage horse drivers, and we do include

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: that key safety provision in that training.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The enforcement of that action is under review at the health department right now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would also add, Speaker, please, to echo, I believe, testimony that's been submitted by the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Is that I think the incident overall, it goes back to the inherent challenges that exist when horses are working in these busy urban environments, and ultimately why I think the administration is supportive of a long-term solution to the horse-drawn carriage

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: industry, keeping in mind, of course, outcomes and pathways that are sustainable for the workers in this industry as well, which is of critical importance for the Department of Consumer Worker Protection as well.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You mentioned in your testimony, the health department testimony, about worker protections in the bill, which is something I am focused on as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
