utterances.text: What are the specific worker protections, and I guess why is that testimony coming not from DCWP?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think for the, just for an operational side, I just have one testimony to represent the administration's position.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Of course, I'm here to comment on the worker touchpoint as well.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: You know, certainly I think to provide some concrete feedback, workforce development is not necessarily something that DCBP engages in now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are many other agencies that do that work.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Notwithstanding, we would like to be actively involved in this conversation as well as it relates to outcomes for the workers and ensuring that we have sustainable pathways forward for them.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Is there specific language that the administration is recommending?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I don't have language available at this time, but I think as we move through the legislative process, we will be providing language.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about the phase-out timeline, administration plans for worker support, and whether specific worker protection language is being recommended. Ortiz says the administration is open to discussing timeline and will provide language.
chapters.title: Menin questions on phase-out timeline and worker transition plans

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How many workers would be affected by the ban?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We obviously know how many licenses DCWP has.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We've been looking at the open data portal, but I want to get on the record this information, which is why I'm asking these questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So how many workers would be affected by a ban, including carriage drivers, stable workers, and other staff?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I apologize, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I don't know if I have the stable worker numbers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I do know that there are 208 licensed horse-drawn carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: My understanding from public reporting is that approximately 170 of them are engaging in the work day in, day out.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what does the administration's view in terms of the...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Proper phase-out timeline that both agencies would recommend?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Do you agree with the language in the bill currently?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Do you believe differently?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think, and just for me to confer with my colleague, I don't think we have concerns necessarily with the phase-out timeline itself.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think a setting such as this, to hear from the stakeholders, whether that's industry and workers and other advocates, is an important step in the legislative process to know if the timeline is appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what are the administration's plans to support the workers?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, I think we're having ongoing conversations with our partners in labor about what outcomes we can support, what outcomes we can, what we can do to support outcomes for workers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I've heard of your public comments recently as well, Speaker, about securing employment for workers as well.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think for us, all options are on the table.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We want to make sure that this works.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to turn it back over to Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks for current numbers of horses, stables, carriage licenses, and drivers. Schiff reports just under 160 licensed horses and three stables. Ortiz confirms 68 carriage owner licenses and 208 driver licenses.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on horse, stable, and license numbers

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: First, I want to say that we've been joined by Councilmembers Feliz, Maloney, and Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So how many carriage horses are currently licensed and operating in New York City?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please provide the number of horses, the number of stables, the number of carriage licenses, and drivers.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I guess that's divided between the two of you.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: As of the end of June, we had just under 160 horses licensed, and there are three stables that serve the industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The former mayor issued Executive Order 56 directing agencies to prepare for the cessation of horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What inspections or other work did DOHMH begin, how much was completed, and what continued after the order was rescinded?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We conduct routine inspections of the stables, routine inspections of horses, both at the stables and

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: these are observations of the horses as well as inspections of paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And also we make periodic observations at the hack stands.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would also add, Chair, that DCP conducts compliance inspections three times a year on each of the carriages.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And to touch upon your other part of your question, I know that the previous administration and the Blasey administration, I would say my entire career in city government, that we've been working on this issue as well.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about horses removed from service for medical concerns, stable violation counts, and inspection staff. Schiff says six inspectors work on this among other duties and promises to provide violation data.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on horse removals and stable violations

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Over the past five years, how many horses have been removed from service due to medical concerns?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So it is a very routine process for us to take in complaints, often by video,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: to make observations ourselves, or to take in referrals from NYPD, which is often at the hack stands.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And we will, depending on our veterinarian's assessment of those, we will require the owner to have its veterinarian assess the horse.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And during that time, the horse is not in service.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And we require the veterinarian to, the owner to provide the veterinarian's determination before the horse can be returned to work.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I do not have the count.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We can continue to work on that to try to provide it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We want the count, so we want you to get that to us.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I want to know what are the most common concerns that lead to removal.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I can say some of the common concerns would be that the horse is lame, for example, and we might see a video that either shows that it is or that it needs further assessment to be able to determine that.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: That would be a common consideration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: DOHMH is responsible for enforcing requirements governing stable conditions including adequate heating and ventilation, sufficient food and drinking water, and minimum stall dimensions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Over the past five years, how many violations has DOHMH issued for failure to comply with stable requirements?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And please provide a breakdown by violation type.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you don't have it here, then you need to send it to us.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Answer whatever you can answer right now.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We do make routine inspections of the stables two to three times a year.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I don't have the violation numbers, so we will get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many staff does the OHMH currently employ to inspect and oversee horse carriage stables?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have six health inspectors who work in our veterinary public health program.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They do a variety of work, including the inspections of the horse carriages, and we have equine veterinarians we work with on a contract basis.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Have any stables repeatedly failed inspections or required corrective action plans?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The stables have not.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: required corrective action plans.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about weather-related suspensions under Local Law 203. Schiff reports 102 suspensions in three years, with 28 in 2025 and 19 in Jan-June 2026, and notes NYPD handles on-site enforcement.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on weather-related suspensions and heat monitoring

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Local law 203 of 2019 prohibits horse carriages from working when the temperature is 90 degrees or above, or when the temperature is 80 degrees and the equine heat index reaches 150 or above.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Since the law took effect, how many written warnings and violations has DOHMH issued for noncompliance?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: What I can tell you is the suspensions in the last three years.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There have been 102 weather-related suspensions in the last three years.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I'd like to confer with NYPD on violations.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They are typically at the hack stands at the time of the suspension, and so I would like to check with them to get you a full count.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have not issued violations for violating the suspension, but it is typically NYPD who is actually on site at the hack stand.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So let me confer with them to be able to provide that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How does DOHMH monitor?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Compliance when temperatures approach the statutory thresholds.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I missed the beginning of your question.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How does DOHMH monitor compliance when temperatures approach the statutory thresholds?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we are, when we know that it is a day where we are potentially going to reach one of those thresholds.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Like today.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The horses have been suspended today.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are monitoring the weather.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The administrative code sets out very specific protocols for how to measure that temperature, including the type of equipment that the health department and the NYPD are required to use.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we do have that equipment that the council has required.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And so we take those measurements as we understand the temperature threshold may be approaching,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: and that lets us be able to suspend the activity when that threshold is met.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How often are readings taken?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I will have to get back to you about the number of times the readings are taken.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Global temperatures are rising and New York City is increasingly experiencing extreme heat and high humidity.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many days in 2025 and 2026 were carriage horses prohibited from working because the temperature reached 90 degrees or the equine heat index reached the threshold?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: What I have is the total weather-related suspensions.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There is also a cold weather threshold, and we also will suspend for other extreme weather conditions, for example, if the paths are icy.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So I have the total numbers.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: In 2025, it was 28, and 2026, from January to June, 19.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about driver training on spooking responses, course curriculum development, and whether DOHMH believes inherent risks exist that training cannot address. Schiff describes the course components; Ortiz notes inherent urban challenges.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on driver training course content and inherent risks

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, DOHMH administers the horse-drawn carriage operators course, which includes testing for safe driving practices.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Horses can become startled by bicycles, scooters, vehicles, and other unexpected activity.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What training do drivers receive on responding to a horse that becomes frightened of bolts?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is that response tested as part of the driver training?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the course that we administer as a prerequisite for the DCWP license includes information about horse behavior and maintaining control of the horse.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And as I noted, it also includes training that it is prohibited under the administrative code to leave a horse.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: unattended.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And there is a written component, a classroom component to the exam, and the aspiring driver has to pass a test at the end of the classroom component.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And then there is a practical component with a horse and our equine veterinarian, where that is the opportunity for the aspiring driver to demonstrate that they are able to

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: manage the horse properly.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, does DOHMH believe there are any inherent risks that cannot be addressed through DOHMH's carriage operators training course?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I will say that the course is designed to be comprehensive, to teach the aspiring driver about horse

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: anatomy, to look for signs of

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: of illness and other issues and to address, to cover the health and safety requirements of the multiple city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would add to that, Chair, that echoing the testimony that was submitted by the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare,

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: that there seem to be inherent challenges with operating horse-drawn carriages in New York City in a busy urban environment in situations where we have exacerbated weather situations now because of climate change.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think these do pose inherent challenges to the industry that is a part of this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: During the fatal incident in June, the driver stepped away from the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We understand that this goes against safety protocols and violates DOT rules, which require the driver to hold the reins at all times.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are these safety protocols identified in the horse-drawn carriage operator's course?

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about curriculum development experts, protocol updates after the fatal incident, and whether the course covers the prohibition on leaving horses unattended. Schiff confirms it does and describes outreach efforts.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on curriculum development and safety protocol updates

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They are.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is also, it is very clear in the administrative code as well that the drivers are required, are prohibited from leaving the horse unattended.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is covered in our course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How is the curriculum for the course developed and what experts are consulted on its development?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The course was developed by our equine veterinarians in consultation with many others, with, for example, the ASPCA,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: NYPD, and our agency colleagues, because it covers and addresses the regulations that live in many other agencies'codes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is DOHMH considering any updates to its protocols to prevent future incidents?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The laws, the regulations, and the protocols are all designed to prohibit the incidents.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We conduct outreach and education.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have delivered notices to the stables for posting to remind drivers that they cannot leave the horse unattended.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would add to that as well.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think that the administration is certainly open to changes we can make, marginal changes we can make around the edges to try and reform this industry in the short term.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But the long-term question, I think, is what is paramount right now and winding down the industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Ultimately, I think that if there's, in general, in the work that we do at DCDP,

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: If there's business activity that is causing harm, we can't necessarily think first of, like, how do we facilitate the activity continuing.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think our first question, the hard question the government has to ask itself is how do we stop the harm?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And so I think that's part of the, that's how we want to approach this, with the guiding principles, of course, of ensuring that worker outcomes are protected, that the animal outcomes are protected as well.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about course enrollment numbers, language access gaps, and course fees. Schiff reports 18 people took the course in 2025, it's English-only, and costs $25. Staffing constraints have limited course offerings.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on course enrollment and language access

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The horse-drawn carriage operators course is offered four times a year.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In 2025, how many people enrolled in the course?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I do want to note that we have not been able to meet that four-time-a-year offering of late.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have equine veterinarians on contract.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have not been able to hire as a staff person to fulfill that role, so we are a little constrained in being able to offer it four times a year.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: In 2025, 18 people took the course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So how many times a year do you do it?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Since you don't have the...

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It has varied, so let me get you the last couple of years, but it has varied because of the staffing constraints.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is that being addressed?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I mean, I know we have this bill, but is that being addressed?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have been actively recruiting for an equine veterinarian.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And just to reiterate, we do have equine veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They're just on a contract, and so that can hamper the number of times we can offer the course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Drivers speak multiple languages, including Spanish, Turkish, and Tajik.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In what languages is the course offered, and are there gaps in language access?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There are gaps in language access.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is an English language course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is there a fee to register for the course?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What about for any course materials or examinations?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is a $25 course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And that includes course materials and everything else, or?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I think that's the total course cost, but if there's something additional, I'll correct that, but I believe it's a total of $25.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please describe the practical examination component of the course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What does this exam consist of and who conducts it?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The practical exam is conducted by an equine veterinarian with a horse, and the driver needs to demonstrate, for example, the proper fitting of the bridle and the harness,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: proper hitching and unhitching, managing and control of the horse.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks whether DOHMH is prepared to coordinate horse transitions to sanctuaries. Schiff says the department is still reviewing the bill's requirements and would work with the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on horse sanctuary transition planning

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The committee has heard from many animal sanctuaries across the country that have stated they are willing to accept the horses in the event that the industry is phased out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is DOHMH equipped to coordinate with city agencies, sanctuaries, and animal welfare organizations to transition horses to safe and caring homes and to ensure that none are sold or disposed of in a manner that leads to slaughter or mistreatment?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the bill would require the health department to collect information about sanctuaries and similar

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: organizations and people and to provide that information to the owners and then to collect certifications from the owner.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: owners about the disposition of their horse.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are reviewing the bill.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we don't have exactly the details for how we would implement that, but we would be prepared to implement should the bill pass.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's what I'm asking you, if you're prepared and do you need other resources to facilitate that?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We haven't fully evaluated the requirements of the bill.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are still reviewing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many staff positions does DOHMH dedicate to carriage horses?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we have six inspectors who work on this industry, but also other animal-related industries.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And then we have program leads, administrative staff, and then equine veterinarians on contract.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If the council enacts Intro 943, does DOHMH have a plan to transition those staff?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So these staff work on a number of different programs, and so there's no concern about having them, there will be other work for them to do.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about multi-agency coordination, the Rental Horse Advisory Board, and whether it has met or made recommendations. Schiff says the board has not delivered recommendations in many years.
chapters.title: Schulman questions on multi-agency coordination and advisory board

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please describe how DOHMH communicates and works with DCWP to enforce local laws governing the carriage horse industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is there a multi-agency team that regularly coordinates on enforcement, trainings, and emergency response?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We do communicate regularly with the many agencies that have a role in oversight of the industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would add to that.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know I've personally participated in...

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: A citywide task force that is focused on Central Park and issues as it relates to particularly horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: That also work happens in coordination with local stakeholders as well.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are any other agencies such as Parks and NYPD involved in this process?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes, they are.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: They're both part of it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The Rental Horse Advisory Board is required to advise the Commission on regulations affecting the health, safety, and welfare of carriage horses and the public.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Has the Advisory Board met in recent years, and if so, what recommendations or actions has it taken to improve passenger safety?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the advisory board is established in the local law.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It has been constituted.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There isn't a formal role for the health department beyond once the health commissioner has designated the members.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we are aware of meetings, but we don't have a role in those meetings, so I can't speak to really their meeting schedule.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They have not delivered recommendations to the commissioner, the health commissioner, in many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The Rental Horse Advisory Board is required to advise the Commissioner on regulations affecting the health, safety, and welfare of carriage horses in the public.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I did?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, yeah, sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Does the agency envision any potential challenges should Intro 943 be enacted?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are continuing to review the bill, but as you have heard from both Deputy Commissioner Ortiz and myself, the administration supports the bill with some interest in continuing to discuss some of the provisions with the council.

chapters.summary: Schulman questions Ortiz on carriage owner licenses: 68 active, transferable, averaging $70,000 in value, with a 59-day grace period for renewal after expiration.
chapters.title: Schulman questions DCWP on owner license values and transferability

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to ask some questions for you, Carlos.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many active carriage owner licenses exist currently?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Our research shows the number at 52.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are 68 carriage licenses currently.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Okay, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many carriages are currently in operation?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm not sure how much are operating throughout the day, but there's 68 that are authorized to work.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is that number different than the number of owner licenses?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: 68 corresponds to the owner.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: It is the owner license.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But is that different from what's in operation?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's what I'm asking.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So there are 68 licenses that relate to the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Those are the owner licenses.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are 208 licenses for drivers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I wouldn't say every driver is operating every day.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: The carriages, I think it fluctuates based on what the owner wants to put out that day.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What is the average value of a carriage owner license?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We looked over the bills of sales.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think on average, over the many years, there's maybe $70,000 approximately.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think that that could fluctuate.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: It could be different negotiating terms in each contract.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So it's something we can continue to evaluate with the council.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are owner licenses transferable?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If so, how?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: They are transferable.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Right now there's a cap on the category that I think was instituted 10 or so years ago.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: It requires an agreement between the previous owner and the new one and the notification to the agency as well.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What happens to an owner license if it expires?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can it be renewed?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes, I believe we're able to issue up to the cap, which is 68.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So if there's an expiration, we'll be able to issue a license.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can expired licenses be recovered in any way?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There's typically a grace period we provide after an expiration to allow a business to come back in and renew.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: This is applicable to all of our categories.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: The grace period is 59 days.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: If you're coming back for a license after the 59-day period, generally you're going to have to get a new license.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilman Amate?

chapters.summary: Marte asks about the independent veterinarian inspection process. Schiff describes a late-2025 pilot program where one vet responded to a solicitation and examined 12 horses, with some limitations due to the stable environment.
chapters.title: Marte questions DOHMH on independent vet inspection pilot program

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Last year, the Department of Health ordered an independent veterinarian to inspect the stables and test the horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: What was the process in choosing that veterinarian?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: In late 2025, the health department undertook a pilot initiative to conduct exams, clinical exams, as part of our stable inspection.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we put out a solicitation broadly to sending it to equine veterinarians we could identify in New York State and the surrounding states.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There was one response.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And so you say that this veterinarian was completely independent from the administration, willing to do the work with an independent mindset?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The veterinarian who responded to the solicitation met the requirements of the solicitation, which was that they were not, we wanted someone who was not engaged already in the industry.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And can you elaborate on the investigation and the findings of the veterinarian?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The veterinarian did an assessment of 12 horses, and following the same,

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: the administrative code sets out, really, a lot of the details, the details for the assessment that a veterinarian needs to do of the horse to show that the horse is fit to work in the industry.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And so this veterinarian was doing that same assessment with the caveat that these assessments were conducted in the stable.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So there were some limitations because of that environment.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And is 12 an average amount of inspection of horses that typically happen, or is that far below the average if someone were to go and investigate fully the stables and the horses?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The pilot program that we did at the end of 2025 looked at, was completed for 12 horses.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I have a question for Mr.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Ortiz.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Would the administration be open to a shorter phase-out period if we included worker protections that will allow workers to find new employment sooner?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we would have to discuss.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm certainly encouraged by the perspective of ensuring that we have pathways for workers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think on balance, we'd have to review the bill in totality to see if a shorter phase-out is accomplished.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: It was something we can accomplish.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But I think we're very much open to ideas right now on the table for this.

chapters.summary: Marte asks whether the administration would support a shorter phase-out period if worker protections are included. Ortiz says the administration is open to discussing it but would need to review the bill in totality.
chapters.title: Marte questions administration on shorter phase-out timeline

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: One final question.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: All right, I'll come back.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now, ask the one final question, but we're trying to keep on time here because we have a lot of people testifying.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I respect, I don't need to ask this question.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I respect that.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember, before I do that, I just want to say one thing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, I'm a big fan of, well, two things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is we've been joined by Councilmember Hanif.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The other is that I'm a big fan of DOHMH, but I'm disappointed at the data questions that we asked that we didn't get answers to because those questions were submitted to you guys beforehand.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I just want to make mention of that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to ask Councilmember Epstein to ask questions.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks what percentage of time six health inspectors spend on horse issues (estimated one FTE), and questions Ortiz on worker transition plans. Ortiz says conversations are ongoing with labor partners.
chapters.title: Epstein questions on staff capacity and worker transitions

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can I just ask about the six staff?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You said they are working in other industries.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What percentage of their time are dealing with horse issues for the six staff?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I don't have a percentage of time.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I would estimate it's probably equivalent to one FTE.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: One FTE for all the horses.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Carlos, can I talk about the transitions?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You flagged the concerns about making sure there's sufficient worker protections in this transition.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: When you say that, when you say worker protections, do you mean helping them transition to a new industry or transition into a related industry just without the horses?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think it's really about securing outcomes for the workers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So I think a transition into employment, I think, is something that we'd be interested in exploring.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know it's come up in our conversations with labor partners.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know the bill already contemplates workforce development, for example.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So I think that's what we're referring to.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So workforce development, but it could be in this industry, like tourism, or within a separate industry?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is that what you're flagging?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think, well, I think part of that conversation needs to happen with workers as well in terms of what industries they want to be moving into.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And, yeah, I think that's an important question for them.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we are certainly open to facilitating what outcomes they need.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And what is your timeline for that?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Just so, obviously, this is a pressing issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Fortunately, we've already just had a lawsuit.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Of life.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So are you thinking that conversations are going to be happening in the next couple of weeks?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think the conversations are already happening on this, and I think the timeline will have to align with the prospective timeline for this bill to engage in the legislative process.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I do, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I just want to clarify my comment on our staff was about our health inspectors.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There are other staff who work on this industry as well, reviewing documentation, and as I mentioned, our equine veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, one full-time equivalent of person going out.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: For the health inspector.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I just want to make sure that was clear.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Helpful.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Councilmember Wilson?

chapters.summary: Won, who lives near the stables, asks about the horse transition process, sanctuary follow-up, and long-term welfare monitoring. Schiff says the department is still reviewing; Ortiz notes the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare has received sanctuary outreach.
chapters.title: Won questions on horse transition and sanctuary oversight

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I live across the street from the stables in Hill's Kitchen.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The stables are homed in District 3.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I see the horses quite a bit, walking to and from the park every morning.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And my partner is a lifelong equestrian, so horse care is very important to me and my family.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I have a couple of questions about the transition for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I respect that you said you're still putting this together, but you mentioned in your testimony that you would collect information about the sanctuaries or I guess retirement farms that these horses would go to.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So approximately how long will that process take?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And once that's given to the drivers, will that be the list of who they can go to or would they be allowed to find other homes for the horses?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the bill specifies, gives the department direction for information to collect and to provide to the owners.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are still looking at that, and should the bill pass, which, as you know, we support with caveats, we would be refining that process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So would there be any, once the horse is there, is thought to how, would there be any follow-up at the sanctuaries or retirement homes, retirement farms to ensure that they are indeed being taken care of?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So I think we're continuing to review the bill to assess that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I guess my point is that, you know, it's very nice to say that they, you know,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: They'll be transitioned and sent to a retirement farm or sanctuary, but oftentimes there's more to it than that for the well-being of the horses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The transition from one environment to another can be disruptive, and we'd want to just make sure that they are actually being taken care for.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: It's not just a line written on the page, but something the city continues to follow up on to ensure.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think that point is well taken, Councilmember, and we're also encouraged that our colleagues in the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare have already received outreach from equestrian sanctuaries to engage in this work, but I think

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: even as we phase out, it'll have to be an interdisciplinary and multi-agency effort, and ensuring, for example, that that office is involved and running checks on this is important too.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Morano asks at what point a regulator must acknowledge inherent risk remains despite regulation. Schiff says that's why they're here today. Morano then asks if the health department would approve a new horse carriage industry if proposed today.
chapters.title: Morano questions administration on regulatory limits and inherent risks

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Marano?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for your willingness to hold this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I know it's been a long time coming.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also want to thank the Speaker for her leadership on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I know it takes a lot of courage to be the first sitting Speaker to take this particular position on this issue, but

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: aside from taking a position that I happen to agree with, I want to commend you for the really responsible way in which you're addressing concerns about job loss and things of that nature and not seeking to demagogue the issue at all.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And it's a tough thing to do, and I want to thank you for your leadership on that.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'd also be remiss if I didn't mention the leadership of former Councilman Holden.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And had we acted on this legislation when he was in the council, perhaps a young man would still be alive today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That being said, thank you, Deputy Commissioner, for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And to the extent that you need Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Ortiz to assist you with my questions, please just have him do so.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to start with something in your testimony that really jumped out at me.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You tell us the health department reviews health certificates.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You check that horses are examined every four to eight months.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You review furlough records.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You make periodic observations.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You monitor weather.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You issue weather-related suspensions.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And you actually conduct the training course that aspiring carriage drivers must complete.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That's a lot of regulation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And yet an 18-year-old young man is dead.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I want to ask you a question that I don't mean rhetorically at all.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: At what?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: At what point does a regulator have an obligation to say, well, we've regulated this activity as much as we reasonably can, and the inherent risk remains?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So I would say that the health department is exercising the authority that the city council has given to us.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And my understanding is that is that is your question is why we are here today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And because I keep hearing proposals for another inspection, another training requirement, another rule, another layer of enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'm wondering, can you identify for me a regulation that would eliminate a horse's instinct to bolt when frightened?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think, Council Member, this is, I would say this is probably the third administration I've worked in now where we, as an administration, have been supportive of winding down this industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And I think your point is well taken that, you know, we could be working on the margins to try and address what's happening in the industry to find short-term solutions.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But ultimately, we as an administration are supportive of a long-term solution that protects the conditions of workers, the horses, and New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let me ask you something else, and I'll be considerate of everyone else's time.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If there were no horse-drawn carriage industry in New York City today, and someone came to your department with a proposal to create one, to place large prey animals in one of the most heavily visited urban parks in the world,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: attach open passenger carriages to them, and sell rides to the public, knowing...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Everything your department knows today, would the health department recommend that the city approve that new industry?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It is your, what you imply is well taken, and I think as my colleague is saying, that is why we are here today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thanks very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Councilmember Murano.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Councilmember Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about horse rehoming plans, financial responsibility, stable hand transition eligibility, and whether workforce development is a real pipeline or just resume workshops. Schiff and Ortiz respond on bill provisions and worker inclusion.
chapters.title: Narcisse questions on horse rehoming and worker transition specifics

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And first and foremost, thank you, Madam Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And the family, I feel you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you so much for being here, being present, and using the time to let us know what's going on with you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's a difficult time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And as a mother of four, I can feel you sitting here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Now, is it difficult for me to even

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Let me go to the question.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: This is, you know, listening to you is just in my head.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What is the administration's plan for the horses themselves?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Rehoming, sanctuaries, placement oversight, and who is financially responsible for that transition?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The bill that we have here on our hands, focus on the license of the drivers, what happens to the stable, I mean,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: the stable hands and other workers, local 100 and the union have said the industry supports.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are they eligible for any of this transition benefits?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Third, what specific industries or role is DCWP targeting for placement

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is this a real pipeline into union jobs or a genetic resume workshop programs?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Do you know?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I'll start with the questions about the horses, and the bill is quite detailed, directing the health department to collect information about where the horses

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: might go and to provide that information to the owners and then requiring that the owners certify to the disposition of the horses and provide that certification to the health department.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are still reviewing those provisions and to determine exactly how we would do that should the bill pass.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And as Deputy Commissioner Ortiz mentioned, and I agree, we would work very closely with the Mayor's Office of Animal Welfare on that.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And I would add, Council Member, you know, the bill already describes a workforce development program for horse-drawn carriage drivers and other workers engaged in the business of operating such horse-drawn cabs.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: To me, I mean, I'm encouraged that the Council has already drafted or is keeping these workers in mind, both the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: and the stable hands, I think we want to make sure that that is preserved throughout a final version of the bill in whichever mechanism we decide to be able to support the workers.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Tough one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What could I have done here in this situation to prevent the death of this young man?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: As the speaker eloquently articulated, this was preventable.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The council has set out a law that clearly says that a horse must be attended to, cannot be left unattended.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And one life is too many.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks how DOHMH and NYPD communicate on enforcement, and whether the administration has studied other cities' worker transition programs. Ortiz describes a joint task force; neither agency has studied other cities' programs yet.
chapters.title: Brewer questions on inter-agency communication and other cities' programs

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: A couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: One is just in terms of how does Health Department and NYPD, how do they communicate?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You said that when there is a situation where the Health Department has perhaps given some summonses,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But the NYPD does the actual violations depending on the situation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How does that communication take place?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are in regular communication with NYPD, particularly on days like today and yesterday where we know we're most likely going to reach those weather thresholds, those

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: temperature and humidity thresholds.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So it is routine.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: What I meant to say about the summons is that we are not necessarily at the hack stands at the time that those suspensions occur.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So in order to provide the chair with full information about violations, I wanted to be able to get that, to talk to NYPD first.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so you're basically back and forth with email situations.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Emails, phone calls.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: You know, not only during the business day, off hours, weekends, it is a regular communication.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would also add...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm sorry, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead, Carla.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would just add to the processes of having this joint task force that we've had centralized in Central Park, the CDP has also facilitated trainings that we provide Central Park Command.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And Parks Enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: As well on what issues they need to look out for as they conduct their on-street or in-park enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Other cities, in the briefing paper, it talks about Philly and other places.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My issue is workers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In some cases, they've had job fairs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They're much smaller numbers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think it were 10 people who actually, or Chicago was even fewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But have you looked to see what worker programs have been effective in other cities, or they're too small to even consider?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because you have obviously many more.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thus far, the CUP has not looked into other worker programs.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know we've had initial conversations with our colleagues, such as at the Mayor's Office of Talent Workforce Development, that will have feedback on how to build a sustainable program.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But I think if there's lessons to be learned from other municipalities about how they transitioned out, we are certainly open to taking that feedback.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, obviously the briefing paper had some.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You could get the same, but there were some ideas there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been on this bill.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been the city council 2002, a long time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I've been on this bill except when there was an electric car.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I am absolutely opposed to any car more in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm the author of the bill to get them out.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I want to make sure that the administration is not talking about electric cars or anything like it in terms of this discussion.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I, thus far, have had no conversations on my end related to electric cars.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Health department?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Nor have I, and I did not see that in the bill.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know it's not in the bill.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to make sure nobody...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: pops up with it.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises pedicabs as a tourism alternative and asks the administration's position. Ortiz says DCWP is ready to engage on pedicab reforms. Brewer also confirms no one is discussing electric cars as a replacement.
chapters.title: Brewer questions on pedicabs as alternative and electric cars

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and then the other, so we got workers, horses, jobs, and tourists.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I am supportive of tourists using pedicabs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you support?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Have you had any thoughts about it?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously we have legislation that hasn't been finalized, but we're thinking about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you think could address the need for tourists?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I take pedicabs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You should see me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know how to do it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I know how to organize how much I pay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering what you're thinking about tourism for the future in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think pedicabs will still be an ongoing activity that's happening in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: You know, I did notice a few months back, I think, that the Midtown Community Justice Center released a report of recommendations as it relates to the pedicab industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think many of the recommendations in there were well taken, and certainly if we're looking to strengthen or improve the pedicab industry as a substitute in the future, DCUP will be ready to engage in that conversation too.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think it had a lot to do with that report, so I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Hanif?

chapters.summary: Hanif asks about DOHMH's oversight authority limits, standardized fitness criteria, vet check frequency, and violation counts. Schiff provides 17 summonses for missing medical exams and 12 for missing furlough records from 2023-April 2026.
chapters.title: Hanif questions DOHMH on oversight authority and violation counts

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I just want to begin by talking to the Mahajan family.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'm so sorry for your loss.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And thank you for trusting the council with Romanta's story.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'm thinking of you and your family.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And passing this piece of legislation is the most important thing that must come out of today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And we're grateful that the speaker and our mayor are supportive.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'd like to know what oversight does DOHMH currently have over the health and welfare of carriage horses?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And what are the limits of that authority?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And if you could keep it brief.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: It's a big question.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The Ant Code is quite specific in the role, in setting out the role of the health department and what we can require from the industry.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So there is, as I mentioned in my testimony, that's highlighting some of the key areas.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The horse owners are required to have veterinary exams every four to eight months and to submit certifications of those exams to us.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We are reviewing those.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: They require to submit documentation showing proof of vacation furlough five weeks a year at a place where there is a pasture opportunity for turnout.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The license is renewable.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So there's all of those things that we are checking.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And then is there a standardized criteria for determining when a horse is not fit?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have a form, we have a certification form that a veterinarian licensed in New York State signs off on, and our equine veterinarian reviews those.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: How frequently are vets checking horses'health?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the owner's vets?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Is there a DOHMH vet and then an owner's vet?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And what's the frequency?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the administrative code requires a veterinarian check.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Every four to eight months, the owners have veterinarians who check their horses.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have veterinarians who review those certifications.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: A veterinarian would also, we also, as I noted earlier, when we have reason to think that there's need for an assessment

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: of a horse because of our observation or something referred to us by NYPD or we are observing based on a complaint, we will also direct the owner to have the veterinarian, its veterinarian, assess the horse.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And are you able to share how many violations do HMH issued over the last five years for noncompliance?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Or adherence to the many forms and check-ins?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I have 2023 through April 2026.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So for lacking a record of a medical exam or the exam not within the timeframe, we've issued 17 summonses.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Missing proof of five weeks of furlough.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have 12.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'll come back for a second round.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about Mayor Adams' Executive Order 56 on worker transition, whether any workers were contacted, and about horse deaths (Ryder, Denise) and pre-death inspections. Ortiz says the first deputy mayor's office led the effort; Schiff addresses Denise's toxic plant ingestion.
chapters.title: Wong questions on Executive Order 56 and horse death inspections

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: A question regarding

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: and mayor's executive order.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Let me get to that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Mayor Eric Adams issued Executive Order 56 in September 2025, directing your agency,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: that's DCWP, to identify alternative employment for carriage workers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Between September 2025 and today, were any workers contacted?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And how many will offer services and how many will place the new jobs?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, my understanding at the time is the first deputy mayor's office actually ended up taking the lead on engaging with the industry on winding down pursuant to the executive order.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know they engaged in active conversations, particularly with labor partners, but also had staff operating in the field in Central Park to liaise with workers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: That's my understanding of the touch point of what the Adams administration ended up doing under that executive order.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So there's not the responsibility of DCW to reach out to the carriage workers?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We work closely with them to provide them contact information and data, and then the mayor's office ended up taking the lead on the next steps on that.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: You know, I think as I mentioned as well earlier in my testimony, workforce development is not necessarily something that DCBP currently administers or engages in.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are other agencies across the city that do that work.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And I think are part of the administration's now response to implementing this bill.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Question for DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Your agency has been responsible for inspecting and licensing carriage horses in the city.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The horse rider was cleared as fit to work, yet was found to be nearly 30 years old and suffering from a neurological disease.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The horse lady died in the street.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Another horse, Denise, died in the park.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Question, how many inspections were conducted on each of these horses in the 12 months before their deaths, and what did those inspections actually find?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So for Denise, I did want to note that

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: the report following Denise's death, as everyone will probably remember, was that that horse had ingested a toxic plant.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And I did want to highlight that our course for drivers includes the Parks Department requirement that horses not disturb the vegetation.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Well, were there any inspections done on these horses before they died?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We can pull up the certifications that the horses'veterinarians would have provided to us in order to be licensed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Areola.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks for the names of contracted equine veterinarians, raising concerns about vets with disciplinary histories. Schiff declines to provide names for privacy reasons but clarifies that owners' own vets certify horses, not DOHMH vets.
chapters.title: Ariola questions DOHMH on contracted veterinarian identities and oversight

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you for coming to testify.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So I have a question.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I would want to go back to the vet.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Who is the contracted vet for the Department of Health?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We have multiple contracted veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, who would be one that is a large animal veterinarian, an equine veterinarian?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The veterinarians, we have other veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The veterinarians I've been speaking about are equine veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
