utterances.text: Do you know the names of those veterinarians?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Or the one that you use most of the time?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There are multiple veterinarians, equine veterinarians that we work with.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And do you have their names?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I would rather not provide their names here.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Why would that be?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The equine veterinarians work with us at the health department under contract, and I would prefer not to provide their names.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is the reason why you do not want to provide their names is because they have been suspended in the past or relieved from their position at the ASPCA or have any type of history of using steroids

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: in horses or submitting certificates of...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Examination for horses to the department without doing those exams?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would that be why you wouldn't want to mention their names?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I'm not aware of those, the implications that you're making.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I don't, I'm trying to protect their privacy.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This is an issue that is very important because part and parcel of why this legislation is so important is because

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: we're just as concerned about the health of the horses, the health and well-being of the horses, and if we have compromised veterinarians that are

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: checking the health of the horses before they're going out onto the street, then that is a problem.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That is why it's important that this committee, and perhaps you'd like to share those names with the chair privately so that it's not made public.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: I do want to say that the veterinarians

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The veterinarians that certify the horses as fit to work are not health department veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Those are the veterinarians that the owners engage to be the veterinarians for their own horses.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And I don't have their names, but we would be happy to communicate privately.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So then what oversight do you have over the veterinarians that the owners choose to...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: To examine their horses, which could benefit them if they got a very good health report on their horse.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the horses need to be licensed in New York State and to sign off under their license.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: But again, to just remind everybody, we are here to support the intent of this bill.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We are here to provide reasons why the intent of this bill is necessary.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember De La Rosa.

chapters.summary: De La Rosa asks whether the administration would also support banning helicopters and pedicabs given tourism-related deaths. Ortiz says they're not prepared to discuss those industries today but is open to conversations.
chapters.title: De La Rosa questions administration on banning other tourism industries

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: First, I want to thank the Chair and the Speaker for their leadership on this issue, certainly one that is important for us to be taking up today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I'm the former Labor Chair of this Council, and so last session we had a spirited discussion around labor protections, and it is a part of the bill that I am interested in us digging into.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Would you agree, Commissioners, that

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: the horse-drawn carriage industry is part of the tourism industry in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think historically it's been a part of the tourist industry in New York City, yes.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Even including in new outreach materials that we've developed at the agency pursuant to council legislation, we've incorporated information on horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I want to also join the choir of council members.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I think all of us are reeling over the death of Roma and his family's pain is something that we all feel.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: A lot of us here on the council are parents.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Those of us that aren't parents can certainly see the pain and the anguish in these families'lives after this incident.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to ask you, however, about other families that have experienced similar deaths in New York City due to tourism-related activities.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In April 10, 2025, a sightseeing helicopter crashed into the Hudson, and six tourists in New York City were killed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In July 2021, a pedicab driver was hit by an SUV.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: The pedicab driver passed, and the two people who were in the pedicabs were injured.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Would the administration also be in favor of banning those industries as they exist today in New York City?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: You know, I think to answer your question directly, this is not something we're prepared to talk to you about today or not something that we've been presented on to discuss.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I don't think we're there yet necessarily with those industries, albeit I think we're open to any types of reforms.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I could say to the pedicab industry, for example.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think when it comes to horse-drawn carriages, I think your initial question, is it a part of the tourist industry in New York City?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think what this hearing is about is should it be, and our position is that it should not be.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Ultimately, there's a tragic death, and I'm a father as well, and I can't imagine the pain that this family is feeling right now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And on top of that, there's been deaths of animals, and it's seemingly happening every year.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: After robust efforts that many administrations have made to try and reform this industry, I think we are past the point of reform of the industry, and we're now at a place of looking to wind down the industry,

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: keeping in mind the livelihoods of workers and how we can phase those out responsibly as well.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I appreciate your response.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I would urge us to also look at those other industries because one death is one too many, whether it's a human death, an animal death, a person on a helicopter's death.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: You know, all of these people come to New York City to take advantage of the amenities that New York City have to offer.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And these activities have been part of the...

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: cultural and touristic experiences of people who come all over the world to visit our city.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So as we look to reform this industry, we should also be taking a look at those other industries.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I know Gail is brave and will get in a pedicab, but if an accident happens,

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: two years from now in a pedicab in Central Park, then we will be in the same position.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And I maintain that if previous administrations would have done what they needed to do, in 2026 we would not be here having this conversation once again.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's easy to say we could have passed a bill one year ago.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We could have passed a bill 10 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And still here we are in 2026.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So it is on all of us as we legislate to also look at what future industry issues could arise so that we're not creating a situation where we're ending one industry in exchange for another industry that is equally unsafe.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: DCP, I should say, has never shied away from a hard conversation about what we have to do in the city to improve outcomes for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And in particularly, I would just stress that the ethos of our agency is to deliver concrete outcomes that improve the lives of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So if that's a conversation that the council wants to have about those industries that we regulate in particular, I am quite willing to have that conversation.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Feliz.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about worker count, seasonal nature of employment, average salary, and whether city agencies could absorb displaced workers. Ortiz doesn't have salary data. Schiff notes the administrative code doesn't require reporting of medical incidents.
chapters.title: Feliz questions on worker employment details and horse incidents

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for this hearing, and thank you, DOH and DCWP, for your testimony and for all the work you've been doing on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I just want to start by briefly speaking in favor of the

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We've seen a lot of issues related to these horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Animal welfare issues, including horses literally dying in our streets.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Also escalating public safety incidents, including the incident that we saw last month.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: These are issues that cannot be resolved through legislation or reform or training.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We have to do what's right.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So just want to thank everyone who has joined today and also everyone who has been in this fight since day one.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So a few questions first about the workers and also then on the horses.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So you mentioned there's approximately 170 workers in the horse carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would say that there are 208 licensed horse carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: My understanding from some reporting is that not all of those folks are actively engaged in the work.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And then I don't know if we have a count necessarily yet on the amount of folks involved in the stables.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Is this seasonal work or is it year-round work?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And also, what's the general salary?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: What was the last part of the question?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: What's their average salary for these workers?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm not sure if I'm aware of the salary.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think that would be helpful to learn through this hearing process.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we see horse-drawn carriages out there throughout the year, albeit there are limitations when there are severe weather conditions that my colleague referred to on when they can operate.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are also time restrictions that have been put in place.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: There are geographic restrictions that have been put in place in this industry over the very many years.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, so what do you say this is seasonal employment?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Let's say like eight months.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Out of the 12 months per year?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I'm not sure if I can speak to if it's seasonal or not.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Would you say it could potentially be possible to, for example, if it's 200 workers, we obviously have a lot, as you know, we obviously have a lot of vacancies in almost every single city agency.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Do you think it could potentially be possible to bring all of these workers to our city agencies, or do we see any obstacles that are foreseeable?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I know that the city hiring process itself, I guess it would depend on what role, and certainly people can apply to positions that are available right now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: They can sign up for civil service exams right now.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: This option already exists for workers across the city.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: A few questions related to the horses.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: How many incidents related to medical issues have we had in the last, more or less, two years that you're aware of?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the administrative code does not require reporting to us of medical incidents.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So that is part of the challenge in responding to some of the data questions.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We certainly follow the news and get complaints that we follow up on and get referrals from NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The administrative code does not allow horses to be worked when they are ill or injured.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So that's part of why we don't have a count.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There have been counts in the press of incidents, and we would have those same counts based on our review of the news that we monitor.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Give us one second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're trying to see, we think one of the members is online.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're just trying to check on that, so give us a second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Schulman announces a five-minute break before resuming with public testimony.
chapters.title: Chair announces five-minute break

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank the admin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're going to take a five-minute break, and then we're going to resume with the rest of the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Ladies and gentlemen, please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The hearing's about to re-begin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Ladies and gentlemen, quiet on the floor.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Find your seats, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The hearing's about to start again.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please take the conversations outside.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet on the floor, quiet on the floor, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We will be starting momentarily.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're back in session.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to call up State Senator Eric Botcher.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's just me up here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's just you because your, I don't know, your colleagues were supposed to come.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I don't know where they are, but go ahead.

chapters.summary: State Senator Eric Bottcher testifies about representing Hell's Kitchen where stables are located, describes horses navigating midtown traffic, references horse deaths in his district, and urges passage while protecting workers.
chapters.title: State Senator Bottcher testifies in support of ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Shulman, members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: I represent New York State Senate District 47, which includes Hell's Kitchen, where the horse carriage stables are located, and parts of Central Park where these carriages operate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: And every day, I and my colleagues who also represent this district, Councilmember Carl Wilson, Councilmember Gil Brewer, we witness 1,500 pound animals

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: pulling carriages nose to tailpipe through Manhattan

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: gridlock behind buses, trucks, taxis, through construction sites.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: And I've watched this issue unfold firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Our district has been the site of multiple horse carriage deaths, including Ryder and Lady.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: And after years of seeing these tragedies repeat themselves, I've reached one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: unavoidable conclusion, the status quo is not sustainable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: And then, of course, we witnessed what so many of us feared would happen eventually, the loss of human life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Romange came to New York to experience the world's greatest city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Instead, his family experienced the most unimaginable tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: His death made clear what many of us have known for years, what the Central Park Conservancy has been saying, that tourist horse carriages are fundamentally incompatible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: With modern midtown Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: I want to make a very important point that this bill is the best path forward for the workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Now is the time to help them transition before the industry's decline leaves them with fewer options.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Waiting only makes this transition harder.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: As we move forward, the carriage drivers must be part of this process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: They must be kept informed every step of the way and have a seat at the table and be given access to new employment opportunities with the wages, benefits, health care, and job security that they do not currently have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Finally, I want to say that it is so important that we do not demonize each other during this process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: That includes...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: The drivers, the stable owners, the union, the animal welfare advocates, and the elected officials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: I want to thank my former colleagues in the City Council for supporting this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: I know firsthand that this is not an easy issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: When I was a sponsor of this bill, I had full-page ads taken out against me in the New York Post, and they were really nasty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: But leadership sometimes comes with a political cost, and I want to thank all of you for your courage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: And I know that one day we'll look back at this moment and be proud that New York City chose compassion, safety, and common sense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: I urge you to pass this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: As soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Senator.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I apologize, I was supposed to read something before the Senator spoke, so I'll do it now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank the members of the administration who were here earlier.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We are now opening the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to remind members of the public that this is a government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Furthermore, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recording to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: For inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: When recognized, you will have two minutes to speak on introduction 943.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of the adjournment of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to ask Assemblymember Rosenthal to please come up and testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, it's on now.

chapters.summary: Assemblymember Linda Rosenthal testifies about her decade-long efforts to ban carriages, lists names of dead horses, cites over 100 documented injury incidents, and urges passage of Romanch's Law.
chapters.title: Assemblymember Rosenthal testifies in support of ban

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Great, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Speaker Menon, Chair Schulman, Councilmember Marte, esteemed members of the Health Committee, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: I'm Assemblymember Linda B.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Rosenthal.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: I'm chair of the Assembly Housing Committee, and I proudly represent the Upper West Side and parts of Hell's Kitchen, as well as parts of Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Where tragically, Roman Shmahajan, an 18-year-old tourist from India, died after being thrown from a horse carriage on June 17, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: I'm also an ardent animal rights champion who has passed many laws protecting the rights and lives of New York's

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: most defenseless creatures, yet despite our collective progress, we still have much to accomplish when it comes to protecting the city's carriage horses from injury and abuse.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: And the way to safeguard these majestic creatures is to finally phase out the industry by passing Romach's law.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: In 2011, to combat the rising scourge of deaths and injuries of horses on our streets, I introduced state legislation to ban the use of horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: My intention was to spur the city to take action, but up until today, despite promises and agreements,

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Traffic collisions and dead horses, no bill has passed.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: But no one gave up.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Charlie, Tickler, Billy, Blackjack, Lady, Denise, Spotty, Juliet, Smoothie, Clancy, Tommy, Aisha, Misty, and Ryder.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Those are the names of some of the horses that have died in New York City as a result of their forced employment.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: There have been more than 100 documented incidences of other horses being badly injured on the job.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Some have been hit by cars, motorcycles, and buses.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Others have been badly spooked, endangering the lives of riders, carriage drivers, pedestrians, and of course the horses themselves.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: We can't wait for more horses or people to die on the streets in order to act.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Cities across the globe have far fewer tourist distractions, have shown us we don't have to tolerate animal abuse to churn a profit.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: I will wind up by saying the New York City Council and the administration can put an end to this cruelty once and for all.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: It's amazing what a change in administration and leadership can do.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: I applaud Councilmember Marte, all the co-sponsors, and of course the Speaker for putting forth this bill that will not only ban this archaic practice, it will also help to transition current carriage drivers into new jobs.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: One last thing, today would not be possible without the unyielding support and dedication to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: By all the advocates and to the many horses who have

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: lost their lives.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Thank you for honoring the life of Ramach Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: We will ensure that his death will not be in vain.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: It's high time we join other countries and cities in refusing to accept abuse as the cost of doing business.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Assemblymember.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No clapping, folks.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can go like this, but no clapping, all right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, now I'm going to call up the next panel, which is Ashley...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Byron?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Byrne.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Byrne.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ashley Byrne.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ashley Byrne here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Edie Falco.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Bob Holden.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Adita.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Burncrant?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, and then this is going to be a hybrid panel, folks.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We also have somebody, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Craig Kulikowski, is going to be joining us remotely.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, sure.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's going first?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Adita?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Ashley Byrne from PETA testifies that the industry is cruel and reckless, cites oversight failures, names dead horses including Ryder, Lady, and Denise, and urges passage of the ban.
chapters.title: Ashley Byrne (PETA) testifies in support of ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: My name is Ashley Byrne, and I am here speaking on behalf of People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: You cannot regulate the abuse and danger out of the horse-drawn carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: It is cruel and reckless to force a horse, a prey animal, to live and work in the noise, traffic, and chaos of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: The carriage operators fight and flout the most mild and common sense rules with little to no enforcement from the city, as you've heard today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Oversight is almost non-existent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Lame, sick, emaciated, elderly horses are regularly documented being forced to pull carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: This hearing is taking place in the middle of what has already proved to be a deadly summer delivered by the horse-drawn carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: The life of 18-year-old Romanch Mahajan was tragically added to the long death toll of horses whose lives have been claimed by this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Some are names that we know well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: The footage of Ryder, Lady, and Denise collapsing in our streets sparked widespread outrage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: and calls for a ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Many more have died in anonymity, often sold to slaughter, after spending their days in traffic being worked to exhaustion,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: and their nights in cramped, solitary, parking garage-like buildings denied everything natural, enriching, and comforting to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: New Yorkers have been fed up with this cruel, deadly industry for years, and polls have consistently shown that New Yorkers want the carriages banned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: We applaud Speaker Julie-Menin for listening to New Yorkers and taking courageous action to move this ban forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Thank you to Councilmember Christopher-Marte for sponsoring the bill, to Committee Chair Lynn Schulman for holding this hearing, and to the committee members for your strong inquiries into the practices of this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: And to the family of Romesh Mahajan, we send our deepest condolences and gratitude.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: It's time to pass Romesh's law.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Falco?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Like that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Actress and animal advocate Edie Falco testifies that animals are not ours to use, expresses horror at carriage conditions, and says naming the law for Romanch is the greatest honor of her lifetime.
chapters.title: Edie Falco testifies in support of ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Edie Falco.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I am an actress and I'm an animal lover and advocate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: For some reason, I was asked to be here today to tell people how I feel about this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: And I think I'm going to go way out on a limb here and say from the deepest part of my heart,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: These animals are not ours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: These animals are not ours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: They are not put on this planet to do our bidding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: They're not for us to look at and say, oh, that one's going to pull me through the park, and this one I'm going to eat, and this one I'm going to wear, and this one I'm going to experiment on for drugs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: You know, this is the wrong meeting for this perhaps, but insofar as my animal advocacy, these are not our animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: They have the right to a God-given, if that's what you believe, life just as we do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I have a fantasy of us looking back on this in 200 years and saying, remember that when we had those big, beautiful, majestic animals pulling us on carriages?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: The fact that there's anybody in this room that can look at an animal with the apparatus and the carriage and the people in the traffic and the sounds and the smells and the heat and say, sure, yeah, that's reasonable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: The first time I saw that 40 years ago, I thought, what is this?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's 40 years later.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I've looked face to face with politicians in this city since then who have said, no, we're on it, we're on it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's our platform.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: We're going to make it happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: To be here, I'm going to say very quickly, I'm an empath.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's what I do for a living, and I can't always turn it off.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: And to listen to the Romantics family and what they are going through knocked the wind out of me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I have an 18-year-old, and I came to this city as an 18-year-old with dreams and a life to start.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's 40 years later, and I've lived that dream 4,000 times over, and that I am here with the honor of potentially naming this law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: For Romant, for the future he will not have and the dreams he will not get to realize, is the greatest honor of my lifetime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's time, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: You can't pretend that you don't know what you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: No, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Bob Holden, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Holden.

chapters.summary: Former Councilmember Bob Holden, original author of the legislation, testifies that his bill would have banned carriages on June 1, 2026—16 days before Romanch died—and blames four years of obstruction by former Speaker Adrienne Adams.
chapters.title: Former Councilmember Holden testifies, blames obstruction for Romanch's death

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair, and thank you for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And I want to thank Speaker Menon and Chris Marte for taking up the banner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: My name is Bob Holden, and I had the honor of representing District 30 in Queens from 2018 to 2025.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And I'm the original author of the legislation before you today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The bill that began as writer's law and is now rightful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: fully Romantia's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: I introduced this bill in 2022 after a horse named Ryder, nearly 30 years old, malnourished and suffering

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: a disease, collapsed on 9th Avenue in a heat wave while his driver struck him with the reins.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: I spent the next four years begging the council, the former council that preceded this, for a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Four years trying to get a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Adrienne Adams, the Speaker, refused to allow it, flat out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And I was told that, that it would never get heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: A deal was made.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Year after year, she personally blocked this bill from reaching the floor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: No hearing, no vote, no voice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: 70% of New Yorkers who supported it, they had no voice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The Conservancy, Central Park Conservancy supports it, endorsed it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The mayor endorsed it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: She still said no.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Now I'm going to say something that's difficult to say, and the family I know is here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And my original bill would have banned horse-drawn carriages in New York City on June 1st of this year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Ramones died on June 17th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: That should be chilling to the members listening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: 16 days we're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: That is the cost of four years of obstruction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: An 18-year-old boy on his family's first trip to America celebrating his graduation is gone because this industry was allowed to keep operating 16 days past the date my bill would have shut it down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The hearing I fought for four years is now happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The bill is in front of you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The public is behind you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Pass Ramones'law now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Bottcher
utterances.text: Adita?

chapters.summary: Edita Birnkrant of NYCLASS testifies, sharing a statement from Anita McGill of Chateau Stables that some current carriage owners want out of the business, and argues hitching posts are dangerous and encourage drivers to walk away.
chapters.title: Edita Birnkrant (NYCLASS) testifies, shares stable owner's call for exit

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Thank you, committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: My name is Adita Bernkrant, executive director of NYCLASS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: For more than a decade, I've documented and reported the dangers of horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: But today, rather than repeat any of that, I want to amplify a voice that deserves this council's attention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Anita McGill, whose family owned and operated Chateau Stables, once the largest horse stable in New York City for nearly 50 years, reached out to me

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: because she says several carriage owners are now desperate for a way to leave this business before a ban goes into effect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: My name is Anita McGill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I am part of Chateau Stables, my family's business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: We owned the largest fleet of horse and carriages at our stable at 608 West 48th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: In 2018, we were the first to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: We sold our stable and semi-retired to our family farm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I've been asked to submit a statement by some current horse and carriage owners to seek some relief and get help for them to be offered an option to get out of the carriage business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: My opinion talking with them is the quaint leisure horse and carriage ride is no longer like it used to be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: They are now squeezing past city bikes, pedicabs, runners, baby strollers, and millions of extra park goers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: This is a safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I feel there are no more safety solutions that can be applied to lower the risk of more and more accidents happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Frederick Olmsted designed the park to be seen by horse and carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Yes, he did, but not in 2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Central Park is totally different just in the past 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It's exploded with millions more population and traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Business is way down, I hear from some owners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: There is nobody applying for a horse and carriage license.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Owners struggle to pay the board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: What is the benefit?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I hope the city allows the horse and carriage owners to get out graciously and compensate them so that they and their horses can live peacefully.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Also, she made a point about hitching posts, which Councilmember Gennaro is obsessed with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Hitching posts, to me, are only a recipe for more problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It encourages drivers to walk away and leave the horses alone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Not every horse accepts being tethered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: They can feel tension, panic, and back up and bust up what's behind them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Horses can rub their bridles off on hitching posts, creating a whole different set of problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So I think you know, please pass Romancia's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Intro 943, no more tragedies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, before this panel goes anywhere, we have some council members that have questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council member?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry, we have the hybrid, I forgot.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Craig Kulikowski, you have two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Linda B. Rosenthal
utterances.text: They know.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Kulikowski, are you online?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are we having technical issues?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Because he says he's online.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, we'll come back to him.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In the meantime, Councilmember Wong has questions, and we have another councilmember also with questions.

chapters.summary: Wong questions Holden about the workforce provisions he authored. Holden says most drivers are independent contractors without benefits and questions TWU's representation of management.
chapters.title: Wong questions Holden on workforce provisions

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Councilman Holden, I still call you Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Critics of this bill have argued it would destroy jobs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And when I was getting my water, someone also told me the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: As the original author of the workforce provisions in this legislation, what is your response to that argument?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Well, first of all, there's some lies out there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Most of the drivers, the vast majority, are independent contractors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: That means they're not getting benefits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: I'd like to know, I've asked TWU, how many union members actually are drivers, real drivers, independent contractors?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: How many are paying union dues, and what benefits do they get?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: So right now, I would say, in just talking to some of the drivers, they don't get any benefits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: If they have city jobs, well-paying city jobs, they'll get health coverage, a pension, and they'll get other things that go along with regular jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: It's a big scam that's going on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And if the TWU would just reveal how many dues-paying union members they have, we might get somewhere, but we won't get that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: I never got that in four years, over four years of actually trying to get some movement on this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: So there's not great paying jobs out there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: These are day workers or independent contractors.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Gennaro.

chapters.summary: Gennaro questions PETA's Ashley Byrne about whether the organization addressed toxic Japanese yew plants in Central Park after Denise's death. Byrne says horses shouldn't have been in the park to eat the plants. Gennaro says he filed a bill request about toxic plants.
chapters.title: Gennaro questions PETA on toxic plants in Central Park

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I think I'm going to start from my right and go left.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I wrote down some of the names.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: First would be to Ashley from PETA.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: When you found out, or when PETA found out, that Denise had, the horse Denise had died,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: from eating toxic Japanese yew plants, you being a premier animal rights organization, did you do anything?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Yes, we pointed out that Denise never should have been in Central Park in the first place, and that if his driver was attending to him properly, he wouldn't have been eating the vegetation in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: And if his driver was following regulations, he wouldn't have eaten the vegetation.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Well,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Let's kind of expand this a little bit.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So we have toxic plants in the park that are toxic to every species that is native to the park,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: species that migrate through the park, people walking their dogs in the park, stray cats, whatever.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Every single animal is subject to being poisoned or killed by Japanese yew.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And as an animal rights organization, did you say anything to the Central Park Conservancy about this was not the kind of plant that should be in a park with such a diverse array of animals?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Did you address the concerns?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Forget about the horses right now because, you know, Denise is already dead.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I, you know, as someone who's been an equestrian for 60 years, I know that horses graze.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Have you ever driven by a field of horses where even one was not grazing?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: That's not how this works.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But anyway, moving to other animals, did you have any concerns about any of the other animals in the park, which your agency is supposedly all about?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yes or no?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: What you just pointed out about horses grazing is precisely one of the reasons they don't belong in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Could you please answer my question?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: They're denied everything natural to them and forced to be in an environment where they don't belong.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Hey, I get to ask the questions and you answer my questions.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So my question is,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Did PETA do anything about the presence of toxic plants in the park?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: Are you asking if we started a campaign to ban a plant?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No clapping, no clapping.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I asked the questions here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You put order with the general.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Come on.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: When I heard about the plants, I issued a statement.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I put in a bill request about toxic plants in New York City parks.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I wrote the Central Park Conservancy.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So I guess I'm more of an animal lover than you are.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Edie Falco.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ashley Byrne
utterances.text: That's cute, but the fact is that the horses should not have been in the park to eat the plants in the first place.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Edie Falco.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council Member Gennaro, your time is up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council Member Hanif.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks Falco how she responds to people who believe supporting horse welfare and carriage drivers are mutually exclusive. Falco says things change and workers need to move on, but she's here for the horses who can't speak.
chapters.title: Hanif questions Falco on horse welfare vs. worker concerns

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I want to just first say to Edie, I'm a big fan.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And I love The Sopranos.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: It is my favorite show.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I would love to hear from you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: How do you respond to people who are

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: who believe that supporting horse welfare and supporting carriage drivers are mutually exclusive.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Why do you think it's important to care about both?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: As an animal lover, I am here on behalf of the animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I'm an advocate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Things change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: Life changes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: You know, the tobacco farmers had to find other things to do when people started to say, you know, this is killing us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: We can't smoke as much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: You know, I think you learn that when it's time to move on, it's time to move on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I think it is deeply important to make sure that these people who have spent their lives as carriage drivers, potentially the lives of their parents and their families, it's very important to look out for them, for sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: And I'm so glad that the speaker has made that a big part of what she's doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: other people here, I am here to speak for the horses, you know, because they can't say, like a pedicab driver, when you say to him, you know, do you want to pedal?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: These people all the way up here in 90 degree heat, he could be like, you know what, no, it's too hot, I'm not doing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: You know, the horse can't say it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: And I think for those of us who are moved to do so, because you think it is right and humane, someone's got to look out for them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: So that is my priority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: It's my reason for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edie Falco
utterances.text: I believe the humans will most definitely be taken care of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I'm here for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, and thank you for using your voice and platform, Years of Advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next person is Gail Brewer.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks Birnkrant about the stable owners who want out. Birnkrant says they're desperate to leave, afraid of another spooking incident, and want compensation. Brewer notes the stables sit on valuable real estate.
chapters.title: Brewer questions Birnkrant on owners wanting to exit industry

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Klaas for that conversation that you just shared with us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, because that's not something that we've heard before.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, do you think then at this point, what's next?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do they as owners feel...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They could contribute to ending this carriage horse industry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was quite shocking and surprising and gleeful what you just said.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I think there's a lot of fear among some of these people to speak out, but I think they are desperate to get out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: They are afraid of another horse spooking and killing someone because it's happening so often.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: And I think they want to get out of this business even before a ban goes into effect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: And she said that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: The several that she's speaking with, there are many more, but many are afraid to speak out for fear of retaliation or whatnot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So, yes, I think that there is a strong feeling that they want to get out now and they would like to be compensated, which I know is already in the legislation, but I think that there's a worry that that won't happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They do sit on very expensive real estate, so that will be helpful to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, we have Councilmember Feliz.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about worker pay (~$60,000/year, seasonal, no benefits as independent contractors) and how often horses are left unattended. Birnkrant says it happens daily and presents video taken the day before showing a driver stepping away with a baby in the carriage.
chapters.title: Feliz questions on worker pay and unattended horse incidents

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you again, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you all for being here, and also thank you for all the work you do on this issue, using your voice to help the voiceless.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So two questions.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I know time is very limited, so I'll be fast.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Two questions.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: First, thinking about the transition phase and helping the workers with employment, to your knowledge, approximately how much do these workers...

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Earn and also is it full-time employment seasonal i asked these questions to the administration didn't have any answers so wondering if you i have industry spokes people have said that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: i think 60 000 a year but it is seasonal work because for many months out of the year they're in the winter there's no business

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: it is an independent contractor job i've spoken with carriage drivers who were injured badly injured

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: if they are injured they simply get no income, they have no health insurance, they have no worker compensation, no overtime, no sick leave, no worker protections of any kind because they're not classified as employees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Why would TWU be representing wealthy millionaire carriage owners who misclassify their workers and deny them even the most basic worker protections, guaranteed salary,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: no kind of living wage, no benefits of any kind?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: That's a question to ask TWU, but industry spoke, and there's been many carriage drivers quoted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: during heat wave saying, I can't pay my bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: We can't survive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: This is not a lucrative job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: You can Google these things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: They're all coming from the horse's mouths, the horse carriage driver's mouths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So I have long wondered why a union would be representing this kind of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: setup of a labor situation when there are real union jobs on the table of guaranteed full-time jobs with salaries, worker protections, benefits, health insurance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: You know, 20 years ago, there was a carriage driver who nearly died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: He was thrown from the carriage, suffered a skull fracture, and was in a coma for months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: And his wife was interviewed saying that he was often scared driving the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So 20 years ago, we nearly lost the life of a carriage driver in the same way that Romange died 20 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, right, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And also my second question is, as we know, leaving a horse carriage unattended can create the public safety risks that we continue to see in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm just curious, how often have we seen that be the case?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Literally every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I have a video which I would love to show to all the council members, especially Council Member Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: This carriage driver is doing exactly what got Romange killed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: He's on a busy street, busier than where Cherry Hill is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: There's a cop car behind him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: He's in Grand Army Plaza in traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: He is literally stepping far away from the carriage with a baby in the carriage and a family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Videos?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So this was taken yesterday, doing exactly what got Romange killed, but in more danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: If this horse had spooked and taken off, a baby could be dead, a family, and everyone in traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It happens every single day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I have hours and hours of footage of this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: And when reported to the city, they do nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: They're not capable of enforcing these laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: We saw the poor testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I think it's just not possible to run after 68 carriages who don't give a damn about the safety and welfare of their own horses or the public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It's not going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So we've got to shut it down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member Feliz.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: A couple of things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is I just want to mention that when the Department of Work and Consumer Protection testified today, they said that drivers make $70,000.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I want to point that out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It was in their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to acknowledge that we've been joined by Council Member Ressler, and Council Member Marte has questions.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you all for being here and thank you former Councilmember Bob Holden for your leadership on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I know we've we stood together for a real long time and you know I'm glad that you're here to play a part in this hearing as well.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I wanted just to piggyback on the questioning of Councilmember Felice.

chapters.summary: Marte asks Birnkrant about documentation of unattended horses. She describes finding about 10 unattended horses in a brief park visit, says drivers take photos for tips as part of their business model, and has hours of footage.
chapters.title: Marte questions Birnkrant on unattended horse documentation

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Adita, how often do you see unintended horses in Central Park and how many documented episodes do you have that illustrates what we know is reality in the park?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I mean, I've been involved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: I've been involved in animal advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: different facets, the wild horse issue, wildlife, urban wildlife, but also the horse carriages for almost 20 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It has been happening for 20 years so constantly that at a certain point documenting it and reporting it became futile, but I still do it all the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: The day after TWU held their press conference saying that they had taken extraordinary measures and retrained, I just stopped by the park for a brief period of time, and in that period I found about

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: 10 horses just from 6th Avenue and 7th Avenue being left unattended.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Some of them, no drivers anywhere around.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Drivers taking pictures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It's literally happening every day, multiple times a day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: And I believe there's going to be testimony from a pedicab driver who's in the business for 23 years who can attest to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It is in part of their...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Advertisements is that like the the ride that Romange took they take three pictures
