utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: who's taking the picture the carriage drivers get out this is how they make tips is by taking pictures and leaving the horses unattended but not only that they're left unattended on the hack lines

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: they're left unattended in areas they're not even supposed to operate and at any moment those horses can spook and run but it's important to say that many of the worst spooking crashes and injuries have occurred when the driver is in the driver's seat in control

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: because when a horse spooks and takes off

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It often can't be stopped.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It's a huge, powerful animal with a flight drive, and they...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: take off and that has happened many times but clearly just leaving your horses abandoned in the middle of one of the busiest parks in the world is a very reckless thing to do and they're still taking pictures

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: yesterday I have the video I will submit it of a family with a toddler in the carriage right in Grand Army Plaza with a cop car behind them and a lot of chaos going on

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: He did this in the open because there's really, I just don't know that there's any other way that they know how to operate this business, and that's why it needs to be shut down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: We can't expect DOH to run after 68 carriages constantly preventing them from killing people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: It's not going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you for your advocacy and leadership on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Do you want to respond?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: If I could just elaborate a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: How could you think that a prey animal like a horse, 1,500 to 2,000 pounds, will not get spooked again, even with the driver, like Adita said, with the driver at the reins?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: It can happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: It can happen any time in a more crowded and a noisier city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: This is going to happen again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: And we're just fooling ourselves to think that we can control that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: We can't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: So let's wake up, folks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: This is the handwriting's on the wall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: This industry has to go by the wayside, and we will retrain the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: The city will get great jobs, period.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Who would be against that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Except the owners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Why the TWU?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: I never heard of a union representing management.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bob Holden
utterances.text: Have you?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Bernkrant, can I ask you a question?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So if you would be kind enough to give us specific amendments to the council of what you'd like to see changed in the bill, we would like to get that from you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: So if you could submit that, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel very much for taking the time to be here today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really, really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to call up now Borough President Brad Hoylman-Siegel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And we have...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Erica Sullivan, who works for Assemblymember Deborah Glick.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is she here?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Borrow President, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Manhattan Borough President Brad Hoylman-Sigal presents data analysis showing 96 serious carriage incidents and 40 horse deaths since 1982, with incident rates quadrupling. A Columbia University forecast model predicts 16-30 more incidents by 2030 without action.
chapters.title: BP Hoylman-Sigal presents carriage incident data analysis

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Shulman, members of the committee, Council of Ramarte, sponsor of Ramantia's Law, and thank you, Speaker Julie-Menin, for your support of this legislation and holding this hearing and being here personally.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: It means a lot.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: I'm Brad Hoylman Siegel.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: I have the honor of serving 1.6 million New Yorkers as Manhattan Borough President.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: You know, Times Square was once lined with blacksmiths and stables and home to the Vanderbilt's American Horse Exchange.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: In fact, my extended family once ran a small business called Miller's Harnesses.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Even though Miller's Harnesses is no longer in business, and there have been dramatic changes in the transport of goods and services and people in Manhattan, as we all know, the horse carriage industry continues to operate on the busiest pathways of Central Park,

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: the world's third most visited tourist attraction.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Today we're reckoning not only with an industry many say needs to be more tightly regulated, if not outlawed altogether, but the death of a young man, Ramach

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Mahajan, who came to New York for what was supposed to be the trip of a lifetime, and who fairly assumed, along with his family, that when they took a city-regulated tourist ride, it would be safe.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: That was an avoidable tragedy, one no family should ever have to face.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: The death of anyone, especially a young person, should be enough to demand change.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: But for my mind,

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: My testimony today, my team and I wanted to take a step back and look at some sober statistics around the issue.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: So we reviewed public records, including the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene data, for every documented report of carriage horse incidents in and around Central Park dating back to 1982.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: What we found was evidence of 96 serious carriage horse incidents, including human injuries, and 40 horse deaths.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Between 1982 and 2026, the annual incident rate has more than quadrupled.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: In just under half those cases, a horse died.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: And when a horse escapes its carriage, our data show that a person is hurt over half the time.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: The trend is only getting worse.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Over the last 13 months alone, we counted eight incidents, more than double the pace the industry has averaged since 2019.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: We collaborated with a professor from the mathematics department at Columbia University on a forecast model

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: based on this data set.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: If the pattern holds, our forecast shows we could see 16 to 30 more carriage incidents by 2030,

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: almost certainly with human injuries, and upwards of 11 more horse deaths.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: New Yorkers are concerned about this trend line too.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: We've seen the recent

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Polling data by Zenith Research that shows 68% of respondents supported ending horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: In concert with this finding, 311...

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: One data shows 175 horse-related animal abuse complaints since 2020, two-thirds of them from inside two streets of the park in the industry's own stables.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: It's clear the status quo is unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: That's why I joined my colleagues in co-sponsoring Romance's Law.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: But any serious case for winding this industry down has to reckon with the hundreds of workers who will be at risk of losing their livelihood.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: I want to be clear that the drivers doing this job every day are not to blame for these tragedies.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: And the city has an obligation to collaborate with them on finding a professional landing.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Intro 943 directs DCWP to build a workforce transition program.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: I urge this committee and DCWP to make sure it is funded and staffed before drivers lose their routes.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Intro 943 also prohibits selling or transferring any horses for slaughter or into other city carriage businesses.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Last September, two dozen equine rescues and sanctuaries offered to place every horse this industry retires.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: I hope this council, and I know this council, will work with that coalition to ensure a smooth and safe transition.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: The full horse incident record, a review of our 311 data, and our analysis is available online in our report, Should New York City Ban Horse Carriages?

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Here's what 44 years'worth of data shows.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: In honor of Ramach and in tribute to his loving family, who I understand are

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: with us today, I urge the council to pass Romancia's law.

utterances.speaker: Brad Hoylman-Sigal
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sullivan?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you can keep it brief, we would appreciate that.

chapters.summary: Erica Sullivan reads testimony from Assemblymember Deborah Glick strongly supporting the ban, comparing carriages to circuses the city has already outlawed, and calling for an end to the debate.
chapters.title: Erica Sullivan reads Assemblymember Glick's testimony supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: My name is Erica Sullivan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: I'm here from New York State Assemblymember Deborah Glick's office to read her testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify in strong support of the passage of Introduction 943

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: to ban the operation of horse-drawn carriages in New York City, ensure the drivers a transition to new employment, and retire the horses to sanctuaries for their healing and freedom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages are an outdated industry where animals are exploited for profit as entertainment, comparable to the use of wild animals in circuses, which the city has outlawed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: The incidents involving horse-drawn carriages are predictable results of horses being put to work in our city and represent preventable chaos and tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: We learn the news of horses fleeing and colliding with cars, resulting in injury, collapsing and taking their last breaths on our streets, and the tragic death of Ramanj Mahajan, to whose family and friends I extend my sincere condolences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Horses should not be subjected to breathing in exhaust fumes, dressed in costume with blinders on, and living in buildings that are ill-suited to comfortably shelter them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Even on our city's most beautiful day, this attraction

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Is antiquated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: 15 years ago, we were having the same discussion, with calls for replacing horse-drawn cabs with electric vehicles to carry visitors through our parks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: It's time for this debate to finally end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Horses will flee when frightened and collapse when overworked, and no amount of tighter

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: reins, hitching posts, studies, regulation, or reform could effectively avoid or alter the innate behavioral responses of these powerful animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: The only way to avoid the next tragedy involving horses is to pass Intro 943 and ban horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Sullivan
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and attention.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and thank you, Brother President, for taking the time to come and do this today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next panel is Kana McHugh.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: John Chee.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember General had a question for you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, we have to, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: John Cielo.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Gabriel Cook, Christina Hansen, Angel J., and Cherise Dubois.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, we'll make sure.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's going first?

chapters.summary: John Chiarello, President of TWU Local 100, testifies representing 44,000 union members including carriage drivers, gives condolences to the Mahajan family, compares carriage safety favorably to other transportation, and asks the council to table the bill.
chapters.title: John Chiarello (TWU Local 100 President) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So good afternoon, City Council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Julie-Menin, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Shulman, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I'm John Chiarello, president of TW Local 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I represent 44,000 union members across this great city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: And I also proudly represent the horse and carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: First, I would like to give my sincerest condolences to the family of Raminash Naharan, killed in an accident last month, tragic and unforeseen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Our hearts go out to him and his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: My members in the horse and carriage industry are mostly immigrant workers and laborers that are in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: What about my members'American dream?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Councilman Marte was nice enough to give statistics on the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Let's talk about racetracks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That in the last 10 years, 10,000 horses have died in racetracks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't see anything happening there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Let's talk about the food delivery services that run right past this building that nearly ran me down and maim and kill people every single day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Electric bikes, Amazon boxes that are propelled by electric means that do not have a license on them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Nobody's talking about that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: But this small microcosm of an industry that has been iconic to Central Park is now under attack, and it's been under attack for quite some time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: There's a 165-year history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I would put them up against any other means of transportation, and I all but guarantee you that they've done worse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: The other people that aren't my people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Uber, pedestrians are run down all the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Are we banning Uber?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: No, we're not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So where is the justice?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: The animal rights advocates talk about the horses not being treated fairly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That's another misrepresentation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: They get fed and they get five weeks of vacation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I am asking the city council to table.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Hey, decorum in the room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That's all right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Everybody has their moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I've been very patient, so I'm going to finish this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I'm asking the City Council to table this and also take up Councilman Gennaro's offer of making a safer place in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: This is, the horse and carriage business has been around before the city was even incorporated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: It's part of the history of this city, and it would be a shame for tourism to annihilate them in one swoop of the hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: And as far as union jobs, I don't even know what the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: that looks like, and I don't know what it is, but if you're a horse and carriage driver, I don't think you wanna be a host at a Racino.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't think you're looking to do hotel bell hopping or reception.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So I would caution the council on what they put in this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: And speak with the union, Local 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We move this city, and I want to thank everybody for their time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next on the panel?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Conor McHugh, a carriage driver for 40 years and medallion owner, testifies about the industry's history, claims advocates have harassed drivers and caused incidents, blames Central Park Conservancy for planting toxic yews, and urges the council to drop the bill.
chapters.title: Conor McHugh (carriage driver/owner) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: Connor McHugh, thank you for having the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: I've been a carriage driver for 40 years, a carriage medallion owner for 36 years, and a stable manager for 22 years, all collectively at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: I will tell you that being a carriage driver in Central Park is one of the best jobs you can have in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: There are 68 horse-drawn cab medallions in New York City, and collectively we have invested millions of dollars over the years in our business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: Taking a horse and carriage ride in Central Park is one of the most iconic things to do in New York City, and people from all over the world know about us and travel to New York to come for a carriage ride in the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: A ride in the park is folklore.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: In the past 20 years, there's been a concerted effort to drive us off our land on the west side and out of our buildings in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: And this coincides with the fact that we are a very small town.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: with the rezoning of the area now referred to as the Hudson Yards, where all of our stables are located.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: And this intimidation has taken various forms over the years between reporting us to ICE and the IRS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: Keyboard warriors trashing us on social media and animal rights volunteers who have come to Central Park to harass our drivers and the business in general and create an unsafe situation in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: It is no wonder that their actions have caused incidents and accidents over the year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: On more than one occasion, some of these people, the people who testified here before you today, have been arrested for attacking and assaulting carriage drivers and our customers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: And I'm sure you're well aware that New York class over the years had to pay thousands of dollars in fines because of campaign finance violations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: As we know from history, the best way to steal people's land is to ridicule and demonize and ultimately bankrupt them until they no longer can pay their bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: In Ireland, the British planted yew trees and shrubs on the land that they stole from us in order to poison the peasants'livestock if they wandered into their lands.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: Central Park Conservancy, I see, has adopted the same policy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: While paying themselves tens of millions of dollars in salary, they have planted poisonous yews all over Central Park, and nobody knows who's been poisoned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: by them, except that thankfully we were able to identify what happened to our horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: God knows how many horses they have poisoned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: And just like the Brits, they blame the victim.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: In addition, the Central Park Conservancy has introduced in the last 10 years or so approximately 800 motorized pedicabs to Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: I know the Speaker did a study on this, and you found that 93% of the pedicabs operating in Central Park are motorized illegally, and as you know, almost none of them are actually licensed at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: And they ramshackle through Central Park every day, but you want to blame us for the racket that has been created in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: I beseech you to drop this bill completely and instead consider Jim Gennaro's bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: If you pass this, this is a total act of vandalism and a miscarriage of justice on trumped-up charges, the worst that New York City will ever perpetrate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Conor McHugh
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Christina Hansen, TWU shop steward and carriage driver for 20 years, testifies about the industry's long history, safety record, and her personal bond with her horse Sherman. She questions what job the city would give her and asks about working with police horses.
chapters.title: Christina Hansen (TWU shop steward) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: My name is Christina Hansen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I am the shop steward for TW Local 100 Central Park Carriages and a carriage driver.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I have been a commercial carriage driver in Philadelphia and in New York City for 20 years this month.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And I've been here in New York for 14 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: It's hard to fit a 5,000.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: four centuries of history and decades of experience into two minutes of testimony, but at least it's longer than a TikTok.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: It's good to be here in the same room with all of you, in person and not online in some comment section.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Horses helped build this very building 200 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: There's George Washington and his horse, old Blueskin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: But at the same time that we in this industry have been devastated, have been mourning the Mahajan family, we are now being forced to fight for our own.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I have noticed that many people in this room have spent more time creating content for social media on this issue than coming to the stables, petting our horses, and meeting our drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And unlike the last panel that purports to speak for us,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: In the rest of this hearing, you are going to hear from our drivers, our carriage owners, and you can hear from them about how much they love their horses, and who are other people to speak for our horses than the people that know them best.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Horse-drawn cabs have been licensed and regulated by the city since 1692.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Our rates have been set by the city since 1813.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And we've been giving rides in Central Park since the day the park opened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: So that is the rental horse licensing and protection law, which was enacted in 1981.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: We are the definition of a lawful, regulated business, and our safety statistics bear it out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: If anything has changed in...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Burrow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: President Hoylman's testimony in the last 40 years is smartphones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: People are disconnected from reality, but at the same time they can post things they see online all the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: History has shown us that absolutely horrible things have been done in the name of progress, and many beautiful things are destroyed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Opponents of the carriage industry have weaponized push polls to make everybody say, oh, the majority of New Yorkers want this band.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: That has not been borne out by our unions polls of Central Park users.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: 70% of Central Park users found that carriage horses add beauty and charm to the park, and 60% opposed a ban when this was done last fall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: My real-world experience bears this out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: The smiles we see from people in Hell's Kitchen, the delight in children petting horses in the park, that's real-world experience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: We were closed for six months during the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: We went into the pandemic with 68 carriages and three stables, and we came out with 68 carriages and three stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Think of all the New York City businesses that we lost, and now you're sitting here and trying to lose another one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: There's been a lot of talk about job retraining, workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I am a horse person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I grew up in Lexington, Kentucky, horse capital of the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I own a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: His name is Sherman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: He's 12 years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I expect him to live for another 20 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And this bill is telling me that I can't go work with my horse in another place to pull a horse and carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And I would wonder then if I could have my horse as a rental riding horse in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: The borough president just said his family's from Miller's Harness Company.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Miller's Horse Company from Manhattan Saddlery started up riding horses in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: What about the mounted unit?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Could I get a job with them in the stable or riding as a police officer?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: Those horses are here in Midtown, in traffic, in the streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: They're owned by the city, but that's a good city job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: You know, and that's what I am skilled at.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I don't need to be told to go work in a hotel or something like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: I am a horse person, so will the city be giving me a job with one of your police horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christina Hansen
utterances.text: And who's next?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Cook?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Yes, can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Edita Birnkrant
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: First, I am so sorry for the tragedy that brings us all here today.

chapters.summary: Dr. Gabriel Cook, a board-certified equine surgeon providing medical care to carriage horses, testifies the horses are not abused or neglected, warns that displaced horses will end up in slaughterhouses, and calls the bill a death sentence for many horses.
chapters.title: Dr. Gabriel Cook (equine surgeon) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: My name is Gabriel Cook, and I'm a board-certified equine surgeon, and I provide medical care to many of the New York City carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Four years ago, members of the industry reached out to me and asked if I'd be willing to offer more consistent medical care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I agreed with the understanding that my examinations would be more critical in nature.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Complete physical examinations would always be performed and horses would be trotted during the examinations to ensure soundness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: If found unfit in any way, the horse would be temporarily suspended until healthy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, he is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I also provide telehealth consultations when needed and made my tertiary referral.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: hospital available to them 24 hours a day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I made it clear that I would be an advocate for the horse and they accepted wholeheartedly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I will not agree with the other panel that horses should not work and we can get into that later if you'd like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Having been doing this for the past four years, let me tell you what I do know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: These horses are not abused.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They are not neglected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They are not starved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They are not overworked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They are provided with proper stabling, clean stalls, good ventilation, and as much hay, grain, and water as they desire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Stablemen attend to these horses 24 hours a day, a standard beyond what I am used to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Let me tell you what I also know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Despite the intention of the council's bill to guarantee the welfare of these horses, it is not possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: There are already innumerable horses unsuccessfully seeking homes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I work with sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I work with rescues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They are underfunded.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They struggle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And sometimes they overburden themselves with horses, and the horses don't get the proper feed there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I am in the trenches.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I see these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I do this seven days a week for the last 35 days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And I will tell you this, they will, and when they do become displaced, they will end up in slaughterhouses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I have seen it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They will end up in auctions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They will be euthanized because people don't want to starve these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: This is not okay, folks, and it's not right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And finally,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Having worked in this industry for the past four years, I am a

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: an agreement that the industry has its challenges, and almost all of them related to failure in regulation and enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: As you so kindly predicted, sir, that we'd be talking about regulation and enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Almost all of my frustration has involved this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I've had frustrations resolving this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It is extremely disappointing that no New York City agency or the New York City government has been willing to take control and responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I truly, truly believe that with the proper regulation, enforcement, collaboration, hear the word collaboration, and teamwork, this could be a robust, healthy, and safe industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: To ban it now without making that attempt would be unjust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I thank you all very much for your time, and I would be extremely eager to answer lots of questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Angel?

chapters.summary: Angel, a carriage driver, testifies about his love for his horses and his work, denies mistreatment, and pleads to keep his livelihood and his horses.
chapters.title: Angel (carriage driver) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: Hi, so my name is Angel, and I've been doing this for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I'll be driving.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Bring the mic a little bit closer to you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, it's all right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: So my name is Angel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I've been working with horses almost all my life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I love what I do, and I just want to make sure the guys that are our horses stay in our hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: We have family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: Everybody here in this business, we have families to support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I'm one of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I have two kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And I really don't want to see my horses go away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I love what I do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And I wouldn't do anything wrong to one of my horses ever, ever.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: A lot of these people, they say that we have used the horses, that we don't take care of the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And that's not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I'm here because I love what I do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I love my horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: My family, they know that I love what I do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And they don't want to see me giving up on what I do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: So everybody talks and says that our horses, they're not in a good health, that we don't take care of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And like I said before, it's not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: It's not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: My kids, they know that I spend so many times, almost all the time, taking care of my horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And even Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: Cook, he knows that every time I have a question, I call him, I make sure to do the right thing with my horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And like I said, everybody here has had a family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: And to end up with this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: business today, I don't think this is the right thing to do, guys, to be honest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: I love my job, and I wouldn't do anything else.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Cherise?

chapters.summary: Cherise DeBose, Political Director for TWU Local 100, criticizes the legislative process, says council members signed on before hearing from workers, raises Fifth Amendment property concerns, and questions sanctuary funding and horse placement plans.
chapters.title: Cherise DeBose (TWU Political Director) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Shulman, committee members, staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: My name is Cherise DeBose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: I'm the political director for TWU Local 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: We appreciate the opportunity to be able to testify today, but I would be remiss if I did not begin to express our disappointment with this legislative process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Many members of this council have chose to co-sponsor Intro 943 before ever sitting down with the workers whose lives would forever be changed by this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Before speaking with the TWU, before hearing from the families who have devoted generations to this industry, and before this public hearing was even held, many council members had already decided the outcome.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: This is not what meaningful public participation looks like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Public hearings should exist to inform legislative decisions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Not simply ratified decisions that have already been made like a kangaroo court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Our members deserve due process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: They deserve to be heard before the city council votes to eliminate their livelihoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: You say that people don't want this business, but this business has survived two pandemics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: The Civil War, Superstorm Sandy, there's so many things that occurred in this city, and this business is still here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: The Fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution prohibits government from taking private property for public use without just compensation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Nobody can say what's going to happen to them financially and what will happen to the contributions that they have already made and the investments that they've already made to operate this business that was regulated by this city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Where are the sanctuaries that you speak of?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: How many horses have been committed to placements?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Who is paying for the decades of feed, veterinary care, transportation, and maintenance?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Those questions remain unanswered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Our members consider these horses part of their families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: They do not want them sold at auctions or placed in uncertain circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Ironically, this council recognized in this year's budget just how expensive animal care can be by funding assistance for low-income pet owners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Central Park belongs to every New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: It does not belong to the exclusively wealthy donors and advocacy organizations or private conservancies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: We offer our condolences as well to the Mahjong family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: But last year, also a pilot named Sean Johnson, Augustine Escobar, his wife, and three children died in a helicopter that crashed in the Hudson, celebrating one of their children's ninth birthdays.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: You can never say what could happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: I pray to God that we all could.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Not have these tragedies and avoid them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: But that's the fabric of our lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: We can't avoid these tragedies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: It's not, and I'm not holding any industry accountable for any wrongdoing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: But like Ramach, their deaths are also very tragic and heartbreaking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: But their deaths have not been tied to a political agenda.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Transit workers work very hard and some of them have died on their jobs along with other members in the labor movement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: And sometimes we don't even get an offer of condolence from anybody that's sitting in this room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: And I pray that that also change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: I appreciate your time today to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have some questions by the council members.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: First, Speaker Menon has questions.

chapters.summary: Menin questions Chiarello on average driver salary ($65,000-$70,000), benefits (dental and optical only, no medical), and the union's position on testing after the fatal incident. She commits to sitting with workers on additional language.
chapters.title: Menin questions TWU on driver salary, benefits, and training

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First of all, I really want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We hear the emotion and passion in your voice for this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: John, a couple, thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: A couple questions for you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Could you, there's been some testimony today about salary.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Could you sort of really clarify this?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What is the average driver's salary?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What are the benefits that are offered to the drivers as well?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So the average salary is anywhere from $65,000 to $70,000.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Now, as far as the benefits, understand there's an independent portion to this thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: They're not civil servants, so we're not able to bargain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We're actually bargaining with the city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That's what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So they're an association under our...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Our charter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: And as far as benefits, we could offer them dental and eyeglass.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: The major medical is a whole different issue.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Is there a collective bargaining agreement that covers the city's horse carriage drivers currently?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: None that I know of other than the charter that allows them into Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And after the fatal incident, TWU issued a statement calling for an increase in carriage driver training and implementation of more rigorous testing of driver applicants.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What is the union's position on the city's existing testing and oversight?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Well, testing and oversight is great, but we've also went above and beyond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We have a whole safety department.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We've collaborated with our shop steward, Christine, in making sure that all the members who you've heard testimony from, and most of them are here today,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: You know, this is their livelihood, and I don't think that they take it for granted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: And accidents do happen, as we always say, and I cry like everyone for the family, the Maharaj.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: It's a horrible, horrible, horrible thing, and there's no question about it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You mentioned at the beginning about other modes of transportation, e-bikes, etc.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I do want to say, and I want to respond to that, the council and pedicabs, which I know Council Member Brewer has also been very focused on.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Council is deeply committed to reforming the pedicab industry, dealing with the fact that so many operators are unlicensed or overcharging tourists.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're committed to focus on the e-bike legislation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We have a package of bills we're about to be introducing on that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I do just want to clarify that because we are very focused on those modes of modality as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I just end by saying, and I've been saying this from the beginning,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We are committed to ensuring your workers'livelihood.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The council, when we understand that right now the language in the bill talks about worker training, we know that there needs to be additional language negotiated.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so we look forward to sitting with you and your members and doing just that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro?

chapters.summary: Gennaro calls the hearing a kangaroo court, criticizes the Speaker's pre-announcement, and questions Dr. Cook about sanctuary capacity. Cook says sanctuaries are underfunded and calls the bill a death sentence for horses, estimating costs even higher than $15,000-$25,000 per horse per year.
chapters.title: Gennaro questions Dr. Cook on sanctuary capacity and death sentence claim

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I noticed you gave a little extra time to the witnesses.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I certainly appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: What I don't appreciate was that it took more than three hours to have a panel that speaks for the industry actually come and speak for the industry.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I want to talk to Charisse.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, you did mention a kangaroo court, and that's precisely what this hearing is.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It was pronounced in the New York Times.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: yesterday that the speaker had made a decision and here's what we're going to do with the workers and here's what we're going to do with the horses and we're going to do with this and then she spoke with all the advocates this morning and when she opened up the hearing today

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: it was just like this is what we're going to do and this is how we're going to take care of this part and this is how we're going to take care of that part so in her mind it's already done i will just mention that not even half of the members of the council are even

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: are even on this bill as sponsors.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so when you talk about the process, you are very right when, you know, members can sign on to whatever bills they want, but when a speaker comes out,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: and goes to the New York Times and makes a video on the same day and comes out the next day and talks about all the advocates, opens up the hearing and says that this is how, like this is happening, we just have to figure out like how we get it done.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: That is, I'm the longest serving active council member.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: This is not how the council is supposed to work.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I apologize for that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I have a minute and 20.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Left, I want to, you know, one of my major concerns, Dr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Cook, is about the fallacy that there are, I mean, I've been at a question for more than 60 years.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I've been riding horses longer than anybody in this room.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I know horses, saddle horses, for me, you know, you guys with the draft horses and the, you know, cold bloods and all that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But, you know, the whole notion, because I know as a horse guy that, you know, if you want that, the sanctuaries are really for horses that have been abused.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Or have been neglected and whatever.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: They're very, very underfunded.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And the animals in your industry wouldn't even go there because they need whatever meager slots they have for the horses that have really been abused.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So this whole notion that there are sanctuaries and entities that will pay between $15,000 and $25,000 a year per horse per year, this is fantasy.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'd like you to comment on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: The number might even be higher than that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Oh, you mean in terms of what it costs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Of what it costs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: The number's even higher than that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And I think everyone has goodwill here, and I think people do want to find new jobs for the drivers, and they want to find beautiful homes and pastures for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It is so multifactorial and complex.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: There are a lot of these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I work on fairly nice, I work on very nice Olympic quality horses in Fairfield and Westchester County.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Some of these horses, when they get turned out, don't even like being out in fields.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They like the security of the barn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So just to think you're going to put these horses out in the field and they're all going to be happy is also a fallacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It is one movement away for a horse, and then it will be lost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And there are horses now that cannot find homes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So the answer is it's not going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So just realize that you are providing a death sentence to these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It's not just these hundreds of horses that are in New York City now.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You used the word death sentence.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Did you not?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I think this is a death sentence.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I think this is a death sentence for not all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Some will get placed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: But it is a death sentence for a lot of these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And I think you need to think further than that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It's not just the number of horses that we have in the city now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: It's the next 150 horses that are in Amish country that are going to be repurposed as carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Because I'll tell you, in Amish country, if they don't get repurposed, they're being sold at auction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And they're getting euthanized or sent to slaughterhouses out of the country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So if you put it all together, it's more than just the present horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So the other panel, and I appreciate their passion for what they do, and we are never going to agree that horses shouldn't work, dogs shouldn't work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I watch horses play polo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They're playing the game.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: The horses are watching the ball.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: So I do believe there's a joy in the bond between people and animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: There's a joy that animals get from working bird dogs that are hunting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I see that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: My role in all that is to provide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Medical care for them so that when I

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: feel that they're not fit to do that, we can address that and we can find a solution to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: But if this ends, forget about these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Just forget, it's forget about the horses, they're gone.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, doctor.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: My time is up, but I look forward to someday possibly doing another successful reform bill like I did in 2010, which made a big difference.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have other questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Madam Chair, if I could answer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro's question really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Okay, about the kangaroo court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: So it was very disappointing that members signed on to the bill without meeting or talking to the workers, especially people in leadership positions, because I feel like you all have a...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: This is what you're supposed to do when you're in a leadership role.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: And I've been getting calls the whole time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: I've been here from council members saying that they're being pressured to sign on to the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: And I can't help but wonder where would we be if the people that came before us, the trailblazers that came before us,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: just got stopped in the line of opposition and threats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Like none of us will be where we are today if those people had fallen prey to the opposition that came before them when they fought hard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: So I'm hoping that you at least give us more time and you have a dialogue because there are things

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: that we all agree on, and we should start there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Sharice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Council Member Marte.

chapters.summary: Marte states for the record that his office reached out to TWU before introducing the bill but no meeting was scheduled. He questions whether drivers are W-2 or 1099 workers, who pays union dues, and how many dues-paying members exist. Hansen explains both classifications exist.
chapters.title: Marte questions TWU on worker classification and union membership

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I just want to state for the record that before we introduced this bill, my office reached out to the union, TWU, to set up a meeting.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And I also, in that email, said I'm willing to visit the stables and meet the horse owners.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And we never got an appointment set up.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And so, you know, we tried doing that olive branch to have the discussion before the introduction of the bill, but it was never accepted on the other side.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I just wanted to say that because I feel like some of the allegations could be thrown at us inappropriately.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I have a question for the union.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: For horse carriage drivers who do not own the business or medallion and are hired to drive for someone else, are they classified as W-2 employees or 1099 independent contractors?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And if they are employees, who are their employer?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: All right, I'll answer this as our shop steward and the representative of our members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We have both W-2 and 1099 employees and independent contractors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We are some of the last truly independent contractors because, you know, the individual carriage driver

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: makes an individual deal with the carriage owner over what percentage of the trip card the carriage driver keeps before they hand the remainder of the take for the day to the owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So there are drivers who will not work with certain owners because they don't like the percentage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are drivers who will not work with a carriage because they don't like the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are carriage drivers who, I mean, we've set when we go on vacation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We are two independent contractors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are some W-2 employees that work for carriage owners that own multiple carriages, so they're more of a fleet, more than just the single owner-operator or single owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And then we do have a lot of owner-operators in the business.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I have a follow-up question.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Who pays the dues to TW Local 100 in connection with the carriage trade?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Is it the higher carriage driver, medallion owners, or their owner-operators?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The drivers and the owners all pay union dues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The drivers, if you don't own a medallion, you don't pay as high a number.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: do structure every month, but we are all here together because you guys are management.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, you guys are the ones that set the terms of our employments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You set the terms of when we get to go to work under what conditions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You set how much we can charge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So you are management, and our union here is here to represent us in our negotiations with you and the New York City Administrative Code.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's our contract.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay, I have two quick questions.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: How many do paying carriage drivers does the union currently have?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And you mentioned earlier that the average salary is $65,000.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Is that through a guaranteed salary or is it an hourly wage negotiated through a contract?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
