utterances.text: Well, again, like I just told you, we negotiate with the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Carriage owners, you know, do I get 40% of the ride and keep the tips?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We always keep the tips.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The drivers always get the tips, whoever's doing the driver, the owner, or the driver.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Is it 50%?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Does the driver get to keep the first ride of the day?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: These are little small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And, you know, so it's not a wage job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, today, if we weren't...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: here, you know, we would have been suspended because of the heat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So it's not a salary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is not an hourly wage job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is a way of life with horses in small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And in this case, the workers own the means of production.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I own a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I work for somebody who owns a carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I run his small family business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He has horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He owns the medallion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are stable owners here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They own the stables, but they're also workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So we own the horses, we own the carriages, we own the medallions, we own the stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that's who we are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Madam Chair, can I answer?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Councilmember Marte.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Councilmember Marte, TWU, we've reached out to every council member to visit the stable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Some of them do get a chance to, and that offer still stands if you're ready to come by.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'll follow up on my email.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Also, I would like to note, I think there was a discussion about there being a delay in the introduction of the bill so that you could meet with us, but also our union is also engaged in contract negotiations with the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And our other members are working without a contract now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So there was a request to delay, but that bill was introduced on schedule to coincide with other legislation in Philadelphia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: May I say one thing?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cherise DeBose
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I'm sorry, may I say one thing?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Regarding the invitations, there have been a couple of council members that have visited.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Councilman Epstein did visit, and we met and spent, I think, probably close to an hour together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: You seemed really intrigued, really interested.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: You were focused.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I explained to you what I look for in a horse to make sure that they are healthy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Do you remember this, sir?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Whether they're healthy, whether they're alert.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: Voices standing in the back of the stall not facing you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: They're stressed when they're looking at you with their ears forward, when they're breathing normally, how we take heart rates.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: You listen to everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I showed you that there was manure and shavings on the stall during the time of day when we cleaned stalls.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I said, once you leave, this will all be swept.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: The stalls were clean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: There was fresh water.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: There was hay in every stall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: You listened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I think we agreed that despite, and you made it very clear your stance, and I appreciate that, and we can agree to disagree.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: But at the same time, I asked you to at least appreciate or understand what I was trying to get across to you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And you said you did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And then you crucified me the next day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: With your approach, you showed a picture of a healthy horse in a stall that is in jail.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I was very naive walking into that because I do believe that information is information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: And you failed me on that one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I think you failed the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: You did not represent us properly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Gabriel Cook
utterances.text: I'd be interested in your thoughts.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You're going to go next anyway.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I just wanted to make sure that it wasn't part of a condition that we were going to introduce the bill if you didn't meet with us.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: We reached out, I think, more than a month before our intended introduction, and it wasn't in line with what other cities have done.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: As you mentioned, Philadelphia has banned horse carriages from their city, and that just was a coincidence, and we're willing to follow their lead now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, I don't want to have, I don't want back and forth here because we'll be here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All day.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So you want to just take it outside of here, that's fine.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, you're next.

chapters.summary: Epstein responds to criticism from Dr. Cook about his stable visit, defends his right to disagree, and questions whether there's a collective bargaining agreement (none) and medical insurance (none beyond dental/optical). He expresses concern about the adversarial approach.
chapters.title: Epstein questions TWU on CBA and benefits, defends stable visit

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And yeah, I did visit and I raised concerns there.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And you say I crucified you because I disagreed with you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, we have a right to disagree about issues.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, I'd want to, you know, again, like I said, I made an offer to visit and I did and made a decision that I think is an important bill.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank Councilmember Marte for sponsoring it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I just want to reiterate with the speakers that we've talked a lot about e-bikes and we had a hearing on the last mile bill about the Amazon trucks on the street.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We already did a lot of things you're referencing to talk about these issues.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I think the council, in the short month, the six months that this speaker's been speaker, we've taken a lot of these issues on.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is a hearing about getting feedback from people, and I don't, I mean, it's important, you know, that we hear what your perspective is.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We're allowed to have perspectives based on the information we have, and, you know, I think the characterization, I feel deeply troubling the way you've characterized this as we're not willing to visit, we're crucifying you, we're not talking to the union.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I met with Sam Newsom myself, and I talked to him about this issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I think, you know, I really take issue with this, the way you all are talking about us.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to talk about the people who are working in the industry.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm understanding.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So there's no CBA in effect, right?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They have no CBA, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: Who are you talking to?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Anyone, whoever, you or the person who's your delegate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So, as I stated earlier, there is no CBA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That's why we're here speaking with you because, you know.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: No, there's CBA and you said there's no medical insurance, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: When you say medical insurance provided by Local 100?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: No, there's no medical insurance other than we have the ability to offer them dental and optical because that's what's under our purview.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And do they pay the dental and optical or does it come through their dues?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So I don't have that information in front of me and I don't know how many people are in the dental and optical plan and I quite honestly don't know why it would concern you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I just want to know the benefits that people have of being a member of the union.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They're paying dues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So the benefits of being a member of a union is what I'm doing right now and advocating for them.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: That's what unions are.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay, I appreciate that saying that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: If the crowd doesn't like it, I'm sorry, but this is what we do.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So my father, you know, actually, it is a respectful way.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: My father was...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I was just clapping.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, my father was a president of the union.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I was a shop steward when I was in the union.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I respect the unions.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm a chair of a committee that works on you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Were you a part of?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I was a legal aid lawyer and legal services lawyer, local 2320, UAW.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Did you have benefits?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I did have benefits, and I worked full-time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Did you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: You pay for them?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I pay through my dues.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Listen, wait.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to stop this right now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The panelists are not to ask questions of the council members.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I am telling you now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you do, you'll be asked to leave.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So let me just finish this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Let me just finish this so I can ask questions, actually.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Yes, yes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I want to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many of these members do you have in total?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: How many members do I have in total?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I represent 150.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many of those are full-time, work full-time versus part-time?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I do not have that information in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm just asking a question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: If I represented one of them, I would be here today.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I really view your approach to be deeply troubling and adversarial in a way.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You have your opinion.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm just asking you a question.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, you can just say whatever.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm allowed to speak.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You can answer the question any way you want.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm happy to talk to you after the fact.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But I'm just explaining that this, you know, oppositional adversarial approach is maybe not the ideal situation in a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So 150 members, you don't know how many are full or part-time.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And what are the dues that the people who are the members pay versus the owners of the stores?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: How much time is Councilmember Epstein going to get?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He's finishing up a question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So, again, as I stated earlier, I don't have the treasury report in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't see the relevance of how many people are paying or what they're paying or what they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So you may call it adversarial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't see the point in anything you're saying at all.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Chair, I really appreciate the time.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about workforce training provisions and notes this is the first hearing on the bill. Chiarello says he hasn't seen workforce development details and questions what jobs drivers would transition to.
chapters.title: Wong questions TWU on workforce training provisions

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council Member Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, I think it's a fair question that how many members are dues paying carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: So I'll answer it exactly as I answered it before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I do not have the treasurer's report in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We have 150 members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: Most of them are here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: If you'd like them to stand up, we could do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I do not see what the correlation is to this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't see what they're paying, what their health benefit is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: We are looking to preserve their jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: That's what unions do.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But there is workforceman training on this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I haven't seen any of it, so I can't speak on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angel
utterances.text: But go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But there is workforce training for these horse-drawn carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: As I stated earlier, these men and women are in the horse carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I don't know what type of industry is in the workplace development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: I haven't been a part of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: But I don't think you're going to have anything close.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Chiarello
utterances.text: A hotel host or...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Casino worker is not going to be a job that they're adapt to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So unless you have something that I don't know about, feel free because I don't know what it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There's been no discussion with Local 100 about what that particular job is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And workforce development is a lovely word, but I don't know what it is.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, my predecessor, Robert Holden, when he introduced the bill, he asked for hearings.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: He'd been asking for these hearings.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong asked a question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, the question is, I was involved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Bob Holden likes to put TW in his mouth for some reason, and I don't know what his interests are, but...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He's, you know, the past council, but if you have a question, I'll answer it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: There was never a hearing about this until today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I remember in September I came to a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Well, all right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So move on.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Relevance.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ask your questions, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, well.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I've been involved with the city council for several years, and this is the first hearing that I know of in this room regarding this bill.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So, and I ask myself, how come there was never a hearing on this?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Either the previous speaker never wanted a hearing, or there are people out there that just don't even want to discuss this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I would ask Bob Holden.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council Member, we're not arguing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Listen, ask them a question that relates to the union.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Do not ask them about the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have no further questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Hanif.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks drivers about the process when a horse is sick or injured and who decides if it continues working. Hansen says drivers know their horses and call Dr. Cook. Dr. Cook describes the protocol but acknowledges no standardized process exists.
chapters.title: Hanif questions drivers and Dr. Cook on horse health protocols

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'd love to address these questions to the workers.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Could you describe what process you follow when a horse is sick or your horse is sick, injured, showing signs of dehydration, and then who decides whether the horse continues to work or stop?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So, like I said, I've been a commercial carriage driver for 20 years.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Just describe the process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So the process is, I'm with my horse every day, okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I know what they are normally like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If there's anything wrong, I stop what I'm doing and I call Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Cook.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So essentially it's up to the driver.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I mean, of course it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We know our horse best.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Obviously, if somebody said something to me, one of my coworkers, the stable man who noticed something different overnight, you know, if it was somebody on the street who said, hey, what's going on with this?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But aside from...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The driver knowing their horse, there isn't a set standardized process.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But I want to continue asking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I want to answer your question about dehydration.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Could you respond to my next question, please?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: What policies are in place to ensure a horse is removed from service when there are concerns about its health?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And I know Dr.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Cook was mentioned here, if you want to chime in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Yes, so horses are creatures of habit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: They do things all the time, meaning if they normally pass five piles of manure overnight, there's usually five piles of manure every morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: If they're depressed, you can see it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Their heads are low.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Their ears are back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: They can look not okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: And that's where my telehealth comes in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: That's a signal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Pardon me?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: That's a signal.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Those are definitely all signals.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: For them to not work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: For life-threatening problems with colic or abdominal pain being the main one, they are very vocal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: They are pawing at the ground.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: They're violent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: They're lying down, standing up, walking in circles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: It's obvious.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: So in those moments, I'm usually receiving a telephone call.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: We have a protocol in place because I am not there at all every day.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Which is?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: So we have, there are medicines that I have directed them to give in that event.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: My rule, and this has been implemented, and the vast majority of people have followed it, that if they do not respond to that medicine, the horse is to be trailered to my hospital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Because it's silly for me to drive all the way down and then say we have a problem, and then have the horse, so the horses come to my hospital, and we evaluate.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Have there been times when a horse...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Was sick, was depressed, having physical, physiological issues, and continued to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: So not to my knowledge, but I think, but I, but for a hundred, for how many horses do we have for a hundred, whatever that is, it has probably happened, but it can't continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Meaning that they may have started to work like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Maybe not to my knowledge, but if they did, they couldn't sustain a day and be, and, and be that sick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: So ultimately, it comes to our knowledge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Now, I will say this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Let's take a bad apple moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Let's say for a second, and this hasn't happened in the last year or two, but early on, again, I've been there four years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: In the event that I recognize there's a problem, and this has barely happened, if the person says, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Cook, you don't know what you're doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: I know my horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: The horse is fine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: I'm going to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: And I disagree with that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: My only authority, and I have used it a few times, is to call the Department of Health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: And I consider the Department of Health, I believe this is a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: It's up to the Department of Health at that point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: And in those events, they have suspended the horse until we can evaluate the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: I hope that answers your question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, that's it.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Could I just say one comment?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Just one comment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One comment.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But your responses still demonstrate that there isn't a standardized process and having to call you and rely on the owner or call Department of Health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: So what would be a standardized process if my 10-year-old son...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Had a knee ache or had a belly ache or had a pin the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: CITES.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: The standard process is you have to call for help, recognize there's a problem, and then process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Cook
utterances.text: Well, schools have our systems.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'll move on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Let's not do the back and forth here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Brewer.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks what circumstances would help members if the ban happens. McHugh says they're not interested in selling their land and want to continue providing carriage rides, disputing the real estate narrative.
chapters.title: Brewer questions carriage owners on transition circumstances

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do believe, I've been to the stables years ago, and I do believe that the people who are with their horse do care about their horses.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Let me be clear on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The issue for me is this, I believe, is going to stop this industry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know, I'm just being realistic here.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question to the union or anybody else is, earlier we heard from one of the speakers that there is some interest from the owners of getting out of the business.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was surprising to me.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question to you is, what circumstances, even though I know you are not interested in getting out of the business, what circumstances would you feel could be of assistance to your members if in fact this happens?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know it's a hard question, but that is my question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A couple of occasions you have referenced there's big real estate here, and this is something that really rubs me and my other owners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Myself and 14 other people own the stable on 52nd Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: All of us are carriage medallion owners, and the other two stables are owned by people who own carriage medallions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we bought our land because we wanted it and we needed it and we didn't buy it to sell it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that is rubbing a lot of people the wrong way nowadays, especially since the whole rezoning took place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And our...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: is not to give up our land, ever.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that's, I don't know how many times I've heard about big real estate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We're not interested in big real estate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We are interested in providing carriage rides in Central Park, and we're not interested in being fobbed off with some stories, because the day you sell your land is the day you...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Are finished.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I hear that, but there was a family apparently that is interested that we heard earlier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The family that's interested sold their stable 10 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it was 10 years ago that they sold it?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Wilson.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks about procedures when horses are left for photographs and how often horses spook. Hansen says the law requires horses to be attended or tethered, spooking is very rare, and the cause of Romanch's incident is still under investigation.
chapters.title: Wilson questions drivers on spooking and unattended horse procedures

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: I have a question about procedures while in the park.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: We heard from testimony earlier, and I guess this is for the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: We heard testimony earlier that it is very common for drivers to leave the carriage for, I think it was said, for photographs, that that's advertised, that there's sections in the park where they're often left.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: I'm just curious your response to that.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And if that is, if it is routine in perhaps a way that, you know, to step away in a practical way of managing the carriages and the business in the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I've worked as a commercial carriage driver for 20 years, and there's different rules and regulations in different cities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So here in New York, the law is that the horse must be attended or tethered at all times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So when we can, we tie our horses to the lampposts and infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: attended, you know, we have people that are right there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When this accident took place, my understanding from the people that are investigating it still, it is still an ongoing investigation into this accident as to what spooked the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: My understanding is that the driver, as he was getting on the carriage, had the lines in his hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When I worked in Philadelphia, the law was not attended or tethered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It was that you could not ever tether and that you had to maintain control of your horse's head, which was actually defined.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So, you know, we've been a very, very safe industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You can look at our guys doing this business back for decades and decades and decades when we're out on Central Park South.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And basically, one of the things that the health department did not mention in their discussion of our training process is that we have an apprenticeship.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We learn from other carriage drivers like how it's done and that is what you know This is how our business has been you know safe and handed down from generations to generations to generations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Given that we don't know what spooked the horse and given that there is like a completely

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: new thing in Central Park with all these electric vehicles whining and screaming and going on at high rates of speed, you know, we have had to then change what we do because the park is different.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And so, you know, I have been out there every single day literally reminding people to hold their horses and to change the way that they are used to attending their horses and being nearby.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Adita was up here testifying earlier about unattended horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: She has come by taking pictures, and a lot of times she's taking pictures of her horses, and the driver steps out of frame because, A, she's trying to take a picture of the horse, not of us, and, B, people don't want to be in her social media.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: How often is a horse spooked?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Very, very rarely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, horses are creatures of habit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They don't spook because they're prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So, you know, a horse's main concern in life

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Life is not to be eaten by a mountain lion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And, you know, you can't spook at everything that moves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I mean, spooking is a defense fact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It doesn't run away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So it happens very rarely because our horses get used to what they experience in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And the taxis and the buses and the construction equipment, all that might as well be rocks and trees and shrubs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's just a part of their environment so they can be on the lookout of mountain lions.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: I guess what I'm struggling with is, like, we don't know what...

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Spooks them.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: No matter what, you know, what is the step we can take to stop that?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council member, you have to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: So that's kind of what I'm, you know, we have a sentient animal that we don't believe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We do, and because they're creatures of habit, as Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Cook said, and we are with them all the time, we know them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Like, my horse Sherman doesn't like off-leash dogs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Particularly medium-sized light-colored ones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Like, he notices them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He was obviously chased before I bought him when he was on some Amish farm up in the Finger Lakes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He doesn't like little wheelie things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, some horses don't like garbage trucks, and some walk right past them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's not that we don't know, but in this particular instance, in this particular accident, we have no idea.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But that's what's unusual about it because as horse people, we can't, you know, this one is a stumper for us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So that's why it's still under investigation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And, you know, so, I mean, we are horse people all the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Ask the mounted cops what spooks their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You know, they know their horses just as well as we know ours.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Madam Chair, I think I have something to add to Carl Wilson's question, and I think we'll...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, listen, you had your time, and...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: No, I just think it would be a help...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm just telling you, this panel's dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm just trying to answer his question.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: All right, I'll answer it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can answer it with him.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He's right next to you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you all very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: For the next panel, we're going to do a hybrid.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: There's a doctor who was supposed to be on earlier.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We had some technical issues, so we're going to see if he's still available.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Craig.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Shh.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Stuff.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Craig Kulikowski.

chapters.summary: Dr. Craig Kulikowski, an equine veterinarian with 30 years of experience, testifies remotely about his 2025 DOHMH-commissioned examinations where owners refused blood testing and lameness evaluations, and found old scars, insufficient dental care, and lameness visible at a walk.
chapters.title: Dr. Craig Kulikowski testifies on independent vet exam findings

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Yes, can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, oh, amazing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, so why don't we just, why don't you go ahead and testify and then we'll call the other parts of the panel after, okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: I would like to thank the City Council for considering the welfare and safety of New York City's carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Craig Kulikowski.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: I'm a New Yorker, an equine veterinarian with almost 30 years of experience, and I have been a horseman for more than 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Before I was a veterinarian, I competed internationally as an equestrian in several countries and on multiple continents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: I believe that horses, like humans, can work for a living.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: But when humans employ horses for entertainment or profit, we assume a serious responsibility to protect their health and welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: In many equine industries, including racing and competitive sport, working horses undergo consistent, thorough, and independent veterinary examinations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: These evaluations include observing the horse at the trot to identify lameness, along with blood testing for painkillers, sedatives, stimulants, and other medications that could mask an underlying disease or lameness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: When I was commissioned by the Department of Health to conduct veterinary examinations of 16 carriage horses last year,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: The owners did not comply with two critical components of typical examinations, blood testing and the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: lameness evaluation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: In fact, two owners did not even show up to present their horses for examination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: In spite of the refusal to comply fully, the truncated exams that I did conduct revealed some troubling concerns for me, including widespread old scars on horses'backs from ill-fitting tack and gear, insufficient dental care,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: and lameness that could be visible even at the walk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: While these clinical findings gave me concerns, it was the lack of natural access to pasture that was my biggest concern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: A horse that has consistent access to natural pasture is not only going to be a healthier horse,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: but most likely less apt to frantically grab for unnatural toxic forage in Central Park, which may have contributed to at least one recent horse death in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Not having access to consistent pasture also means these horses spend the majority of their days indoors inside horse stalls.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Please bear in mind that a typical 12-foot...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you please summarize?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can always submit testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have another 10 hours of testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I just want people to know, based on who's signed up, so we have to keep it short.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Yep, I'll be done in just a second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Please bear in mind that a typical 12-foot by 12-foot horse stall for a 1,000-pound horse would be similar to a 200-pound human living in a 5-foot by 6-foot closet for the majority of the day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: It is not hard to imagine how this type of daily confinement might be used.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: might lead to unpredictable and extremely dangerous actions by a thousand pound animal when it finally gets outside of its confinement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: The concern is not only that one can evaluate the horse's welfare without full cooperation from owners, but how do we provide adequate relief from prolonged confinement and the dangers associated with cooped up and pent up horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Craig Kulikowski
utterances.text: Thank you for your consideration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, now we're going to do the in-person panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Roger Mosika, I think it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Huh?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: David Saltonstall, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I probably butchered that a little.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Natalie Juras.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Folks here?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, no?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Laura Spielman, Curtis Sliwa, and Danielle Chin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, I just want to reiterate to everyone, you have two minutes, because I'm going to start cutting people off and cutting the mics off, because we have a lot of testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You could submit testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It all gets looked at to testimony at council.nyc.gov, and all of it is going to be looked at and taken into account.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So when the buzzer goes off, just summarize, and then we'll go on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, who's going first?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

chapters.summary: David Saltonstall of the Central Park Conservancy testifies in support of the ban, citing eight incidents in 13 months, 68% public support, and arguing horse carriages are incompatible with a modern crowded park.
chapters.title: David Saltonstall (Central Park Conservancy) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: My name is David Saltonstall, and I'm Vice President for Government Relations and Policy at the Central Park Conservancy, the nonprofit that manages the park on behalf of the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: And I'm joined by Roger Mosier, the Conservancy's Chief of Park Operations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: The Conservancy supports Romantia's law, and we urge the Council to pass it without delay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: We do not take this position lightly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Over the past 13 months, Central Park has seen eight separate horse-related incidents, runaway carriages, collisions, injured drivers, and a horse that collapsed and died in front of stunned park goers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Last month, that pattern ended in tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: India was killed when a horse bolted near Cherry Hill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: It was a devastating loss and our hearts go out to this family who have spoken so eloquently here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Public safety is the Conservancy's paramount concern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages are simply incompatible with a modern crowded park used by millions of visitors a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Again, this has become painfully clear over the last 13 months, during which there have been eight frightening incidents, injuring and sadly causing the death of Ramach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Park is not built for the volume of people we welcome every day to share safely with half-ton animals whose natural instinct is to bolt when spooked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: No amount of regulation and no single piece of hardware can correct for an animal's basic instincts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: The public agrees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: A poll the Conservancy Commission two weeks ago found that 68% of New Yorkers support ending city licensing of horse-drawn carriages in the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Support that holds across every borough, every demographic, and every political affiliation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Keeping New Yorkers safe does not and must not come at the expense of those affected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: We strongly support the provision of real jobs and workforce development services for the drivers and stable workers who will be displaced, as well as humane homes for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: We would not be the first to do so.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: London, Paris, Salt Lake City, Philadelphia already have shown how

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: had to end this practice responsibly, and it's time for New York City to join the rest of the world.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Dr. Natalie Juras, an equine veterinarian, testifies that horses' innate flight response cannot be eliminated through training, and that NYC's dense traffic and noise create inherently challenging conditions for horses.
chapters.title: Dr. Natalie Juras (equine vet) testifies on biological risks

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Natalie Juras and I'm an equine veterinarian.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: My role is to evaluate equine health and soundness, diagnose disease and injury, and provide recommendations that prioritize both equine welfare and public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: I appreciate the opportunity to offer a veterinary perspective on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: One of the most fundamental principles of equine medicine is that horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Even healthy, highly trained horses retain an innate flight response, a biological survival mechanism that cannot be eliminated through training, experience, or routine veterinary care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: As veterinarians, we can determine whether a horse is medically fit for work at the time of an examination, but we cannot predict or guarantee that a horse will respond to an unexpected stimulus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: In a complex and rapidly changing environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: As veterinarians, we also recognize that animal welfare extends beyond the absence of disease or lameness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Welfare includes an animal's physical health, behavioral needs, and its ability to cope with the environment in which it lives and works in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: A veterinary examination is a snapshot in time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: It tells us whether a horse appears clinically fit on the data as evaluated, but it cannot predict every future medical condition or behavioral response.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: In my professional opinion, New York City's dense traffic, continuous noise, construction, emergency vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, and other unpredictable environmental stimuli create an inherently challenging

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: and stressful situation for horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Appropriate veterinary care, thoughtful training, and responsible management are all essential for protecting horse welfare, but they cannot eliminate

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: the biological realities of the equine species or the inherent risks associated with asking prey animals to work in one of the world's most complex urban environments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Veterinary medicine can help manage these risks, but it cannot eliminate them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Natalie Juras
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and consideration.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next, want to go, Curtis?

chapters.summary: Curtis Sliwa testifies as an animal welfare activist, comparing the ban to other cities worldwide, criticizing TWU's influence, and calling for electric carriages as replacements.
chapters.title: Curtis Sliwa testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Curtis Sliwa, animal welfare activist, ran for mayor on the first ever animal welfare line.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Animals are people, and it is the responsibility of you elected officials to protect the rights of all animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: This family that came from India to celebrate the graduation of their 18-year-old son, if they had gone to Bombay, now called Mumbai,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: They would no longer have been able to take a horse-drawn carriage that predated back to Queen Victoria because the Bombay High Court ruled it to be a barbaric act.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: In city after city around the world, first world, third world cities,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: We should be ashamed of ourselves that they have led in banning this barbaric behavior and most times replacing them with electric carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: that the horse drivers, the carriage drivers, can continue to have an income.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: We're not even considering that here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: We're saying, oh, we need to keep the tradition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Let me tell you about the tradition at the Bronx Zoo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: They always had elephants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Remember Happy the Elephant wasn't all that happy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Happy died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: And the Bronx Zoo decided to send Patty, the remaining elephant, to an elephant sanctuary and will have elephants no more, even though they will lose money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Because they recognized finally, after many of us animal welfare activists pounded their door, that is enough is enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: We know why this continues to be a problem here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: It's the result of the former president of TWU Local 100, the bully John Samuelson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Who has threatened to sue anyone who stands in his way, the state senator who spoke here, former Mayor Eric Adams.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Did you know of any issue that ever brought mayoral candidates together as in this past election?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: Eric Adams, Andrew Cuomo, Zohan Mondami, Curtis Sliwa, your speaker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Curtis Sliwa
utterances.text: So if you're going to take the money from TWU Local 100, the blood money, you are not humanitarians.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: People will be escorted out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Stop.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If people are going to act out, we're going to have people removed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm telling you now.

chapters.summary: Danielle, a NYC resident, testifies about witnessing two horse incidents firsthand—a spooked horse nearly trampling her and the collapse and death of Denise—and says you cannot regulate instinct.
chapters.title: Danielle testifies about witnessing horse incidents firsthand

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: It's a very tough act to follow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: Danielle, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: You have two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: Council members, thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And to the Mahajan family, I'm so sorry for your loss.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I am also so sorry for all of those who claim to mourn your loss, but do not even take the time to pronounce your name correctly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: My name is Danielle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I'm a native New Yorker and a longtime Hell's Kitchen and Upper West Side resident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: When previously living on 57th and 10th, I'd watch those horses make the trek to Central Park, have them be spooked by cars honking, bikes cutting them off, and overall just not in a comfortable state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: When now living near the Museum of Natural History, every day I walk my two dogs in the park daily, multiple times a day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: In the last 13 months, there have been eight serious incidents, and I have been firsthand witness to two of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: On September 1st, the horse Samson was spooked by loud sounds, and myself and my dogs were part of the pedestrians that needed to jump out of the way to not get trampled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: Had my instinct not been quick enough, I would have been trampled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: My heart is dropping just thinking about that experience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And I soon watched as Samson the horse then smashed into a stop sign.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: On June 9th, I was walking with my dogs and saw the horse Denise collapse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And while there is something awful about witnessing an animal dying, there is nothing more horrible than watching two of my animals react to another animal dying in front of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I am here today because in my interview with various media outlets, including CBS, ABC, on September 1st, and in my interview on June 9th, I quoted that it was only a matter of time until that stop sign was a human or a child.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I invite you to look up that footage and see that footage of me saying that with fear in my eyes of what might happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And on June 16th, I was leaving the park when friends had texted me about the incident and the critical condition of Ramange.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I am also here because as a dog owner, if my animal is hurt, my instinct is to react.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And in both of those incidents, I know I'm short on time, but I think it's important to express that my instinct as an animal owner and someone who genuinely cares for animals is to react to them in danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: While I am not speaking to all horse carriage drivers, I am speaking to those two in front of me that I saw.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I saw them have complete apathy for the situation, and then as soon as the news cameras came on, have staged performance of care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, if you could wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: I will wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: While those in opposition might say it was one off in decades of Romange's tragedy and the industry just needs to be regulated, my perspective is that you cannot regulate instinct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: It was instinct for Romange to protect his mother.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: It was instinct for horses to be spooked, to be bolted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: And if this legislation is not passed, it is only a matter of time that another human will lose its life.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Danielle
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Laura Spielman, founder of the Coalition for Responsible Carriage Horse Retirement and certified equine specialist, testifies about horse biology making them unsuitable for city work and her experience successfully placing 160 horses in rescue operations.
chapters.title: Laura Spielman (equine specialist) testifies on horse biology and rescue experience

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Firstly, to the Mahajan family, I'm so very sorry for your loss.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: My name is Laura Spielman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I'm a founder of the Coalition for Responsible Carriage Horse Retirement, and I'm here to ask you to vote yes on Romacha's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I'm here as a New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I was born and raised here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Both sides of my family have lived here since the 1800s.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: My great-grandfather served as mounted PD in the park in the 20s.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Mounted police serve a vital public function.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages do not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I'm a certified equine specialist for two international organizations working with humans and horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I serve on both the Humane Society and ASPCA's large animal response teams.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I built and currently run an equine program for the Department of Defense to work with the Navy, the Marines, and Wounded Warriors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I work with the VA, the prison system, law enforcement, children with developmental disabilities, and the general public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: In the last five years, I've worked with over 200 people and 20 or so horses in my program, and I hold a perfect safety record because I put safety first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: That means the safety of humans and the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I know that the retirement plan is a central concern here, and it can be done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I've done it before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I've worked on a large-scale rescue effort involving over 160 horses, everyone evaluated, treated, and placed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: I know from direct experience that a coordinated retirement of this very size can be done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: And here's the science why they should be retired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: A horse sees nearly 340 degrees around its body, but it has blind spots directly in front and behind.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: So traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Agreed is an unseen threat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Its eyes adjust to light far slower than ours, so a shift from sun to shadow or a camera flash causes real disorientation, not disobedience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: Their hearing can trigger a full flight response from a siren or a backfire in under a second, faster than any driver can react.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: And small dogs and little wheeled things are allowed in the park, and they always will be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: This is not a training failure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: More training will not fix it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: It's biology doing what millions of years of evolution designed it to do in an environment that will never stop asking it to do the opposite.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Spielman
utterances.text: This is why accidents will not stop.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Schulman questions the Conservancy on costs related to horse carriage wear and tear (~$500,000 for Cherry Hill asphalt), sanitation (none spent on horse waste), and whether banning carriages would affect visitor numbers (no).
chapters.title: Schulman questions Conservancy on carriage-related costs

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions for the Conservancy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How much money annually does the Conservancy spend on repairs and maintenance directly related to wear and tear caused

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: by horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Hi, thanks for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Roger Mosier, Chief of Park Operations for the Conservancy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: There's not an annual figure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: It really depends on the projects that we might pursue from time to time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: We find that the surfaces that the horses walk on are extremely deteriorated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: For example, Cherry Hill has a loop around the fountain there, and we need to replace that asphalt due to horse damage, and that project is probably $500,000.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How much money does the Conservancy spend on sanitation related to horse waste?
