utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: We don't spend money on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: We don't attempt to clean up after the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: We do have a little bit of inefficiency related to working around the horses on our own trash removal, but we don't clean up after the horses.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Has the Conservancy seen an increase in the number of people visiting the park?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: You know, it's very hard to know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Our general estimate is that we get 42 million visitors a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: I think we saw a dramatic increase in general after cars were banned from the park in 2019.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: And also during the pandemic, many people came to the park because it was the only place to go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: And I think they have continued those patterns since that time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: So we really have seen a dramatic increase in the last five or six years.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Do you think the banning of horse carriages would affect the number of visitors?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: I do not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: I know that there are a certain percentage of tourists who do avail themselves of horse carriages, but I don't think those 42 million people are coming to the park just to take a horse carriage ride.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Those are my questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to ask Councilmember Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He has some questions for you.

chapters.summary: Gennaro questions the Conservancy's Roger Mosier about planting toxic Japanese yew plants throughout Central Park. Mosier says yews are original to the park and ubiquitous. Gennaro criticizes both Conservancy witnesses for lacking horse knowledge.
chapters.title: Gennaro questions Conservancy on toxic yew plants

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: This is for David Saltonstall.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: The person to David's left, what's your name, sir?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Roger Mosier.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Okay, and so what's your title?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Chief of Park Operations for the Consumer.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Chief of Park Operations.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So it was you that authorized the planting of the Japanese yews all over the park?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Is that you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: The yews.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: The yews are a ubiquitous ornamental shrub in North America.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: But it was planted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Northeast, yes, it was planted.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Right, but they are toxic to...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Every animal that is native to the park or any animal that goes through the park or migrates through it or somebody walking the dark, you understand this, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: There are a number of plants in any park that have some level of toxicity.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yeah, but you went out of your way to plant these for aesthetics, knowing that animals could die.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: The U is essentially original to the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: We have planning plans from the 1800s that show that it was present.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: been regularly planted throughout the 20th century and at a time when horses were much more ubiquitous in the park and everyone has lived together for that period of time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: And as far as toxicity- But you have no idea how many animals have been killed by them.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Like, you don't know anything.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You don't know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: The, the,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: All right, enough for you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Enough for you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm done.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm going to go to David now.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so you talked about, you know, animal habits and, you know, horse habits and features and, you know, how they react.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Do you know anything about horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: I am not a veterinarian.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: That's not why I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: I think you've heard abundant testimony from experts today that horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: But you talked about bolting.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, you talked about bolting.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so do you know what kind of horses the industry uses?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Like, do you know the difference between like a hot blood and cold blood?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Do you know the difference?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: I am not a veterinarian.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: I've seen the results.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Saltonstall
utterances.text: Well, let me tell you something, okay?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It's just like the industry uses what we call draft horses or cold bloods.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And these horses are bred to pull a plow all day long.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And the amount of work that they have to do in order to pull a carriage is nothing compared to what they have to do if they were plowing a field.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But the industry uses these horses because they are much less likely to spook because that is their temperament.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: A saddle horse is a hot blood.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: They'll spook for anything in between, like a...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: combination like would be a warm blood, which is also could be a saddle horse and could pull a little wagon.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Warm blood would be fine, you know, for the work that they have to do, but they prefer to use the cold blood because it's a much more stable temperament.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so for you to be commenting on something that you know nothing about, not to mention the fact that, you know, you've got hundreds and hundreds of

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: unlicensed, illegal pedicabs, and people have died in pedicabs, but now you're coming down on the horse carriage.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I think it's disgraceful, both of you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, that's it, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Stop, no clapping.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We've said this before.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, we're done with the panel, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Alexis Lawson.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Pete Donahue, Alexander Kemp,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: G.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: David Koch, Mindy Pless, and Joshua Sarsfield.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, before this panel starts, I wanna stress to everyone, there needs to be decorum here, because I'm telling you, and I've said it once, I'm gonna have people removed, and I will do it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So just, you know, it's a very fraught emotional issue.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're trying to hear everybody out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But people have to have decorum here, all right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So if you want to stay in the room and listen to everything that's going on, please do that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's going first here?

chapters.summary: Alexander Kemp, Administrative VP of TWU Local 100, testifies that the tragedy is being weaponized to eliminate an industry, compares it to other transportation safety issues, and argues one incident shouldn't end a 160-year industry.
chapters.title: Alexander Kemp (TWU) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Alexander Kemp.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I am the administrative vice president for TW Local 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I appreciate you guys taking the time to hear the concerns of the local and the union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: My condolences go out to the Mahajan family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I just want to state for the record to the Mahajan family that I am a father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I have an 11-year-old daughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I have a 17-year-old son.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: And one of my biggest fears in life is ever finding out that my son might not be coming home or my daughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: So for the sake of my condolences and...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: and a heartfelt condolences to the family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I am very, very sorry that this has become your reality and that this tragic event, you will spend the rest of your life reliving.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I also want to say for the record that an equal tragedy is that this is being used to weaponize

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: removing the property of business owners who have gave everything they have to try to live and act out the American dream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: And because of a tragedy, people who are bloodthirsty for this industry have exploited it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: And absolutely used disgusting experience to make this their framework to eliminate an industry that has been represented for over 160 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: The truth is that this is one of the safest mechanisms of tourism on the planet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: The truth is that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Although, and unfortunately, we have lost a life, we do not take a consequence and throw the baby out with the bathwater.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: We look for ways to make it safer so that it never happens again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: We can cite the triangle fires and how it modified a whole industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: We can speak to the fact that when planes crash, we look for ways to make it safer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: We in transportation, in buses, in trains, we have 400 to 500 people, members, employees of the transit authority getting assaulted every year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I just heard a stat from 1980 up until now has been 98 instances with the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Truth is, everyday transit workers are getting annihilated, and I don't see any caucus about the safety of the people that you guys are tasked with protecting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: So I would just demand that prior to you making a decision on this industry, you look at all of the responsibilities that you have to hold up your end of the bargain, and then collectively we can figure out what the future looks like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: But nobody should be making decisions over one experience that is a tragedy that I believe we can ultimately learn from and make the industry safer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please keep it to two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Press the button.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: My honor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I cleared with the staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm going to read a statement from International President John Samuelson, and then I'm going to give my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That was approved by the council staff, just so you know.

chapters.summary: A TWU member reads a statement from International President John Samuelson calling the hearing political, criticizing Councilmember Marte's comments, and arguing the bill strips workers of jobs with no real compensation. The member then gives his own testimony opposing the ban.
chapters.title: John Samuelson's statement read, followed by TWU member testimony opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is from the testimony of the council, TW International President John Samuelson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This hearing is based on politics, not on the pursuit of animal welfare or public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Let's start with the prime sponsor, Councilman Marte.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm ashamed he left here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: At a hearing on rider's law last year, Marte said this about horses giving carriage rides.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If it was an individual, if it was a human pushing a horse carriage, we would call that inhumane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If it was any other living animal, whether it's a dog or a cat pushing a carriage, we would call that inhumane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Why do we have to put horses at a different level?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is one of the most unintelligent comments ever uttered by a city council member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A 1,600-pound horse is not comparable, councilman, to a chihuahua, a cocker spaniel, or a Siamese horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But that is in the fact the mindset of the animal rights fundamentalists that this council speaker and council are following.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Speaker Menon knows this is not an issue of safety in the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If safety in the park was a real concern, she would hold a hearing on a bill, which 20 council members signed onto when it was introduced, that would prohibit e-bikes and motorized scooters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Nearly 70 people, including pedestrians, were injured in Central Park in accidents involving bicyclists, e-bike riders, and e-scooters in the last five years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is absolutely a political issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Speaker Minin and the council members here want to end the noise from the animal rights fundamentalists and the real estate backers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The constant phone calls, the emails, the social media posts, and the threats posed to council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Death threats to council members who opposed the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Ironically, they are wrong when they think that most New Yorkers don't want to get rid of the horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are polls that show strong support for protecting workers, these immigrant workforces, and the horses.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You need to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'll wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please, because you can submit that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Yeah, I will wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm on the last two sentences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This bill strips blue-collar workers of their jobs and future income.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It offers nothing in return, just a promise of workforce development and the words from the speaker about potential future jobs, with no mention of compensation to medallion owners who paid, in some cases, hundreds of thousands of dollars for the right in the surgery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: NAFTA has come to Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: At the best, this bill is half-baked, exposing workers and, ironically, horses to harm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No, I am next because I'm the second.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Oh, you're next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay, I'm sorry, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Just seven months ago, this same committee, the health committee, rejected this exact same bill, then called Ryder's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You rejected the claims that the carriage horses are being mistreated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You rejected claims that the horses aren't receiving proper care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Can I wait till I get attention?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You rejected claims that the horses aren't receiving proper care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You rejected claims that the horses don't have suitable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You rejected claims that it is cruel somehow for a 1600 pound draft horse to propel a carriage horse with giant wheels.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You have voted against advancing this very same bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So what changed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: For the first time in 167 years of carriage rides being a popular feature of Central Park, a passenger fatality occurred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One fatality in 167 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Carriage rides are the most safe mode of travel in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The fatal accident involving romance was an absolute tragedy, no question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Was it more tragic than the death of bicyclist Salvador Nico Garcia, who was killed in a bicycle accident in the park last year?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Was it more tragic than the death of bicyclist Charles Cheeseborough, who was killed in a bicycle accident in 2019?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Is it more tragic than the death of 28 people killed in accidents involving two-wheeled motor vehicles?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: vehicles in New York City in the first six months of this year?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Are you considering banning walking, biking, scooters, taxi cabs, buses, or the subway?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Of course not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Because that defies all logic and common sense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So does banning carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The city can address any safety concerns it has by doing what it's supposed to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Govern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Regulate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The lawyer for the Romance family earlier said, it's not the workers, it's not the union, it's the city that did zero enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: One of the animal rights activists said, the city has done zero enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And the only thing that this council can come up with doing is banning?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That is not government, not the government that anybody in this city wants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The city can address safety concerns by holding a hearing on Jim Gennaro's bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: In all fairness to the immigrant drivers, carriage owners, and the horses themselves, the city should shelve this ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Govern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Don't cancel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That's what voters want, not a knee-jerk reaction to an unprecedented tragedy, and there are polls to back that up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is the mic on?

chapters.summary: Joshua Sarsfield, a carriage driver, testifies that animal rights advocates' goal is extinction of all domestic animals, criticizes billionaire backers of the ban, and argues horses love pulling and get the best life of any horse in America.
chapters.title: Joshua Sarsfield (carriage driver) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: I'm Joshua Sosville, horse carriage driver.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: If the public has been actively deceived, we as the industry involved and our legislators have a responsibility and a duty to expose that deception.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: We are here because NY Class are animal liberation extremists whose eventual goal is the extinction of not just all working but all domestic animals, period.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Their movement defines captivity as abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: So no change we make could ever be enough because domestic animals are an abomination to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: We are here because Steve Nislick is a billionaire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: You might say, well, that proves his heart's true because what does he need the money for?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: But billionaires don't care about money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: If it was about needing money, they'd probably stop at $100 million and retire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: You've got to have a certain personality to go all the way to the billion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: How can you not get to $100 million and say, okay, now I can finally write that play I never had time for?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: But they keep going because it's not about money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Because if you're a billionaire, dollars aren't money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Dollars are points.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Points in a game.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: And a game is only enjoyable when you're on the field playing it, crushing your opponent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: The problem is your opponent is us, and it's a game we never signed up for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: And your winning point is our lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Your winning point is our children.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Nislik thinks he can control these people, but they'll come for his.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Horse two, if not now, sooner or later, this generation or the next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: The mounted unit, the park rangers, the service dogs, the bodega cats, they could be finding horses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: that need their help, but they focus all their attention on the 68 best kept and most visible horses in the country because the visibility is the point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: A symbolic victory in their long march toward extinction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: These horses get the perfect ratio of exercise, rest, and human attention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: They're getting the best life of any horse in America.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Everybody loves doing what they're born for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Birds love flying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Horses love pulling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: I love running my mouth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: There was a dog escaped in the news.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: They found him herding sheep for three days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: Never seen a sheep in his life, all right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joshua Sarsfield
utterances.text: You may see a poodle on a couch watching TV and a hunting dog covered in rain and dripping with mud and think you know which one's living the good life, but a poodle is a hunting dog, and I promise you the happy dog is the money one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Mindy Pless, a native New Yorker and horsewoman now living on a Virginia farm, testifies about her childhood carriage ride inspiring her career, argues the park's carriage paths were designed for horses, and warns PETA's agenda extends to all equine disciplines.
chapters.title: Mindy Pless (horsewoman) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I will.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: My name is Mindy Pless, a native New Yorker, but I now live on a farm in Virginia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: If I may share a success story of the carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I'm not one of the carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: My career is in animal health, but I've been a lifelong horsewoman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I was a horse-crazy kid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: My parents didn't own horses, but my grandmother indulged me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: She was a buyer at one of the department stores in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: She said, you need to come to Central Park with me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I need to show you something that you'll never believe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: We went on a carriage ride.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: And I was besotted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I just loved it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: It was fantastic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I read about the history of the park and the carriages and the original designers of the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: It was meant for three specific carriage paths, horse trails, and pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: When I'm looking at that recent video, although horrific that that boy died and I let him rest in peace, I'm looking at that video and I'm going, what the heck happened to the carriage paths?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: You have every mode of transportation on there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: As a horsewoman, I would never allow my child, because she's inherited my love for horses, be on a horse with other modes of transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: You have bikes, you have e-bikes, you have rollerbladers, you have kids, you have everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: Those paths were made specifically for the carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: And I'm sorry, but when I hear that word antiquity, I'm still riding, and I'm still riding horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: Just because it's not the 1800s and we don't use horses for transportation doesn't mean people like me that have a passion for riding and being on a horse,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: are you going to come after me next, seizing my horses, because you say, oh, get off, you're hurting that horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: This is my fear with PETA involved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: You know their agenda is to end any equine discipline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: I am here to tell you, no, you cannot allow the precedent to be set.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: And I ask you to reconsider this and listen to the carriage drivers and come up with solutions and let the horses have those carriage paths back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mindy Pless
utterances.text: Thank you for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: You want to go?

chapters.summary: Dave Cook, a carriage driver since 1982, testifies about the real estate motivations behind the ban, pointing to boarded-up buildings near the stables and Hudson Yards development as evidence of a land grab.
chapters.title: Dave Cook (carriage driver since 1982) testifies on real estate motivations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Dave Cook.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: I have been a carriage driver since August of 82.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: And yes, the correct characterization is long in the tooth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: I have been, this is third, possibly fourth of my appearances before this August board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: And the issues are almost always the same.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: The real estate that the carriages hold is extraordinarily valuable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: And the amount of effort to obtain that real estate knows no bounds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: And ethically, morally,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: Precisely what's going on today as in 40 years ago is a walk down 38th Street starting at 10th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You walk west towards the Javits Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You're going to come upon the Manhattan Hotel, 10, 12 stories.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: It looks like 1970s architecture to me, boarded up, ready for demolition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: Directly across the street, what was probably originally built as livery 100 some odd years ago, the last 100 years has been doing automotive repair, boarded up, ready for demolition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You walk down the street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: 100 yards, another building that was probably originally built for livery, has been doing automotive repair, boarded up, set for demolition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You look to the left, and there's our stables, 38th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You walk a few more steps, and you'll see the other stables, identical architecture, directly behind ours at 37th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: But you continue your gaze to the left, and there is the hood ornament of Hudson Yards, the vessel, two blocks right south of there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: There is no more precise...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: Definition of what is going on with the carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: One of my great loves of living in New York City is the level of critical thinking here is high.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: You don't take the testimony of, what are they called, night class?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: They're fully funded by the real estate industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: That is not testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dave Cook
utterances.text: That is propaganda.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Alexis.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I'm, what, the panel's not done yet?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No, I'm just letting him know that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Oh, all right.

chapters.summary: Alexis Lawson, a Queens native and carriage driver since 2021, testifies about her bond with her horse Midnight, who helped her through her mother's death, and argues animal welfare and animal rights are different topics.
chapters.title: Alexis Lawson (carriage driver) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Good afternoon, ladies and gentlemen left of the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: My name is Alexis Lawson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: I am a Queens native who is from South Jamaica, and I'm asking you to oppose Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Being a young girl from the South Side, I never had the opportunity to see the animals I was so obsessed with until the day I was allowed to pet a waiting carriage horse, and it set my path in stone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: I used to parrot animal abuse when I was a little girl.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: You know, I loved animals and I believed what I saw online.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Then I learned that animal welfare should never be about if an animal has a job or not, but rather about how the animal's physical and mental needs are being met.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: I transitioned into carriage driving in 2021, leaving an office job for my passion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: And it was my very first carriage horse, Midnight, who would be my saving grace as I lost my mom at the beginning of my career.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: She pulled me from my depression when nothing could, being my therapist and best friend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: As such, I could not imagine being ripped away from working aside my horse for him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Just the thought of not being able to work aside my horse is a soul question, for no one sees the dedication and love we share.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Instead, I look at her job and callously claim I don't love her.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Animal welfare is incredibly important, and I feel we need to remind people that animal welfare and animal rights are two incredibly different topics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: At this time, I would also like to extend my heartfelt condolences to the family of Ramanak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: The tragic passing of this young man was and still is felt deeply by our entire industry, which is why we are asking to move forward instead with Intro 930.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Because to take advantage of a family's laws for political gain is abhorrent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: And in light of that, we are requesting stronger driver training and safety in his honor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: He came here to take a carriage ride, as millions have done for over 100 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Carriage horses are woven into the fabric of New York City's identity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: How dare you tell them they do not belong?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: How can you claim to love horses but then want to destroy their careers, take food from their mouths, from their families and their owners who love them?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: I strongly urge the council to oppose 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: And instead, I ask you all to focus and support until 937, which is working on reforming the industry as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexis Lawson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro has a question for this panel.

chapters.summary: Gennaro calls the hearing a pageant, criticizes the Speaker's pre-announcement, and asks TWU what mechanisms they'll bring to fight for a hearing on his alternative reform bill. Kemp says truth and facts are their best weapons.
chapters.title: Gennaro questions TWU on strategy for alternative reform bill

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yeah, I was really going to speak to Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Kemp and Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Donahue, but the last witness, I don't know your name yet, very, very compelling.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: testimony, I wish to be associated with your good remarks, and I thank you for being a dedicated horsewoman who has found her niche in life and wants nothing more than to chart her own future

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: in this great industry.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Now I'm going to take it down.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, we've got to get in the weeds here with, you know, Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Kemp and Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Donahue.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: As we saw,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And the Times, you know, yesterday, like the day before the hearing, the day before the hearing, speaker comes out and says,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I know what we're going to do.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: We're going to do this with the workers.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: We're going to do this with the horses.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: We're going to do this with the stables.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I got it all figured out.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Like, this is done.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so what this hearing is, unfortunately, is, I mean, is a pageant.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So the speaker's already decided, like, unilaterally, like, that this is happening.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I'll, you know, and he's got the real estate money, all this other stuff, and I don't know what's going on.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I just don't like the policy, and I don't like, you know, like the way all this is playing out.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so if this is a power game now, you know, this has to be up to...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, TWU, to use all of its muscle, to reach out to immigrant rights organizations.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I don't see them here.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, it must be that the carriage drivers are not

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: you know, are not their kinds of immigrants.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And that is a real shame.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roger Mosier
utterances.text: Jim, do you have a question?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yeah, and so I just want to ask TWU what mechanisms you're going to bring to bear to call for at least a

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: hearing on my bill so that we can have an alternative to shutting the whole thing down.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And because everything that's been discussed today as it turns to what's needed to make it better, I've already done in 2010.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: This would be, you know, 2.0 of the reforms.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And it has a lot to do with hitching posts.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: They can spend all kinds of money.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: What can TWU bring to bear in terms of your own resources and your connections to the immigrant rights groups, to both Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Kemp and Mr.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Donahue?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: So I'll go first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I think the best weapon to bring to this fight is the truth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I think the facts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Are overwhelming that this industry has not represented one that should be removed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I think when we talk about the components that are established when they say that you cannot predict what a horse is going to do if it's spooked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Well, a person riding the F train can't predict whether somebody's going to push them in front of a train.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: A person walking down the street can't predict whether somebody's going to rob them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: A person who walks out of their house cannot guarantee they're going to leave or get back the same way they left.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: If we're basing an industry on the unknown, well, then no industry would be able to survive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I think the foundation of this argument is rooted in people being bullies who have an argument that they feel they can leverage, and that is a dead 18-year-old

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: that they are going to try to bully the union because in years past, the evidence and the facts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: have not been on their side and they feel like this is a golden bullet that they can shoot at the head of the union and the members of the union when all intents and purposes the industry actually does not represent what is being projected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: And I just want to say for what Curtis Sliwa has stated, if he's going to make a statement about Local 100, I think it's important for the record to establish this is a person

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: who said he was kidnapped and lied about it on the record.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: And so his credibility is very limited as it comes when he reports on what Local 100 actually is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, that's it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate you coming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I was asked a question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Am I not allowed to answer?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, you were?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: Yeah, because I directed to both Camp and Donio.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alexander Kemp
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I think there's an education process that's happened, and I think there are still some reasonable council members who are not going to be cowered by the threats in the social media

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: and see the importance of standing up for working immigrants and not just throw them under the bus with some vague language of possible job opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I think we need to get out there and get the immigrant organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Who represent these groups, but also talk more about the bill that Councilman Gennaro introduced.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I mean, several people testified basically today, including the lawyer for the Romance family, that the city hasn't done anything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And so there's a lot of room if the city has

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There's a lot of things, common sense things that can be done if the city has concerns that are short of throwing people under the bus, so to speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And Jim's, Councilman Gennaro's bill does that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And we're still hoping and optimistic that New York City government isn't completely dysfunctional.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That there are council members who have some common sense and that they can realize that, you know, there is a middle ground here and that New York is the greatest city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And New York City, if it puts it, if it can throw a parade for 4 million people in three days to celebrate, God bless the Knicks, if it can have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: every agency to send on a building that was possibly crumbling, that maybe it could actually pay some attention and celebrate this industry and support these drivers and do something other than just driving them into unemployment.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next panel is Brian Shapiro, Wayne Purcell, Bill Ketzer.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Mian Asumi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Kathleen Schatzman and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Joanna Grossman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please keep your, I want to remind people, please keep your testimony to two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Once the bell rings, just wrap it up and then submit your testimony to us, please.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's really important, otherwise we're going to be here until midnight and we can't do that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're not going to be able to accommodate everybody that wants to testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So who's going first?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Brian Shapiro of Humane World for Animals testifies about records revealing DOHMH and DCWP don't effectively share data linking horses to carriages and drivers, and says rescue organizations stand ready to help prevent any horse going to slaughter.
chapters.title: Brian Shapiro (Humane World for Animals) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: Hello, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Shulman, Honorable Council Members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: My name is Brian Shapiro, Director of Humane World for Animals, formerly the Humane Society of the United States.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: We're here supporting Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: This legislation would address many of the safety shortcomings that the horse carriage industry has continued for decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: The industry's answer is to put more rules in place, all funded by taxpayers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: The representatives suddenly acknowledged that existing rules were never consistently followed and authorities are now needed to enforce them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: Compliance should not look like a regulator standing over a horse carriage with an open ticket book and pen in hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: The industry has traditionally controlled its own operations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: It didn't need to wait for another law or death or accident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: It could have done so after the 2007 or 2009 audits, and it could have done so in the past 160 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: City agencies have tried regulations, yet serious problems remain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: In fact, our recent records requests expose serious problems in the oversight that go unnoticed by the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: DOHMH only maintains records on horses, while DCWP maintains records on drivers, carriages, businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: There's no evidence that the agencies effectively share and link records together to say what horse is pulling what carriage, for which driver or which owner, how long are the horses working.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: There needs to be a hard look at this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: Humane World for Animals and other rescue organizations stand ready and have always stood ready to help with this situation so no animal goes to horse slaughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: I think it's fantastical and outrageous to say that these animals are done for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: I am not an equine surgeon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: I am someone who has 26 years'experience in this, and I stand, my organization stands by, Catskill Animal Sanctuary, equine advocates.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: We are not fly-by-night operations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: We are professionals who are here to assist and help these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Shapiro
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to present, and thank you for your time today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Dr. Joanna Grossman of the Animal Welfare Institute testifies about heading the Homeless Horses Coalition of 500+ rescues, supports the ban, and lists welfare concerns including exhaust, noise, traffic, long hours, and lack of pasture access.
chapters.title: Dr. Joanna Grossman (Animal Welfare Institute) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: I can go.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One of you should go.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Good morning, or good afternoon, I should say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Joanna Grossman, and I'm the equine program director for the Animal Welfare Institute.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: We are grateful to Speaker Menon, Councilmember Marte, Chair Schulman, and the committee for calling this hearing on Intro 943, Ramach's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: AWI, one of the nation's oldest animal protection groups, heads up the Homeless Horses Coalition, which is a network of over 500 equine rescues and sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: We fully support this legislation to face out horse-drawn carriages in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: This reform is long overdue, and we trust that the council will do right by ending these unnecessary rides.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: The question is not whether horses can pull these carriages, but whether the circumstances pose too many risks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: And the answer unequivocally is yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages do not need to continue in such a densely populated setting with conditions that run counter to equine welfare, not to mention countless factors that can cause these horses to spook and bolt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Ramach's death is an unimaginable tragedy that no family should have to face.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: It should weigh heavily on all of us as we consider how to prevent more accidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: I don't think there was a dry eye in the room hearing what that family has endured.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: As the Central Park Conservancy put it, this is the tragedy we feared when we first called for horse carriages to be banned from Central Park due to the risks they pose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: In June, immediately after a 16-year-old horse named Denise collapsed and died, struggling to breathe in front of onlookers, Philadelphia became the latest city to prohibit horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: New York must be a leader in this space before the next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: inevitable accident occurs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: There have been far too many troubling incidents in recent years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Continuing the status quo is not tenable for the sake of New Yorkers, visitors to the city, and the horses themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: The risks and problems are well understood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: The exhaust, pollution, noise, lights, traffic, congestion, construction, asphalt, temperature extremes,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Long hours, lack of access to pastures and turnout.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: These are just some of the welfare concerns that exist and the list could easily go on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: How many times do we have to see a horse collapse or spook before taking action?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Hopefully no longer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joanna Grossman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

chapters.summary: Wayne Pacelle of Animal Wellness Action testifies comparing the ban to historical shifts away from whaling, cockfighting, and dogfighting, calling it a balanced approach with worker transition support.
chapters.title: Wayne Pacelle (Animal Wellness Action) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: I'm Wayne Pacelli.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Okay, Wayne.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: I'm president of Animal Wellness Action.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: You know, in the 19th century, there were whale oil lamps around Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Are we going to persist with the understanding that there were innovative energy sources that were coming online?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: With whaling, are we going to say we're going to take the jobs from the whalers and therefore we're going to continue to kill whales because we want those lamps, even though we have superior

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: alternative methods to deliver light when it's dark at night?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: I led a ballot measure in Oklahoma to outlaw cockfighting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: In 2002, not that long ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: It was legal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: There were 2.5 million fighting birds in the state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: There were 10,000 cockfighters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: You, Madam Chair, and other members

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: have been gracious in talking about providing economic support for people who have a vestigial practice that doesn't generate much money, it's just a very small number of people, the city is saying they're going to help them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: There was no support for the people to get money, all the cockfighters in that state of Oklahoma.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: The same thing happened in Louisiana, the same thing happened in the 19th century with dogfighting here in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: This is a very balanced approach that you've taken.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: You're identifying a practice that is demonstrably inhumane to the horses, that led to the death of a boy that if this council had acted a short time ago would be alive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: We have all sorts of other transportation opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: We have all sorts of tourism opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Why are we holding on to this practice, seeing that Chicago and Philadelphia and cities around the country have addressed this issue?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: We hear arguments from the proponents of this that this is going to lead to the end of all animal use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Where are such proposals?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Where have we heard such nonsense before?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: Real estate people, I have no idea what they're talking about with real estate issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: It's a bunch of nonsense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: You all are on the right track.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: I encourage you to have such a balanced approach.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Kathleen Schatzman of the Animal Legal Defense Fund testifies about a 2014 FOIA lawsuit revealing dozens of hit-and-runs and injuries, commits to contributing transport costs for horses, and calls the ban a public safety issue.
chapters.title: Kathleen Schatzman (Animal Legal Defense Fund) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: My name is Kathleen Schatzman, Strategic Legislative Affairs Manager with the Animal Legal Defense Fund.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: And I want to start by thanking you, Council Member Marte.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: for introducing this bill, and to all of the 21 council members that are signed on in support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: We, too, are supportive and committed to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: And the Animal Legal Defense Fund is contributing to the cost of transport for the horses should you vote to pass Intro 943, Ramach's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: I mention that because there has been discussion and talk about the cost and financial aspect of a transition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: We're all here as animal protection organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: However, make no mistake, this is a public safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: In 2014, the Animal Legal Defense Fund's successful State Freedom of Information Act lawsuit revealed that New York City's carriage drivers had committed more than a dozen hit-and-runs over the previous five years and that children,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: cyclists, pedestrians, carriage drivers, passengers, and NYPD officers had all been physically injured by accidents involved in New York City's horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: In one incident, and I think this just needs us to go into detail a bit, one child was rushed to the hospital after falling out of a carriage and run over by a wheel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: In another incident, a carriage driver was found by police lying in the street, not moving, after being thrown to the ground.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: Another carriage driver was hospitalized in a coma after his horse became startled and collided with a station wagon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: This latest...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: unimaginable tragedy exemplifies the urgent need to bring an end to the horse carriage industry in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: A prohibition on horse-drawn carriages is the only meaningful way to definitively protect the public and the horses in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: And we urge you all to pass Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: But I do want to make one last comment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Schatzman
utterances.text: New York City is the greatest city in the world, and it will remain so without horse carriage rides.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Mia?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Mia Nosumi of Voters for Animal Rights testifies representing 100,000+ NYC voters, cites consistent polling showing two-thirds majority support for a ban, and lists decades of horse deaths from various causes.
chapters.title: Mia Nosumi (Voters for Animal Rights) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: My name is Mia Nosumi, and I am the Policy and Advocacy Manager at Voters for Animal Rights.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: We represent a community of over 100,000 New York City voters for whom animal protection is a top priority at the ballot box.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: First, I want to thank Speaker Menon for not only scheduling this hearing, but being an advocate of this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Councilmember Shulman, thank you for your leadership as chair of the Health Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Councilmember Marte for...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Prime sponsoring Romantus Law, previously Ryder's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Thank you as well to the Council's Animal Caucus, led by Councilmember Epstein, for standing behind this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Across multiple polls, New York City voters have consistently supported a horse carriage ban by over a two-thirds majority, and voters are united because the pattern of suffering is undeniable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: As previously mentioned, in the 13 months before Ramanj Mahajan's tragic death on June 17th, and thank you to his family for their immense courage testifying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Central Park saw eight separate horse-related incidents, but the casualties of this industry go back decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: The first named carriage horse who died on the streets of New York City was a horse named Maggio in 1982.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Since then, dozens of horses have been killed by this industry by sheer exhaustion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Traffic collisions, untreated sickness, getting electrocuted, malnourishment, poisonous plants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: The causes of death tell the story of the many ways in which horses simply do not belong in the middle of our bustling modern city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: This industry doesn't just fail horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: It fails the people who work in it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Which is why Voters for Animal Rights eagerly supports Mayor Mamdani's Department of Consumer and Worker Protection to transition these carriage drivers into safer, higher-paying jobs so these workers can not only survive but thrive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: The horses deserve better, and the carriage drivers also deserve better than gig work with no sick pay, retirement, or health benefits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: Each time in...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mia Nosumi
utterances.text: How many more horses and humans have to die by this industry before this council decides the cost is too high?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And Bill, right?

chapters.summary: Bill Ketzer of the ASPCA testifies about the organization's 160-year history protecting horses, calling the industry inherently dangerous and inhumane, and citing eight incidents in 13 months as evidence regulation cannot eliminate risks.
chapters.title: Bill Ketzer (ASPCA) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Madam Chair, members of the Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
