utterances.text: I'm Bill Ketzer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: I'm Senior Director of Government Relations for the ASPCA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: And I appreciate this opportunity to testify in support of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: The ASPCA has worked to protect horses since our

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: founding in 1866, and our support for this legislation is rooted in that 160-year history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: We've endorsed many efforts to regulate carriage horse welfare over the years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: While those reforms have improved certain aspects of the industry, they cannot address the fundamental and persistent challenges associated with horses pulling carriages through one of the busiest and loudest and most densely populated urban environments on the planet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: It is inherently dangerous and inherently inhumane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Our position is not based on this summer's horrific events alone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: It reflects years of experience and recognition that the risks associated with horse-drawn carriage operations are not avoidable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Carriage horses must navigate congested streets, construction zones, emergency vehicles, constant traffic, and thousands of pedestrians every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: These intelligent and sensitive animals are large, and they are powerful, and even the best trained horse can react unpredictably when frightened or startled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: The recent death of carriage horse Denise, followed days later by the tragic death of 18-year-old Romach Mahajan in Central Park, remind us that the consequences extend beyond equine welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: These were not isolated incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: In the last 13 months alone, as others have said, a horse has collapsed and died, horses have run into moving traffic, collisions overturned carriages, tourists have been forced to leap from runaway carriages, and multiple drivers have been injured.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: The growing consensus is that the risks associated with this activity cannot be eliminated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: through additional regulation, and New York City should adopt a lasting solution, not more studies or another decade of incremental reforms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: We need a credible transition that protects horses, supports workers, reflects the values of a modern city, and we respectfully urge you to pass Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks how many violations ASPCA identified during stable inspections. Ketzer says ASPCA no longer does humane law enforcement, having transferred that role to NYPD via MOU in 2014.
chapters.title: Schulman questions ASPCA on stable inspection violations

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have a couple questions actually for you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is ASPCA agents are authorized to inspect stables.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many violations has the ASPCA identified during stable inspections over the past five years?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: We no longer do humane law enforcement for the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: We have an MOU with NYPD since 2014.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That was the other questions followed that one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So no, so don't worry about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Bill Ketzer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much to the, oh, no, Jim, I'm sorry.

chapters.summary: Gennaro criticizes ASPCA for not addressing toxic plants in Central Park and for providing opinion rather than facts. Schulman intervenes, noting witnesses are allowed to testify on their organizational positions.
chapters.title: Gennaro criticizes ASPCA testimony as opinion-based

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro has questions.

utterances.text: The population of Manhattan is 1.6 million people.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: In 1950, it was 2 million.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And the cars used to be a lot stinkier.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Lead it gas.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So there goes that argument.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And regarding the ASPCA, now I understand it's just like a whole lobbying thing.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But once upon a time, you were out there.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And you were where the, you know, you were like in the park, you were in the stables all the time.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I think your people just got like bored of not finding anything.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so, you know, and so you've, and also,

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: As an animal rights organization, you know, I'll say this again.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I mean, have you done anything about like the Japanese you?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Are you concerned about that?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It's toxic to all animals.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But yet I'm sure you guys haven't made a peep about that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so, and...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Anyway, I don't have a high opinion of the ASPCA.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: What you've given us here is not fact, but it's simply your opinion.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so I guess my question is, where do you get off?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Where do you get off?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Just coming here.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Your testimony is like your personal opinion, which I guess you're allowed, but you're representing an organization.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: If you were here by yourself, I'd say, fine, voice any opinion you want.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: But you come here and you just give us your own personal opinion.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I don't know if that's ASPCA policy.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You spout things without any kind of backup.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And this is why people are sick of the ASPCA.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Council member, they're allowed to testify to what they want on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can't question that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you have a question specific to what they do as an organization,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's fine.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Otherwise, I'm not going to allow it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I made my point.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: I'd like to answer about leaded gasoline.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm not asking you a question.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I don't want to back and forth.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm not asking you a question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wayne Pacelle
utterances.text: You raised the issue.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm not asking you a question.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time is out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate your testimony.

chapters.summary: Schulman announces a 15-minute break before the next panel.
chapters.title: Chair announces 15-minute break

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're going to take a 15-minute break, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We'll take a break.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to put our heads together.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Diabetic me is like almost out of snacks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I didn't realize I'd been here for 10.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I was going to also say, I'm signed up to give testimony, but, like, everyone said what I needed to say.

utterances.text: Yeah, so you don't, like, you don't have to, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: You don't have to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No, no, like I'm saying, like, you don't want to, anything I say would be redundant at this point, so, like, I don't have to.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Folks, could we just please all find our seats?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please find our seats.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please take conversations outside the chambers.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Quiet on the floor, please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Quiet on the floor, please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you wish to speak today and you haven't filled out an appearance card, please see one of the sergeant arms and fill out an appearance card, even if you pre-registered to speak online.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman

chapters.summary: Ahmed Bilici, a Turkish immigrant and carriage driver/owner since 2003, testifies through a translator about his American dream, his investment in the business, and his love for his horses as family members.
chapters.title: Ahmed Bilici (Turkish carriage driver) testifies with translator

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: My name is Ahmed Bilici, I'm a Turkish American, I'm an immigrant here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: I'm a horse carriage driver and I'm an owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: I've been a carriage driver since 2003 in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: We want this job very much, because it is our life, our livelihood, our future, our whole life, our family, our children, our future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: And this is not just a livelihood for us, it's a way of life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Not just for me, but also for my family too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: In my family,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: Mind you, actually, I made this as my career for the last 23 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I've put on all of my savings into this business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: We want to protect our athletes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: We want to talk about different things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: We want to make them more secure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: We want to make them more secure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Sheyla yapıyor, da kaya çisinden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Ama bu Central Park'un bir simgesi, New York'un bir simgesi, 150, 160 yılık bir iş.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I believe that this is a heritage, this is a symbol of Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I can tell for myself and for the other carry drivers that we've been doing all of our best for the safety and all the concerns of the New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You're going to get an extra two minutes because of the translation, so just put another two minutes on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Eşte Amerika gelme hayalımızın mıttaníası, burada hayat kurmak, daha iyi şartlarda yaşama, Çuklurun daha iyi geleceğe için,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: daha iyi şartlarda olmasını sağlamak için geliyoruz, çalışıyorumuz bir yer burası.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I came to this country to live the American dream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I spent almost a quarter century.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: and living with this business, put my families and my future into this business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: Now everybody's gathered here and talking about actually putting them on the street, living with no work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Sadece işimizi kaybetmek olmazın, yatırımız şeylerde kaybeteceğiz, yatırımız, 25 yılık birikimizi yatırdımız.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Çocuklarım okula gidiyor buraya, üniversite gidiyor, ikinci oğlum lise gidiyor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Bütün geleceğimizi onlar için yestek.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: They're only talking about a job replacement and all that, good things, but they're not talking about my entire life savings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: I have kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: They're all going to college.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: What do they want me to do, like pack up and leave?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Artı, bizim atlarımız, bizim aileminizden bireyilerden gibi, yani sadece böle sabah 9.85 çalışma işimiz değil, 24 saat

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: ilgide şey yaptımız, 24 saat ilgilendimiz bakım yaptımız hayvanlar.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: So they may think that the horses, with the horses we spend only between 95, but it is not the fact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: Some people also mentioned here that we've been actually living and breathing with them sometimes 24 hours a day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Mesle, çalışmadımız günler da ahlara, onlarla ilgilendir biliyoruz, onlara duş aldırız, bakımları yapıyoruz, emin olmak istiyorum, sağlılar mı, iyi ler mı, konfor, şeyler yerinde mı.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Yani, bu narım, aileminizin parçası.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: So we don't just, we're not with the horses only when we're working.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: On our off days or the times or the hours that we're not working, we're still with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: We're giving them a bath, we're feeding them, making sure that actually they're taken care of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: We see them as a part of our family.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, he needs to summarize and wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Tama.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Yani, nasıl bizim ülkemiz olduğu gibi atlar çok önemli insanlara şeyse, 5.000 yıllık geçmişi var.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: New York'da, Amerika'da at lükesi, yani atların New York...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: New York'un parçası, ona göre şey yapıyoruz.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ahmed Bilici
utterances.text: Hatta burada da hi, yani Amerika'nin duvar da hi atın resim var, orada görmüş olduğumuz gibi hatlar bizim insanlar parçası çok seviyoruz, onları kurmak istiyorum ve işimizi sahib çıkma istiyorum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: Of course, the horses have been all part of our lives for thousands of years, and even here in this room I can see.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: That it is also on a picture, on a painting right here on the wall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: And I believe the part of the city also built with horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: And, you know, sadly that they want to get rid of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Saymaz
utterances.text: We want the horses to be part of our lives in the city.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is the mic on?

chapters.summary: Cornelius Byrne testifies about the need for hitching posts, argues the fatal accident would not have happened with proper infrastructure, and speculates that stray voltage from underground wiring at Cherry Hill may have caused the horse to bolt.
chapters.title: Cornelius Byrne (carriage owner) testifies on hitching posts and stray voltage theory

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Okay?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, and pull it towards your mouth so that we can hear you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: My name is Cornelius Byrne, and when the park was first built, there were hitching places all over the place, and they were very essential, very needed, and they provided safety and security.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Today, I've heard them kind of minimized here today, as if they were a thing that wouldn't work anyway if they were installed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: But for safety and security reasons, we need these, and we have begged and pleaded for them and never got them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And they're so essential, and you've got to listen to the people that are in the business about what they want.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: We needed these hitching posts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: The fatal accident that happened in Central Park would never have happened, and this fellow would be alive, if we were allowed to have hitching posts where we bring those carriages for pictures and where we stand

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: waiting for rides.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: A few horses jumped off the line and ran through the park last summer and it was recorded.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: But as I say again, if a hitching post tied those horses in, that would not have happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Now, the horse at the fountain that ran away, he ran away so viciously, nobody has ever experienced that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Speed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: He acted as if he was electrocuted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And maybe you get varying thoughts on what caused this, but I did a little research, and at Cherry Hill,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: there is a massive infrastructure of underground wiring that is an important part of the power grid for the whole of Central Park and for the surrounding areas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And a recent irrigation project was done there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: The footings were put in wrong, and the wrong materials were used.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And the wires probably were disturbed because they dug those foundations wrong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And as I say, there's a spaghetti of wires down there below the ground.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And not a lot of people would realize that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: This area is now wet and muddy and sandy throughout.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Wet sand is highly conductive to electricity.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You need to summarize your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And a horse standing there with four shoes could easily be hit with stray voltage in the sand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: This could be the very probable cause of what happened to that horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And I need to add one thing, that the same landscape management team that planted the poisonous flowers that killed the horse dentist were the same people who did the irrigation project in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And just to look at it, you would understand that if it's not dangerous, it's at least illegal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: It could never pass code.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Now, one thing else.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, you need to summarize.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm telling you, everybody has two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: We've been here for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But we also have other people that are not going to wait until midnight.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I'm telling you to summarize it or else we're going on to the next person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: There's livelihoods involved here for these people, and they're very determined to keep them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: And there's the lives of the horses, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Let's keep them alive.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: Because you're going to ruin both if you pass this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cornelius Byrne
utterances.text: You're welcome.

chapters.summary: Tarek, a carriage driver and nephew of Denise's owner, testifies about leaving a software career for his passion working with horses, describes Denise as his teacher, and fears losing all his horses.
chapters.title: Tarek (carriage driver) testifies about his bond with horses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Okay, I'll be next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Hello, dear council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: My name is Tarek.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: The thing what I'm doing right now is some kind of a family business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: You probably heard the name, Denis the horse died in the park because of he ate a poisoned plant, unfortunately, and his owner, Nurettin Kurvik, right over here, is my uncle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Like I said, it's some kind of a family business and I started to work here thanks to them, him and his brother, Umet Kurbik.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Before that, I was earning my life with the company that I created with two of my friends by developing computer games.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And before that, I graduated from a good college.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: With a 3.5 GPA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And however, this process that started to help my uncles and started spending time with the horses, is it helped me to discover my actual passion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And that's how, you know, I always defined myself as an animal lover.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And that's how I became able to work with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: There was something precious for me, you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: So much so that I left my company, that left that company that I created and started to work here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: So, I mean, okay, the AI stuff are coming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: It may take many people's job, but it will never replace my horse, my beautiful horse, Leila.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And I'm not afraid of that, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: I mean, I can make money somehow from software stuff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: The actual thing what I fear is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: The possibility of shutting down the horse carriage business because unlike the many of these folks behind me, the animal activists, I'm not

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: calling my, I will never call myself an animal abuser, you know, because

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Like I told you before, I love animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Denise was also my horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: I worked with him for two years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: And I learned everything from him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: So, you know, I lost him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: That was so hard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: He was only one of the eight horses that I worked with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: I cannot imagine how it will be hard for me to lose all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: I just don't want any alternative job or any way to earn money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: I just want to keep going my life in that way with my horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tarek
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Natalia Kovanova, a carriage driver for 10+ years, reads a letter from her daughter about the carriage community being quintessential New Yorkers, and argues for preserving the industry through reform rather than elimination.
chapters.title: Natalia Kovanova (carriage driver) reads daughter's letter opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Natalia Kovanova.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: I'm a carriage driver, more than 10 years working in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: And my daughter, she wants me to read this letter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: She left to Arizona this morning, and she wanted me to say from her.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: I used to work in the center park carriage industry, and my mom still does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: And I can say these people truly have your back, and they care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: The truth is they are some of the best people I know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: They are quintessence of true New Yorkers, hard workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Always looking for a way to lend a hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: They love their community and they love their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: I understand the world is changing and is the city, the city does, but this is such a special group of people and community that is still exist in our world of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: artificial intelligence and advanced technologies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Maybe the focus should be on how we preserve horses and livelihoods, such as this rather than making more room for more boxes of concrete.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Maybe the social justice activists should be focused on the grid that is bringing businesses like this down rather than harassing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Hardworking people, caring people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Perhaps a more reasonable solution is water station, tie posts, perhaps as a green lawns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: There are no, they are not bad guys.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Countless vets defend them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: The horses are shiny and soft shape.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Unfortunately, the reality of horse work is accident happened, but it can tell you this for sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: I can tell you this for sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: They are a lot more common in every often part of the horse world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Jumping, barrel racing, and any other kind of things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Accidents also happen when you ride a bike and don't see a car coming or go swimming and happen to run into the shark.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: It doesn't mean we stop living or doing things we love, but we can always educate and readjust how we do things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: And so I am asking you to help us with how and stand up for the little guys and carriage drivers and beloved horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalia Kovanova
utterances.text: Natalia.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Put your mic on.

chapters.summary: John Quadrosi Jr. testifies opposing the ban, arguing the industry is misunderstood by non-horse people, that horse blinders prevent spooking, and that government should regulate rather than eliminate through infrastructure like hitching posts and water troughs.
chapters.title: John Quadrosi Jr. testifies opposing ban, calls for regulation

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Hi, my name is John Quadrosi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: I'm here in opposition of the ban that's being proposed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: While there has been an uprising as of late and periodically in the past for a ban, it is mostly brought about by exaggeration and acceleration by animal rights organizations that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: have been visibly pushing this agenda, who many have publicly expressed their desire to remove animals from people's lives and habitat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Something that would be a one-sided point of view and tragic on a major scale in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: The carriage horses, which are niche activity and easily misunderstood by non-horse experienced people,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: non-horse care related individuals, where things like horse blinders that protect horses from distractions and spooking, or pulling a carriage on level ground like New York City is less laborious than riding a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: These are easily misunderstood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Like in any activity, there are accidents and bad actors for which government puts in rules and enforcement, as is reasonably needed, without imposing unnecessarily on functionality and enjoyment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Here, too, there must be a balancing of acts of rules, enforcement to keep horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: carriage operators, and the public's right of enjoyment intact and safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: This includes things like have been mentioned already, establishing rules for reasonable care that toxic plants to specific animals are not put where these animals are known to frequent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Reasonable requests for hitching posts and watering troughs are provided so that horses that are typically idle and stopped can be accommodated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: And oddly, this is even something, as mentioned, is not new to New York City, but somehow has gone missing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Historically, there were always...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Water troughs and hitching posts.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please summarize the rest of what you have to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Infrastructure for horse and welfare and signage for public awareness is a key responsibility of government.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: To quote something that I've often seen, this is a historical, cultural, and beautiful activity that we must protect and preserve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: No group, no matter the majority nor minority, should infringe upon this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: I shudder to think that if every

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Every time an emotionally mob-driven mentality prevailed, what function of government would be to their duty in preserving the interests of all?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Here, the effects of one area of...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, you've got to summarize.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Your time is up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So you've got a couple of sentences, and then you can submit the rest of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Here, what seems to be simple enough, that for those that don't like it, they don't have to ride, but for those that do, they should be allowed to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: All horses are domesticated and thrive best by continuous human care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: And their horse-related activities as their exercise in keeping them both healthy, strong, and preserving their mental and physical well-being.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: And if I could just note something about pasture, just some information.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You've got to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tell us what your point is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: So what's mostly, you know, misunderstanding about pasture is that although it is important, it is not all about their health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: It is not the whole picture of horse health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Nor is it without common risk that exposure to invasive plants, even in pasture, happens.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Quadrosi
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Nurettin Kurbik, owner of the horse Denise who died, testifies about receiving threats from animal activists, describes Denise's sudden death in two minutes, and complains about the difficulty of getting a horse driver license, noting the exam is only in English.
chapters.title: Nurettin Kurbik (Denise's owner) testifies on threats and licensing issues

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: My name is Nurettin Kurbik.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm Turkish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm in the United States 26 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: In 1999, I came to the United States.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm a horse carriage driver and owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Last 10 years as owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Dennis was my horse that poisoned me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm still getting threatened from social media.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Even people knew it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm getting a message.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: They said, we know where you work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Not hard to find your address, is they saying that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm just keeping, I never answer nobody, any message.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm pushing myself to work, a very hard situation for me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: And even they knew what's happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I'm in this business over 20 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I know a sick horse giving a sign.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: It's just, you know, looking back and the air is back and trying to lie down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Dennis was very held to horse before dying, two minutes before, until two minutes before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Horse died in two minutes, very quick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: It just shook the body.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I thought he's trying to pee, something happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I have a customer, two customers, I said, please get out, something going wrong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: I see that he just lied down and very quick died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: So it's still threatening us really badly from crazy animal activities, not just us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Even this city, for example, the horse driver exam was four times a year always before COVID.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: They started to do once a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Now it's like two, whatever they want.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: They're treating us like they don't like us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: And the one is the exam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Only 25 chairs that you can get a horse driver exam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: The one of them, 2024, is only six of them passed a test, as two of them passed the writing one, and only one passed practical exam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: She was an Ecuadorian veterinary doctor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: You have to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Easier than become a PhD, NYU or Columbia University, become a horse driver license getting in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: There is no option that the Turkish, because American kids, they don't want the kind of job as they have more chance to do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: This is immigrant job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Please help us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: We have to have a good driver.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: We can help them.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Gennaro notes the driver exam is only in English despite drivers speaking multiple languages, asks staff to investigate, thanks Kurbik for his industry leadership, and emphasizes the need for hitching posts.
chapters.title: Gennaro addresses language access and hitching posts with carriage drivers

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Gennaro?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Yes, I'll try to make it quick.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: The owner of Dennis, I just want to point out that you told me, and I think it's important for people to know, even though the city is obligated to have all of their information that goes out for

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: and whatever in multiple languages for the horse carriage driver test is only in English.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so I think, you know, you didn't have an opportunity to make that point.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I wanted people to know that.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And a note to staff who's here, I want that looked into.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And Neil.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so, you know, we go back to 1990 when we first...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Met in Noah Thier's office.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I just want to thank you for being a pillar of the horse carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Sure, you bet.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And to strongly make the point that I've been making all along, it's just...

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You know, we've been lucky, we, you know, I've been lucky to get by without hitching posts for a long time.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: They're critically necessary.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: They, you know, used to be out there.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And I thank you for making that point.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I'm going to drive home that point, you know, when I have an opportunity to talk to my colleagues about this bill.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And, you know, you go back long enough that you remember them.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And so I'm going to make that point.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Good to see you again.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And it's, you know, I'll do whatever I can for the industry, and I appreciate you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I appreciate you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: You bet.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you for coming to testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, the next panel, James Innes, Jeff Cohen.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Valerie Alton McCarthy, sorry if I mess up any names.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: James Innes, one more time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Jeff Cohen.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Valerie Altson McCarthy, Kyle Paskey, Janet White, and Catherine Freed.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So just want you to know that.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yeah, that's her on the left.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to call a few more folks over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nurettin Kurbik
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Celine Parsney, Parnese, Parnese, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Marilyn Brawlick, Brawlick, Marianne Brawlick, Eleanor Mogbagat.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry if I'm messing up people's names, but Celine.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Parnise.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Marianne Brolic.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Eleanor Walcott.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Maggie Nesfield.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Michael Pericelli?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman

chapters.summary: Catherine Freed, former City Council member who sponsored the original carriage horse bill 35 years ago, testifies that the industry was given chances to reform but hasn't, and 35 years is long enough to get it right.
chapters.title: Former Councilmember Freed testifies supporting ban after 35 years of reform attempts

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: My name is Catherine Freed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I live in Lower Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I'm actually a former city council member from Council District 1, same as Chris Marte.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And I wanted to thank Speaker Menon for calendaring this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Just Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Chair Shulman, and of course, Christopher Marte, who sponsored the bill, and the rest of the council members, and everyone here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I'm speaking in favor of the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I felt I had to come today because I sponsored the original horse carriage bill, and that's almost 35 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And everything I've heard so far here today is very similar to the problems we had then.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: At that time, there was a push also to ban horses from the horse carriages periodically from the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And at that time, however, it was legal for horse carriages to go throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: So we had horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: in the theater district, you know, the tourist district, Upper West Side, even sometimes in the Lower East Side.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And a lot of the arguments we heard today were the same arguments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: As I said, it's been 35 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: What we did is we limited horse carriages to the park, and we really thought that we would give the industry, the industry said we will clean up our act, we will take care of the horses, we won't have the problem with carriage

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: accidents and horses running and people being injured or drivers being injured.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And here we are 35 years later.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And the same problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I think 35 years is long enough for the industry to get it right if it's going to get it right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And the problem is it hasn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And just what's happened in the last several weeks with Denise dying, with Ramash Mahan, Mahaj, Mahajan, Mahajan, sorry,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: dying because the driver did not take proper care of the horse so that the horse would not run.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: The nature of horses hasn't changed in 30 years or 3,000 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: They spook, they run.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: They're not good in a cosmopolitan city like New York that has all kinds of noises and things that will always scare them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: So, and quite frankly, you also heard when Christopher-Marte was speaking initially that the industry has not a

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: allowed the veterinarians in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: We know the stalls are too small.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: We know they don't have enough pasture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: But the fact that they don't let the vets in to check the horses and make sure they're okay, not all those horses out there in the street are okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And I am a horse person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I was riding horses when I was eight years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I've ridden in Central Park or Prospect Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I've ridden

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: actually in Africa and South America and Central America.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I mean, I used to take people out on trail rides in Pennsylvania where I grew up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: So I know about horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I love horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: We tried.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: 35 years is enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: If the industry can't get it together or isn't interested enough to get it together in that amount of time, they're not going to get it together in another 100 years, and it's not going to be just hitching posts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: It's just that in a cosmopolitan city like New York, it just doesn't work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: It's not good for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: It's not good for the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: And quite frankly, they're here for the tourism industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: What does it look like when people are traumatized and crying because they see a horse die in the street?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: For that matter, the family, the tears from the family for Ramage when he died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: This is not a good look.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you please wrap up, Councilmember?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: So I would urge you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: You know, again, 35 years is enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: I would urge you to pass this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Catherine Freed
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And if you all could identify yourself.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just so that we can mark it off.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Eleanor Malbegot, legal counsel for the Humane Society of NY, testifies about drafting the 1981 rental horse law, noting that even after passage, four horses dropped dead in summer heat, and that reforms have not worked.
chapters.title: Eleanor Malbegot (Humane Society of NY) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: I'm Eleanor Malbegot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: I'm legal counsel for the Humane Society of New York right here on East 59th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: And I'm here to testify in favor of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: I'll even go back almost 50 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: I drafted the rental horse licensing and protection law, working with then-Councilmember Stern and Tony Olivieri, Morton Povman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: So these are like way back when.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: At that time, I was with the ASPCA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: We got the law passed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: We thought, great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Things will get better.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: There was concern about lame horses, overworking horses, accidents, horses working in traffic, and horses working in severe weather conditions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Even after the law passed, four horses dropped dead, literally, in New York City during the summer heat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: So concern for the horses, not so much in terms of the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: And even now, when I was listening to the testimony, I was listening to the health department say how many animals, how many horses they suspended.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Because of the heat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: I'm thinking, why did they even have to suspend the horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Why were they out there at all?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: We all know what the weather report is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Why would they even be out there and having to wait to be suspended?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: And just one last thing, because I,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: We all know about the accidents, and I don't want to be too repetitive in the other cities that have passed laws, but...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Another conversation I had with Councilmember Stern, who became Parks Commissioner Stern, after we had worked together on the city legislation, was there were efforts to have the horses go to the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: And he said to me, no, he said, as much as he was an advocate for the horses, he said, this is just going to bring all of the terrible problems we have in the city streets to the park with the pedestrians, with the bicyclists, with the cars, with everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: And he was right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: Like how many more accidents must there be before we do something now?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: The reform has not worked.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: The regulations have not worked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eleanor Malbegot
utterances.text: So it's time, and we thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Kyle Paseka delivers a spoken-word poem from the perspective of horses, describing their exploitation, fear, and desire for freedom.
chapters.title: Kyle Paseka delivers poem from horse's perspective

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Good afternoon, I'm Kyle Paseka, and I am here representing the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: This is for them as their spokesperson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Hey man, take a look at what you've done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Beat us, ride us, so you could have fun.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Whip us, race us, make us run.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: We're your beasts of burden, every single one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: It's our nature to oblige.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: We don't resist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Is that why you persist?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: We were once free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Once we were creatures of beauty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Now look at me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Your greed and vanity is killing me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Please let me be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Please save me and set me free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: How proud you are when we're something new, but when we get old and tired, we're through.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Our awful fate we never knew on somebody's plate, on a menu, dog food or glue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: We're lassoed, we're tattooed, we're broken and saddled, reined in, we're shooed, and then you do with us whatever you want to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Beat us, eat us, brand us, strand us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: We give you thrills and then we're killed and you jump us and dump us, you wear us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Seems you couldn't care less, so few are kind.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: So many are mean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Wonder what it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: It feels like to be a human being.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Please save the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Please save me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: You're killing me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kyle Paseka
utterances.text: Let me be.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Michael Petrelli, horse trainer and founder of Equine Advocacy Alliance, testifies about documenting unattended horses, including two the day before, and argues the risk outweighs tradition.
chapters.title: Michael Petrelli (horse trainer) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Speaker Menon, Chairperson Shulman, and the Health Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: My name is Michael Petrelli.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I'm an experienced horse trainer and the founder of Equine Advocacy Alliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I've spent decades working with horses, and I know firsthand the care, respect, and understanding they require.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: My testimony today is based on years of experience with horses and countless hours documenting the realities of the carriage horse industry in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: As a horseman, I can tell you that horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: They can be gentle and well-trained, but they also are instinctive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: When frightened, they react.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: No law, regulation, inspection, or training program can eliminate thousands of years of instinct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: That is why horses do not belong in one of the busiest and most congested cities in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I have witnessed horses working in heavy traffic, extreme weather, blaring sirens, overwhelming crowds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I have documented horses that have collapsed, horses involved in carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Most recently, just yesterday, two more horses left unattended by its driver, one taking pictures, no different than what happened to Ramanche, and another one who was holding a lead rope and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: sitting in the carriage in front of him in the back seat, just kicking back and relaxing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I've witnessed horses running loose through Central Park and city streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Every one of those incidents placed both the horses and the public in danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Now we have witnessed the unthinkable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: 18-Year-old Ramach Mahajan came to New York City from India to celebrate his graduation with his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Instead of returning home with happy memories, he lost his life during a horse-drawn carriage ride in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: My heart goes out to him and them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I can't put it into words.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: His death is a heartbreaking reminder that this is no longer just an animal welfare issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: It is also a public safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: These are things I learned over three years documenting this horse as coming to the park as a horseman, expecting to be treated and welcomed as one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: These people are not horsemen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: We have reached the point where the risk outweighs the tradition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: We owe to the horses, to New Yorkers, and to every visitor who comes to the city expecting to return home safely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: As an experienced horseman, I know what horses need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: New York is certainly not it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: It's quite the opposite.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: As an advocate, I documented what these horses endure on the streets of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: I respectfully ask that you pass Ramach's law and the horse-drawn carriage industry in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: The time has come to put the welfare of these horses and the safety of the public first.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sir, can you repeat your name just so that we can mark you down?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Petrelli
utterances.text: Michael Petrelli.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thanks.

chapters.summary: Janet White, founder of Carriage Horse Freedom in Philadelphia, testifies about Philadelphia's ban and shares a statement from Philadelphia Councilmember Mark Squilla encouraging NYC to pass the ban.
chapters.title: Janet White (Philadelphia advocate) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Janet White.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: I am the founder and director of Carriage Horse Freedom, which is a animal advocacy organization in Philadelphia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: I'm here to strongly support the passage of Ramansh's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: It's understandably, or rather it's unmistakably clear, especially in light of the recent tragedies, that the horse-drawn carriage, they just pose unacceptable risks to public safety and to animal welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: The sponsor of the bill, the ban horse carriage bill in Philadelphia, named Mark Squilla, he sent a statement to New York City Council members and Mayor Mamdani.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: I'd just like to share that with you briefly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: What he said was,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: Philadelphia's tried incremental welfare reforms over the years, but it became clear that neither public safety nor animal welfare could be fully protected without ending the practice altogether.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: Although we never experienced tragedies of this scale New York City has,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: We acted because the risks were unmistakable, and a full ban was the only way to prevent future harm to both people and horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: We encourage you to pass our Mancha's Law, and I also encourage you, I think,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: The majority of New Yorkers, the majority of reasonable, responsible people urge you to pass Ramansha's law without delay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janet White
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you all for coming, and the panel is dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The next panel is Ahmed Subir, Jill Aramsky, Jeffrey, very beautiful handwriting, but I can't read it, Gomzuelo,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Olga Humphrey and Carolina Roberts.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'll read them again.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Carolina Roberts, Olga Humphrey, Jeffrey Gonzuelo, Jill Ramski, and Ahmed Sabir.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Leo Adja.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Leo.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And again, when you start, just identify yourself so we can check it off.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Whoever.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, turn the button to begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Check if my name is there, Joseph, please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
