utterances.text: First, I want to offer my deepest sympathy to the Mahajan family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When I was in college, I wanted to free the carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I became a journalist and was obsessed with truth, facts, and context.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I learned to research everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: My view of the carriage horses changed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: When I first heard about night class, I researched.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I wonder why they posted a picture of an emaciated horse claiming it was a New York City carriage horse when it was a starving horse in India.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I was appalled by their hatred of the, quote, fat tourists who the poor horses, quote, had to struggle to pull around.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Even night classes veterinarian consultant was a small animal vet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Their entire history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Why was anyone listening to them?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Their entire history continues to be littered with manipulating context and flat-out lying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I have been greatly disappointed that many council members are not doing independent research on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They are using night class.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: talking points instead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They have called the conditions the horses work under abusive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But here's the truth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The horses work under protective laws the council established.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The rules about temperatures, hours, stall sizes, and more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The horses are also monitored by the police and mounted patrol.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Ryder's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Ryder did not die on the streets of New York, as Linda Rosenthal said.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: He was humanely euthanized at his new home outside of the city when he was found to have cancer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Ryder's owner was found not guilty of cruelty by a New York jury.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Why was it called Ryder's Law for so long?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Because the truth was not convenient for Nye class.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Now the false claims are that the carriage horses are a public safety hazard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Carriage rides started over 150 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The recent death was the first of its kind.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So if my math is right, that means that century after century, the carriage horses have had a better safety record than the vehicles that they share the park roads with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I am in the park all the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'd rather step in front of a carriage horse than an e-bike.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Anytime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A ban will create a horrible domino effect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If you want to give a rare sanctuary spot to a carriage horse that already has a good home instead of a horse who is going to slaughter, you don't really care about horses at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that's the truth.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can the last person on the panel identify yourself just so that we have the right list?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No clapping, please.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, she didn't sign up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Not her, you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What's your name?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Carolina.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Carolina.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to start on this end and then continue on the panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please start.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you turn on the microphone?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It appears I'm speaking to a nearly empty room at this point after I waited all day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The speaker and other council members that signed on to this bill apparently just have a fix in because they don't care to hear from many of us that had to speak and sign on to the bill before we spoke.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But I will read my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: What happened to Ramange was a terrible tragedy that broke so many.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But it wasn't my fault, and it wasn't anyone here's fault.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It was a horrible accident, and though the first of its kind in history, already resulted in changes to further improve the safety of this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: An accident, like all the e-bike and car accidents every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: To take the job, livelihood, and beloved animals from everyone in the entire industry doesn't give anyone justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It causes even greater heartbreak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No one should be a voice for horses other than those of us who know them best.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I won't have time to debunk every claim made by the people against this industry, but as someone who reads beyond the headlines and actually understands horses, I can tell you with confidence there are

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No systemic reoccurring issues within this industry and no abuse beyond the harassment carriage drivers receive from people who have dehumanized them because they misunderstand horses and have ideologies that shouldn't dictate legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I see the faces of people who have taunted and obsessively stalked me for years in this room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But because I have so much pride in what I do, and it means so much to me, I find the strength to put up with it, to do what I love.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I left the office world to pursue this career, because happiness is more important than money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I am a horse person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: My two horses are very bonded to me, and over the nine and a half years I have driven in this industry, I have taken great care of them and abided by the city's administrative code.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I have no plans to separate from my horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But this bill gives me no financial aid for their long-term care, while simultaneously stopping me from providing them with the work they enjoy that keeps them healthy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I don't want a different job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I chose this one, and it's my passion in life where I belong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I've always called New York City my home, and it's what my horses call home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I find it very offensive that anyone would suggest we're no longer welcome here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I ask the council to do the right thing and avoid the guilt of ruining families and horses'lives and oppose this awful bill, as every previous administration has done every time it's reintroduced.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I expect to be back here for a hearing on Intro 937, which is the solution to protecting this iconic industry while ensuring its safety without...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Anyone else having to lose what means?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have to keep it to two minutes because we have hundreds of people.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have hundreds of people still signed up, so we have to give everyone ample time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin to identify yourself.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Leti Gomez Coelho, a veterinarian from Ecuador and carriage driver for two years, testifies about the excellent care horses receive, proposes a stable with pastures inside Central Park, and argues against the ban.
chapters.title: Leti Gomez Coelho (carriage driver/vet) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: My name is Leti Gomez Coelho.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: I graduated as a veterinarian in Ecuador, and for the past two years, I proudly worked as a horse carriage driver here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Today, I'm not here to speak about my human family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: I'm here to speak for my equine family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: I heard that one proposal is to send our horses to sanctuaries, but every day I see sanctuaries asking for donations just to feed the animals they already have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Is that really the better future for our horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Our horses receive excellent care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: They are examined by veterinarians twice a year, vaccinated, dewormed, received regular dental and hoof care, eat premium quality hay and grain, and enjoy fresh carrots every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: They don't depend on charity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: They live with dignity because we work together, bringing joy to thousands of visitors while they're earning our honest living.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: We know and love our horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: If I mention Sophia, Handsome Jack, Bella, Cesar, or Blackie, every

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Every carriage driver knows exactly who they are, their personality, what they are afraid of, if they prefer carrots or apples.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: We know them and we love them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: I'm not saying our industry is perfect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: There is always room for improvement and we are willing to improve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: I also believe there is a better solution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: It is a personal thought.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: A stable with pastures inside Central Park, it will eliminate unnecessary travel through city streets,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: allow the public to see how well our horses are cared for, I'm talking about locals and visitors, preserve our jobs, and we already

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: pay rent in our stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: We can keep paying rent and support the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: So please don't let New York lose this historic tradition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leti Gomez Coelho
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Carolina Roberts, an actress and animal rights advocate from Mexico City, testifies about documenting carriage horses in Central Park, witnessing their poor conditions, and experiencing harassment from a carriage driver for advocating.
chapters.title: Carolina Roberts (animal rights advocate) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of the Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: My name is Carolina Roberts, also known as Rocky in the animal rights community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I'm an actress and I'm an animal rights advocate and I traveled here from Mexico City because I believe these horses deserve a voice and should not suffer for human entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: For years, I have documented New York City's carriage horses while speaking up for many other animals who cannot speak for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Like the bulls in Mexico who are victims of the bullfighting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: New York has always led by example.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Humane alternatives already exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: We don't need horse-drawn carriages to welcome visitors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I also recognize that the men and women who work in this industry deserve respect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: This is not about punishing workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: It's about creating a safer, more humane future for both horses and people through a transition of modern alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I have spent countless hours in Central Park observing these horses, photographing them and filming them, speaking with tourists and witnessing this industry firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I have stood beside New York City's carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I have seen them standing for hours on hard pavement, surrounded by traffic, noise, heat, and crowds, breathing exhaust fumes while pulling heavy carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: These horses never chose this life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Ramanj Mahajan should still be alive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: His tragic death reminds us that this industry not only endangers horses, but put human lives at risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I have also experienced harassment while peacefully advocating for these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: My friends and I

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: have been physically confronted by a violent carriage man who left his horse unattended, simply for expressing concern for their welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Today, you have the opportunity to protect these horses and help move New York toward a safer, more compassionate future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: I respectfully ask you to pass Romancia's law and bring an end to horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: And please send these horses to a sanctuary where they belong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Muchisimas gracias.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: To this panel, you're dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The next panel is Helen David, Mary Irby.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Herbie.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Allison Bowling.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Jim Keene, Mel Solowski, Susan Wagner.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Jamie Baldanza.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you also fill out a slip, please?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: He can join the panel when he gets here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry, can you fill out another one?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin and identify yourself for the record, whoever wants to begin.

chapters.summary: Helen David, a pedicab driver for 23 years, testifies about witnessing the industry from inside the stables, says some carriage drivers were former pedicab drivers who could return, and disputes the claim that Denise died from a poisonous plant rather than exhaustion.
chapters.title: Helen David (pedicab driver) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: All right, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I'm Helen David, and I'm a tour guide in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I'm a pedicab driver for 23 years in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I started in 2004, June.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: And when I initially started, I used to park my pedicab in a stable on 52nd Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I've seen countless of things that I wasn't supposed to see as a pedicab driver in a horse stable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: This is back in 06, 07.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: And now working in Central Park alongside some of these horse and carriage drivers behind me,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: I just want to make it clear to the council that some of those guys were pedicab drivers, so if they need to be transitioned to another job, they can always go back to the pedicab, because that's where we started off as pedicab drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: And it's safer in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: And also another thing I want to make clear, the day Dennis'horse died, that horse was out for a long time, for more than what it's supposed to be, because 5 o'clock is when they're supposed to change the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: shift for the horses to go back to the stable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: But the incident happened way after Dennis was supposed to be at a stable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I work alongside the horse and carriage driver, so I see it every single day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: I'm in Central Park sometimes 6.30 in the morning, and I'm there up until 8 o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So I see in and out of what is going on in Central Park with the horse and carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: I'm not here to bash anybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: I'm here to speak of the truth, and I don't want anybody to go out there and try to blame the Central Park Conservancy that the horse, you know, ate a poisonous plant when it was actually exhaustion because I was there the day Dennis died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: I know what time that horse came in, and then when the horses passed away, I was home because that horse was supposed to have left the park before I did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: So therefore, I'm just here to let you guys know that we are out here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen David
utterances.text: The pedicab industry is represented, and if we are to save the city and our tourists in New York City, we have to do the right thing by the city, by the people that live in New York City, and then the horse and carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may continue.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You, sir.

chapters.summary: Dr. Jim Keene, a veterinarian with 38 years of experience, testifies about evaluating the carriage industry firsthand, concluding horses should be phased out, and referencing Dr. Kulikowski's findings of musculoskeletal injuries and dental disease.
chapters.title: Dr. Jim Keene (veterinarian) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of the Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak in support of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Jim Keem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: I'm a veterinarian from South Dakota with 38 years of experience as a large animal vet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: I'm Director of Veterinary Science for the Center of Humane Economy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: I practiced, conducted USDA research, taught at three veterinary schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Four years ago, I came to New York City to evaluate the carriage industry firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: I visited the stables, observed the horses at work in Central Park and on the highways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: I spoke with both drivers and opponents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: My conclusion was clear then and even clear today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Carriage horses should be phased out in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: As others have said, horses are prey animals whose instinctive response to perceived danger is to flee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: That instinct cannot be trained away or regulated away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: In one of the world's busiest urban environments with constant traffic, sirens, construction, crowds, heat, hard pavement, runaways, and serious accidents are not anomalies that are predictable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Horses evolved to graze, roam on soft ground, and live socially in herds, not to pull heavy carriages throughout congested streets and spend their nights confined alone in small stalls without turnout.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: In December 2025, independent equine vet Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Craig Kowalski, who testified earlier today, examined more than 12 licensed carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: As he mentioned, they had multiple recurring musculoskeletal injuries, tendon and ligament disease, harness sores, and dental disease.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: He concluded that several horses could not be certified fit for work because the owners refused standard trotting exams for lameness and blood work needed to detect possible pain-masking meds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: Finally, this is also a public safety issue of the tragic death of Ramanche, together with

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: the numerous carriage horse accidents over the years that have been mentioned today, demonstrate the risk of placing a frightened 1,000- and 2,000-pound prey animal in the middle of Manhattan traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jim Keene
utterances.text: For the welfare of these horses and the safety of the public, I respectfully urge you to pass Romax Law.

chapters.summary: Susan Wagner, president of Equine Advocates, testifies about her sanctuary's three former NYC carriage horses, all broken down and crippled, and documents 529 horses disappearing from city rolls over 7.5 years, presumed dead or slaughtered.
chapters.title: Susan Wagner (Equine Advocates) testifies on disappeared horses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Thank you for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: I'm Susan Wagner, president of Equine Advocates, the nonprofit equine protection organization I founded 30 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: We operate from a 173-acre horse sanctuary in Chatham, New York, home to 82 horses, ponies, donkeys, and mules, which included three former New York City carriage horses,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: all of whom were left broken down and crippled as a direct result of the cruel and inhumane work they were forced to do and unnatural lives they were forced to lead pulling carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: The most famous of the three was the late Bobby Two Freedom, who would have died in a Canadian slaughterhouse if he hadn't been rescued.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Bobby's story proved New York City carriage horses are sent to slaughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Bobby was shipped from a PA slaughter auction to Rhinebeck Equine, where his discharge report stated that he was overloaded with worms, was head-nodding lame, and underweight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Later at Cornell, he was diagnosed with chronic navicular bone disease and fractures from old injuries in both his front legs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Jack, who has been with us since 2016, was diagnosed with navicular disease, evidence of an old bone fracture, and bilateral forelimb osteoarthritis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Sheena, who passed in 2023, was anemic, suffered from left limb lameness and hoof ligament disease.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: A study by the Coalition to Ban Horse-Drawn Carriages from information from the health department documented that an average of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: 71 New York City carriage horses disappeared off the rolls every year over a seven and a half year period for a total of 529 horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Where did they go?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: We believe presumed slaughter, presumed dead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: The Mandami administration says it is progressive, a positive word indicating improvement and moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: In keeping with that progressive platform, I urge the new mayor and the current city council to allow New York City to finally join the number of growing cities globally and in the United States to ban horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Wagner
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Mel Sobolewski, a large animal emergency responder, testifies that no reform can make the industry safe because the risk is a horse's involuntary flight response, and argues hitching posts add new layers of danger.
chapters.title: Mel Sobolewski (emergency responder) testifies on flight response and hitching post dangers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: My name is Mel Sobolewski.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I am a large animal emergency responder with a decade of experience rescuing horses who've been trapped in fires.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I am also a founding member of the Coalition for Responsible Carriage Horse Retirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I urge you to support Intro 943 and pass Roemont's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: In my work, the goal is the same.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Identify the hazard, understand the failure points, and act before people or animals are hurt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I am not here evaluating one isolated incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I am examining a pattern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: No reform can make this industry safe because the inherent risk is not a gap in regulation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: It is a horse's involuntary flight response.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: A horse's body is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: designed for sudden explosive acceleration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: It is the very reason horsepower became the standard metric for engines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: When a horse perceives a threat, the fear response fires and before the horse's brain has time to process what is happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: This is a reflex.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Good training can build a horse's tolerance, but even the best trainers cannot erase survival instincts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: The union's proposed solution is to install hitching posts throughout Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Hitching posts will not remove a horse's flight instinct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: They add new layers of risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Creating dangerous conditions on public property that would be approved, installed, and owned by the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: If a horse is hard-tied with no chance of breaking away, the restraint itself becomes the danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: This is a known, predictable, and documented behavior.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: A panicked horse that cannot flee will continue to escalate its fear response, increasing the risk of catastrophic injury to anyone within the vicinity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: If a horse is tied with a quick-release knot and can break away, it brings us to where we are today, mourning the unnecessary loss of Romange Mahijan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Additionally, I would just like to add that horses are the only grazing animal in Central Park, which is why Japanese yew is

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: an issue for them and not for other animals because a horse is a herbivore, a squirrel, a dog are carnivores.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: So while this plant may be toxic to species in general, how much they consume and what part of the plant needs to be taken into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: And the toxicology report on Dennis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: We got it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mel Sobolewski
utterances.text: I think I made it.

chapters.summary: Allison Bolling of Red Feather Equine Sanctuary testifies about a coalition of 30+ sanctuaries prepared to accept carriage horses, with attorneys developing lifelong placement agreements, and notes a horse was allegedly sold through a slaughter auction just last week.
chapters.title: Allison Bolling (Red Feather Sanctuary) testifies on coalition readiness

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: My name is Allison Bolling, and I am the co-founder and executive director of Red Feather Equine Sanctuary and one of the founding members of the Coalition for Responsible Heritage Horse Retirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: I'm here today to ask you for your support of Ramont's Law, Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: There's been a lot of conjecture, so I'm going to just cut right to it, and that is what will happen with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: You have heard repeated claims today that if Ramont's Law passes, carriage horses will be abandoned, they'll be sent to auction, and they'll be slaughtered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Those are very serious claims and we agree that they should be taken seriously.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: In fact, concern for these horses'future is exactly why our coalition was formed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: The reality is that horses can be sent to auction or slaughter under the status quo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Incidentally, we received a report from a partner rescue that a horse allegedly being described by the auctioneer as a New York City carriage horse was sold through the notorious New Holland, Pennsylvania auction just last week.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: New Holland is, of course, a feeder sale into the Amish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: and slaughter pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We have organized to help ensure that that does not happen here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: As you heard in the testimonies of our colleagues, our coalition is made up of now more than 30 nationally recognized equine rescue and sanctuary organizations with cumulatively hundreds of years of experience protecting vulnerable horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Preventing horses from entering the slaughter pipeline is not a new responsibility for us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: That is our mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: That is why we exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: And this is not just simply a promise to, quote, find homes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: And importantly, I want to state that we're not here to forcibly take away anyone's horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We are offering a coordinated retirement strategy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We have assembled a team to evaluate each horse individually, to coordinate placements, to maintain transparency, and to work collaboratively with all stakeholders throughout the entire transition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We have attorneys prepared to develop lifelong placement agreements with oversight and follow-up protocols designed to help protect every horse long after it leaves New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We stand before you today and publicly commit to working with every carriage owner who wants a safe retirement for their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We have created that opportunity and we are prepared to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Jamie Baldanza, documentarian and sanctuary founder, testifies about her personal experience being thrown from a spooked horse in NYC, describes trigger stacking, and criticizes Councilmember Gennaro's lack of equine behavior knowledge.
chapters.title: Jamie Baldanza (documentarian) testifies on spooking experience

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Jamie Baldanza.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: I'm a documentarian focused on wild horses and equine welfare, founder of a New Jersey horse sanctuary, and co-founder of the Coalition for Responsible Carriage Horse Retirement, and a former New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: I urge the council to pass Romantia's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: The hardest lesson horses have ever taught me is that just because you love them doesn't mean you understand them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: When I was 18, I moved to New York and rode often at Claremont Stables near Central Park and a stable in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Riding the same streets as the carriage horses, I understood why so many people saw them as part of the city's magic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: I loved that magic too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Then I started to see the other side, the small stalls, little freedom to move, limited social contact, and lives shaped around the demands of the city instead of the needs of a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: But what stayed with me the most happened at the park entrance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: One day, the horse I was riding spooked at an umbrella.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: He bolted and I was thrown.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Lying there with serious injuries, my first thought was not about myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: It was about if Bingo reached traffic, he or someone else could get seriously injured or die.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Despite people pleading with me not to move, I got up and ran after him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: That moment changed the course of my life, and it led me to spend years rescuing horses and studying their behavior firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: And as I evolved as a horsewoman and gained better understanding,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: of their behavior, I should have recognized the signs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Bingo was already uneasy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: It was a classic example of trigger stacking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: His stress has been building long before the umbrella.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: By then, he had already absorbed the sirens, crowds, traffic, and constant stimulation of New York City until he reached his threshold.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: And for Council Member Gennaro, who's no longer here, and as a self-proclaimed equestrian for 60 years, I am shocked he doesn't know basic equine behavior.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: He certainly has had the time to learn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Please pass Ramach's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jamie Baldanza
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have the gentleman in the end here.

chapters.summary: Marty Irby, an eight-time world champion equestrian, testifies about horses' flight instincts, notes the military no longer uses horses, and asks why a major city should use them for entertainment.
chapters.title: Marty Irby (champion equestrian) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Yes, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of the Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: My name is Marty Irby.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: I'm an eight-time world champion equestrian, a lifelong horseman, and past president of two different equine breed registries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: An advocate who has spent many years working to protect American equines through state and federal policy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: I've devoted much of my life to this work and ask that you help us see the end of horse-drawn carriages in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: They are flight animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Biology and biomechanics are often overlooked on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: When they become frightened, they don't stop and reason through danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: They run.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: That instinct has allowed horses and other equines to survive for tens of thousands of years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Their natural instincts cannot be trained away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: It can only be managed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Yet every day these horses are expected to navigate one of the busiest, loudest, most unpredictable

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: urban environments in the world, surrounded by taxis, buses, construction, emergency sirens, crowds, bicycles, and constant traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Even the calmest horse can be startled by something beyond anyone's control.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: And when that happens, the consequences can be devastating for the horse, the driver, the passengers, and the public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: This isn't about questioning the dedication of carriage drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Many care deeply for their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: We've heard that and we've seen that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: This is about recognizing that New York City has changed and both our dedication and understanding of animal welfare during this modern day era is quite different than it was several hundred years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Many other major cities have eliminated the use of horse-drawn carriages and replaced them with more modern transportation and entertainment like electric carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Humans have forced those that were born to run free across open fields to spend their lives standing in traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: That is not progress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: It is a compromise that we no longer have to make.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Freedom applies to more than just humans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: We all owe a debt of gratitude to the horses, and they should be respected because America was built on their backs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: I just want to add the picture of President Washington up there today and his horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: We don't utilize horses in our military, and we don't utilize them in war today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Why should we use them in a major city in this country?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marty Irby
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Hanif has a question for the panel.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks sanctuary representatives about capacity concerns raised by the industry. Bolling acknowledges finite capacity but notes the coalition represents three dozen sanctuaries nationwide and describes their enrichment programs.
chapters.title: Hanif questions sanctuary representatives on capacity

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: For the folks affiliated with the sanctuaries, I wanted to hear if you could respond to the concern that was shared here.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: That sanctuaries don't have the capacity or that sanctuaries are not doing what we think will be the new lifestyle of horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Well, that's a loaded question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: It's a good question, and I acknowledge that sanctuary does have finite capacity, and that's certainly a concern.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So if you could maybe share how many folks work at the sanctuary and walk me through what a day with our coalition represents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: Our coalition represents almost three dozen different sanctuaries across the country, so I could speak for my organization singularly, but when we're talking about capacity, we're thinking about dozens of different organizations nationwide.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And then, yeah, I'd love to hear just what's happening at the sanctuary you're affiliated with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: So I'm, again, co-founder of Red Feather Equine Sanctuary, and our core belief is that horses are not inherently defined by the work that they can do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: But that means for us that when they come through our doors that we treat them as we would treat a horse who could do that work, which means that they do actually get enrichment training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We have a trainer that comes to our facility once a week and does groundwork with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We trailer them to her facility once a week so that they can maintain freshness on their trailering skills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: We participate in equine-assisted learning and equine-assisted therapy activities with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: So they're not just being thrown in a field.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Bolling
utterances.text: are utilized, we just have learned to respect them for what their physical limits are and we have a different relationship with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I appreciate the testimony from this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, next panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Waller.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Walker, I'm sorry, my mistake.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Walker, blank.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Blackish ship?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Something like that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Clara Bright.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tommy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Hughes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Abderham Sanganoff.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I think the last name is...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Follero or Vallone is the last name?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I think the first name is Giovanni.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Giovanni, I think.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Natalie Honick.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's it?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I don't know who's who.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can start.

chapters.summary: Clara Bright testifies opposing the ban and supporting Intro 937, arguing horse carriages are part of Central Park's original design, that relocation doesn't guarantee better care, and that improvement comes through better standards not bans.
chapters.title: Clara Bright testifies opposing ban, supports reform bill

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and Honorable Members of the Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to- Identify yourself first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Oh, Clara Bright.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: My name is Clara Bright, and I respectfully submit this testimony in opposition to Intro 943 and in support of Intro 937.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: I respectfully oppose Intro 943 and support Intro 937 because the solution is not elimination, it's improvement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: A nation that forgets its past has no future, widely attributed to Winston Churchill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carolina Roberts
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Since 1858, horse-drawn carriages have been part of Central Park's history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: As the Central Park Conservancy explains, Friedrich Law, Amstetten, and Calvert Vox designed the park's carriage drive specifically for scenic

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: scenic travel by horse-drawn carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: These horses are part of its original design and living heritage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: If there are concerns, let us solve them, not eliminate a tradition that has served New York City for nearly 107 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: If welfare is the goal, continue strengthening oversight, improve infrastructure, install additional hitching posts, and pursue the development of modern stables in Central Park North.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Some argue these horses would be better off in sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: However, relocation does not automatically guarantee a better life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: These horses have been bred and trained over generations to work alongside humans and receive individualized daily care, specialized nutrition, and professional farrier, and every four to five weeks they do need new shoes

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: and new trimming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: It's called hoofy cure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Not every sanctuary has the resources to provide the same level of individualized daily care that many working carriage horses currently receive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Motor vehicle crashes occur every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: When these incidents occur, we investigate them.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We need you to summarize.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Yes, I have just two more paragraphs.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, not two more paragraphs.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Why is the standard different here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Why these horses and why this industry?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Improvement comes through better standards, not bans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: The welfare of these horses deserves evidence-based decisions based on facts, not fictional narratives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: I'll leave you with one final question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Bright
utterances.text: Is banning truly protecting animal welfare, or is improving their care the better path?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you for allowing me to speak.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And you can submit what you have, so.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's next?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: My name's Frank Ricobono.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: So yeah, my name is Francesco Ricobono.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: My family has proudly been a part of the New York City horse-drawn carriage industry since 1979.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: 47 years ago, my father came to America.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Wait, sir.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Were you, was you?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Your name called to come up?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I submitted my tickets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: No, your name was not called yet.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're going by panel, so you'll have to wait.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tommy Hughes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Well, Tommy's not here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are you speaking on his behalf?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I can.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I submitted my ticket.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I was one of the first.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But we're working in panels now, so we didn't call your name yet.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So we can't have you testify right this second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Because there's a method that we're using here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So what?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So who's?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Abdurahim Sanginov, nephew of a carriage owner, testifies about his uncle funding his Rutgers education through the carriage business, describes families and children who love the horses, and asks the council to oppose the bill.
chapters.title: Abdurahim Sanginov (carriage owner's nephew) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Good afternoon, members of the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: My name is Abdurahim Sanginov, and today I'm representing my uncle, named John Sanginov, who has been a proud horse carriage owner in New York City for the last six years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: So two years ago, I received a letter from the Rutgers University that I got accepted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: But there was an obstacle in my way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: My family couldn't provide the funds for my education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: And my dream was looking like it flying away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: And at the moment of darkness, my uncle said that he would do anything to put me in the school and help me achieve my dream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: And there was a question before, how about American Dream?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: I have American Dream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: I want to go to college and study, and my uncle is helping me to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Currently, he has two beautiful horses, Bella and Champion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Every day he is telling me that tens of hundreds of people stop by his horse to take pictures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: New Yorkers love horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: A lot of children come to Central Park to take pictures with his horses, to look at them, to pet them, and to play with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: This is the picture of the champion, beautiful white horse that he loves and cares about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: He's telling me a story.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: He told me to share the story with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: There is a lady, the resident of the New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You don't have that much time left, so you're going to have to either wrap it up, but go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If the story is long, I can't do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: So the story about the lady, the resident of the New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Every day, she knows the schedule of the Bella, of another horse of my uncle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: She brings her children to Central Park, and her children pray with Bella.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: You told about the people who don't want the horses in Central Park, but how about the voices of the people who want them in Central Park?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: There are tons of hundreds of people who want to see their horses in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: They are loved and taken care about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: All of these horse owners that you see, all of them love their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Their horses are beautiful creatures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: All of them are like family members to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: And today, I ask you to oppose the bill that would break a lot of families and would put a lot of people in trouble.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Abdurahim Sanginov
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We'll go back to you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go for it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You'll start over.

chapters.summary: Francesco Ricobono, whose family has been in the industry since 1979, owns 13 horses and three carriages, testifies about spending $40,000/year per horse on care, offers condolences to the Mahajan family, and asks the council to oppose the ban and work together on reforms.
chapters.title: Francesco Ricobono (carriage owner) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Okay, good afternoon, Chair, members of City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: My name is Francesco Ricobono.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: has probably been a part of New York City's horse-drawn carriage industry since 1979.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Today, I own three carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I care and own for 13 horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: And I also am one of the founders of 501c6, a nonprofit organization for the NYC Horse Carriage Alliance, which is devoted to enhancing safety and horse welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Okay, 47 years ago, my father came to America from Sicily with nothing but a dream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: He found that dream in New York City's horse-drawn carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Growing up, I didn't watch my father work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I watched him dedicate his life to caring for his horses with love, patience, and pride.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Those horses shaped my childhood and inspired my future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: They are not equipment or machines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: They are my family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Before I continue, I want to offer my heartfelt condolences to the family of Romance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: His tragic passing was heartbreaking and the entire industry continues to mourn alongside his loved ones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Our thoughts and prayers remain with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: That tragedy has only strengthened our commitment to improving safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: We are actively working with the TWU, the NYC Horse Carriage Alliance, and members of the council to implement stronger driving training certification programs, enhanced horse welfare standards, and so on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Because protecting both people and horses has always been our highest priority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: That is why I respectfully ask you to oppose Intro 0943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: This bill does not solve a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: It destroys an industry that has been a part of New York City's identity for 168 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: It threatens the futures of hardworking families, stable workers, various drivers, countless others whose livelihoods depend on this profession.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: It also jeopardizes the futures of the horses we have devoted our lives to caring for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I personally spend approximately $40,000 a year per horse because their health and well-being comes first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I am a newlywed father.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: I have two beautiful girls at home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: The industry is how I support my family, just as it supported mine as a child.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Like so many others, I have dedicated my life, my savings, and my future to this profession because I truly believe in this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: And I'll just wrap up one more sentence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Changes are needed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Let's work together to make them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Please don't erase generations of honest, hardworking New Yorkers who have devoted their lives to the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Real progress comes from collaboration, compassion, and thoughtful solutions, not from dismantling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: An entire community.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No clapping in the chambers.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tommy Hughes?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Not here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, he's not here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I guess I could speak for Tommy Hughes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I take his time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: And so my name is Joseph Kernigliaro.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Joseph?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Yeah, just find me there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I asked you before.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Did you sign up?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: You should have talked to me before you even come to bring a bill up because I am the original owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: My father was the owner of two stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I'm surprised that you guys don't even consult us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I'm a lawyer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I'm a politician.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I'm a radio announcer, TV announcer, a host, and you don't contact me, and you're just doing this today?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: It's not about the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: It's about the property.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: When I started this business 52 years ago, there was prostitute pimps and drugs on 38th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Jacob Javits did not even exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: They came afterwards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Now everybody is a slave over here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: These people are working for the New York class.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Steve Nislick, that's the people that they're working for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: And you guys are part of the conspiracy if you allow this moron to take you for a ride.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: He's paying politicians.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: He's paying everybody else.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: He is cited for violation of the election law, and therefore he should be reprimanded, not rewarded.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: That's what's going on right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: You guys are rewarding this guy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: You're working for him.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have to maintain decorum, so lower your voice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: You've got to understand that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: You know, as a lawyer, as a politician,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: I do like to speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: And you did not consult me at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Two, we have two stables, okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: And that's why I think, you know, wait a second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No.
