utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Because I asked you to maintain decorum and you wouldn't, so.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's fine.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's Valone?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Or is that you?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry, sir.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Guys, enough.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sir, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Giovanni Vallone, second-generation carriage owner, testifies about his 37 years in the business, his father's 65 years, and organizing a Columbus Day parade with Sicilian carts. He asks the council not to end their business.
chapters.title: Giovanni Vallone (carriage owner) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: My name is Giovanni Vallone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: I'm the second generation of a family of carriage owners and drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: My father started 65 years ago, and I continue his business with the happiness and with the principles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: I was raised to respect my horse and respect the customers and see all my customers happy getting out from the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: I know about horses a lot because it's 37 years I'm here in this business and I'm the last member of the family with my cousin actually that's 30 years in this business and we also always respect everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: We try to respect all the rules and follow all the rules, respect all the people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: And also another thing I want to say, three years ago, I was a promoter of a parade for the Columbus Day with seven Sicilian carts and seven New York horses that did a really great job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: job and we were a lot of cities, big cities in the United States was jealous than what we did in New York because it was a big thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: Seven horses from New York horses and seven Sicilian cars that come all the way from Bagheria that if you check is the base where they always do the Sicilian cars.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: I don't know if you never heard about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: I also want to ask you to please sell our business or try to help us, you know, after 65 years for a family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Giovanni Vallone
utterances.text: It's a little bit kind of hard.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Natalie?

chapters.summary: Natalie Ricobono, spouse of a carriage owner, testifies about her husband's dedication, his non-profit association for safety improvements, and asks the council not to put her family on the streets.
chapters.title: Natalie Ricobono (carriage owner's spouse) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair, and thank you for this opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: My name is Natalie Ricobono, and I am the spouse of a horse and carriage owner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: I want to offer first my deepest condolences to the Mahajan family as we too are grieving this tragic incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: And as a mother of two daughters, I am feeling the pain as we sit here today, and I'm so sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: In all the years of knowing my husband and my father-in-law, I've witnessed two hardworking and dedicated men striving to provide for our family and their colleagues alike.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: My husband has always put his horses before anything else and works on his days off to check on his horses, and I continue to witness my husband's passion in this business like no other.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: He started a not-for-profit association to improve communication with drivers and owners alike, holding meetings every Monday, which include conversations of horse safety, training, and improving opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: E-bikes, school buses, subway trains, et cetera, et cetera, all have accidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: So should we ban them all or only allow pedestrians to walk on city streets?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: We have been blessed with the opportunity to live the American dream because of this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: I beg you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: Please do not put my family on the streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: My husband's profession, along with his drivers, with all the horses he has dedicated his whole life to, let's look for other opportunities that will benefit all sides and for the future for all involved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Natalie Ricobono
utterances.text: Thank you for hearing me out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I just want to address something very quickly that the previous gentleman talked about in terms of consulting.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's what today is about.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're consulting with the public.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're consulting with all sides.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We started at 10 o'clock this morning.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We expect to go very late into this evening so we can hear what everybody has to say.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So that's how we're doing the consulting on this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I just want to make that point.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Francesco Ricobono
utterances.text: Oh, I could leave.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next panel, April Lang, I think it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Gabriel Schaefer.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Gabrick Schaefer, Grace Choi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tara Lauren.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Paola Gavino and Gabriel Stubert.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is everybody here that I called or not?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, it doesn't look like it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Allie Jones, Catalina Toro, Sully Voorhees,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And here's three more.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Let's see if we can find those names.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, why don't you guys start and just say your name and then, you know, your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Microphone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm the founder and executive director of Tamerlane Sanctuary and Preserve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We are the only farm sanctuary in the state of New Jersey that is accredited by the Global Federation of Farm Sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I was surprised to hear testimony today from somebody else on one of the panels that they were almost killed by a horse in Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I also had that experience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I grew up riding horses in California.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I rode a Western saddle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I came to New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I took dressage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And also at Claremont Stables, I rented a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I went into the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The horse was spooked by a photographer, and that horse took off running.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I was terrified that I was going to die or that I was going to kill somebody else on this horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I managed to jump off the horse in front of the horse to stop the horse from running.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: and the horse reared up, could have hit me on the head.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I grabbed the reins and I was able to walk that horse back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But that's just two people at Claremont Stables in this room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: How many other people did that happen to?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Horses are not meant to be in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The thing that I'm concerned about here is that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The retirement of the carriage horses only matters if it's truly permanent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: My fear is that these horses will be retired into places that are not true sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So I'm glad that there are people on this panel who are here to oversee that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: A horse should not leave a carriage and disappear into a system where it can be sold, transferred, auctioned, or eventually end up in the slaughter pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Retirement should mean lifelong protection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And just because a horse is trained to work does not mean that the horse wants to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I grew up riding stable horses and all they did was try to get you off of their back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: They did not enjoy the experience of being ridden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If these horses are retired, they deserve placement in legitimate, accredited sanctuaries that exist solely for the welfare of the animals, not facilities where ownership can change or where horses may once again be commodities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are dozens of GFAs.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, you just need to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Accredited sanctuaries across the country and I myself have been involved in rescues of thousands of animals where sanctuaries across the country have absorbed them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: So they're

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: are homes for these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There are good sanctuaries for them to go to.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just state your name first, please.

chapters.summary: Paola Gaviño, board member of Tamerlan Sanctuary and Hispanic immigrant, testifies in Spanish and English that immigrant workers deserve better, safer jobs with protections, and that we don't have to choose between protecting animals or workers.
chapters.title: Paola Gaviño (Tamerlan Sanctuary) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: My name is Paola Gaviño, and I'm also here as a board member of Tamerlan Sanctuary, where we take care of rescue animals, including horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: But I'm also here as an Hispanic immigrant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: So I'm going to speak Spanish a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: I will translate because my testimony is somehow connected to the experience as an immigrant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: So, as an immigrant Latina, I understand perfectly what it means to come to this country, looking for new opportunities, working hard to have a better life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: But precisely because I understand that, I think all the immigrant workers from all the countries deserve something better.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Jobs that are safe, worthy, with a stable future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Muchos de estos trabajadores de carruajes no tienen protecciones básicas, no tienen un salario garantizado, beneficio, seguro de desempleo, licencia por enfermedad o compensación si se lesiona.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Son clasificados solo como contratistas, no como empleados protegidos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Esta transición propuesta ofrece exactamente eso, trabajos protegidos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: No tenemos que escoger entre proteger a los animales o a los trabajadores inmigrantes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Tenemos y debemos que proteger a todos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Les pido que lo apoyen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Como a la inmigrante latina,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: I don't know how, I know exactly how it's like coming to this country and look for better opportunities, working hard to build a better life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: But precisely because of that, I believe all immigrant workers deserve better, safe jobs, stable futures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Many of these carriage horses don't have that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: So they don't have no guaranteed salary, benefits, employment, insurance, health care, workers'compensation if they get injured.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: They are classified as independent contractors rather than protected employees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: This transition proposed here offers them that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Jobs with real protection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: So at the sanctuary, okay, I'm gonna wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Horses are not machines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: They deserve to be retired and we'll have to choose between immigrant workers or protecting the animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: We should protect them both.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: So please pass from Ansh Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Paola Gaviño
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so now I'm going to recall some of the names I called before.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If they're not here, Gabriel Stubert.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, Gabriel, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Grace Choi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Grace Choi here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tara Lauren Clinney.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Marie Carlissimo.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Kirk Miller.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: April Lang.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry, what?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Which one?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's the last person?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Cindy Finch.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Cindy Finch?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry, what was your name again?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: April.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, April.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead, April.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

chapters.summary: April Lang, NYC resident and animal advocate, testifies that horses were necessary before the 20th century but no longer are, that regulations aren't followed, and that not all traditions deserve to continue.
chapters.title: April Lang (animal advocate) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Hi there, my name is April Lang and I'm a president of New York City and an animal advocate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: So this is just the facts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Horses were a necessary mode of transportation prior to the 20th century.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: They no longer are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Prior to the 20th century, horses were not breathing in the exhaust from cars, dodging traffic, nor in danger of colliding with automobiles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Horses do this quote-unquote work because they are forced to, as has always been the case, not out of choice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: In spite of what we think we know about horses, there is not one person who can say with absolute certainty that the horses are content pulling a 900 to 1500 pound carriage, which is then loaded with passengers,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: whether that be on a temperate day or through the heat, cold, snow, and rain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Note, regulations are not often followed, monitored, or enforced.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Due to a more highly evolved sense of smell, sight, and hearing, horses can and do get triggered by sight, sounds, and smells, which are imperceptible to humans, but which signal danger to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: This means they can and do bolt unexpectedly, putting themselves, the driver, and any passengers or pedestrians in harm's way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: While carriage drivers...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: need and deserve to work, like everyone, neither they nor anyone else can count on permanent job security.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Industries change, and as so as do consumer preferences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: All of us need to adapt to that reality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: If any visitors to New York end up missing the carriage rides, they'll get over it, because our great city has a plethora of things to see, activities to engage in, and opportunities to make lasting memories.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: The horses, however, will not get over the wear and tear of the bodies of this industry and genders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: And those humans injured or killed when horses bolt or collide with cars or pedestrians will not soon get over their losses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Not all traditions deserve to continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Traditions are created by people and can be changed by people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: And when a tradition harms an animal or a person, it must be ended.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: The horse-drawn carriage industry in New York is one tradition that needs to be put out to pasture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - April Lang
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now I'm going to call up John D.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Leonardo and Sam Moon, I think it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are they here?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just state your name for the record.

chapters.summary: Sam Moon, executive director of Catskill Animal Sanctuary, testifies about what horses need to thrive—space, predictable surroundings, natural behaviors—and that NYC streets cannot provide these conditions.
chapters.title: Sam Moon (Catskill Animal Sanctuary) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Sam Moon.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: To the chair and members of the City Council, thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: My name is Sam Moon and I am the executive director of Catskill Animal Sanctuary, located just two miles north, or two hours, excuse me, north of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: And on behalf of our organization, I am here to express our strong support for Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Every day we care for rescued horses and other farmed animals who have found safety, peace, and lifelong refuge at our sanctuary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: That experience has given us a deep understanding of what horses need to thrive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Space to move freely, predictable surroundings, and an environment where they can express their natural behaviors without the constant stress of traffic, noise, and congestion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: New York streets simply cannot provide those conditions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Horses are intelligent and sensitive animals, and even the calmest, best-trained horses, as you have heard today, can be startled by sirens, constructions, and crowds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: When this happens, the risk has extended beyond the horse to carriage operators, passengers, cyclists, and pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: As a sanctuary, we believe in...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Our responsibility is to provide animals with environments that provide their welfare rather than ask them to adapt to conditions that compromise it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: We can acknowledge the role horses have played in our history while embracing a future that does not require them to work in one of the busiest environments in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: For the welfare of our horses and the safety of our public, we respectfully urge the council to pass Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sam Moon
utterances.text: Thank you for your consideration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: John?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joseph Kernigliaro
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: All right, great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: My name is John DiLeonardo.

chapters.summary: John DiLeonardo of Humane Long Island testifies about the industry's refusal of independent vet exams, and reveals that Dr. Camillo Sierra, the industry's vet, has been sanctioned seven times by the NY State Gaming Commission for falsified health certificates and fraud.
chapters.title: John DiLeonardo (Humane Long Island) testifies on vet's disciplinary history

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: I am an anthrozoologist and the executive director of Humane Long Island, serving every borough and performing much of New York City's farm and exotic animal rescue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Romanch's law exists because the status quo has already claimed too many victims.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: We lost Ryder, we lost Denise, we lost 18-year-old Romanch Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: The question before the council is whether those tragedies will finally lead to meaningful change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Today, you've heard from many proponents of the carriage horse industry, and possibly still, you may hear from fellow Long Islander, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Camillo Sierra, urging you to trust that this industry can regulate itself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Before you do, I ask that you consider who is asking you for that trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: The carriage horse industry has repeatedly refused to allow its horses to be examined by independent veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Instead, it asks its council to rely on veterinarians like Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Sierra, who also serves as the NYPD's veterinarian.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Sierra has been disciplined repeatedly by New York State regulators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: The New York State Gaming Commission sanctioned him at least seven times and suspended him from the horse track twice in matters involving falsified health certificates, fraud, corruption, and administering medication to a racehorse he never examined.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: The New York State Board for Veterinary Medicine also found him guilty of professional misconduct for failing to properly document the examination and treatment of a horse, resulting in a two-year suspended stay of his veterinary license.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: When an industry refuses independent veterinary examinations while asking policymakers to rely on a veterinarian with that disciplinary history, transparency is absent and public confidence is impossible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Ramacha's law provides a responsible path forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: It phases out horse-drawn carriages, protects carriage horses by prohibiting their sale for slaughter or transfer to another carriage operation, and establishes a workforce development program to help affected workers transition to new employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: I respectfully urge you to pass Ramach's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John DiLeonardo
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you both very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, next panel, Nicole Grievey, Daniel

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Chaplis, I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Alberto Urzi, I-R or V, Urzi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And Lenore Quadrosi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nope.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'll read the list one more time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nicole Grievey, Danielle Marie Chaplis, Alberto Verzi, and Lenore Quadrosi.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So just state your name for the record and go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: Nicole Rivard from Friends of Animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: We were founded in New York in 1957.

chapters.summary: Nicole Grievey, a NYC resident and former horse owner, testifies opposing the ban, arguing real estate interests are behind it, that equine experts have found horses well cared for, and that the industry gives unwanted horses a chance at life.
chapters.title: Nicole Grievey testifies opposing ban, cites real estate motivations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: I will state my name for the record.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Are we good?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Nicole Grievey.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Like Groovy, but with E's instead of O's.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: I'm a New Yorker, I'm a horse lover, I'm a former horse owner, and I have watched the real estate interests try to kill the carriage industry for decades now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: At first they were honest about the reason, they want the land that the stables are on for development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: As that wasn't popular with average New Yorkers, they changed tactics, pouring a lot of money into pushing the idea that NYC carriage work is inhumane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: It's not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Equine experts who have inspected the horses and stables have said over and over that they are well cared for, protected by city regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Carriage work is not cruel, and it is a lifeline for Amish farm horses and ex-racetrack trotters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: There are horses in those Westside stables now who might have spent their last hours in terror, bound for a slaughterhouse, if not for the carriage industry taking them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: As someone who has rescued a horse myself when its stable in Queens abruptly closed, I know from experience how hard and how hard it is to get a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: how expensive it is to provide a home for unwanted horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: The carriage industry gives these unwanted horses a chance to live a long life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: This is the truth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: On top of that, it offers union jobs, it's work for immigrants, and it is a beautiful and irreplaceable part of the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: If this were truly about noise and pollution and crowds and horses as prey animals who spook at everything,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: if this were truly about that, the anti-carriage activists would also be protesting in front of the NYPD stables and bullying the mounted police, because those horses work much harder and in dirtier, much busier areas than the carriage horses do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: But they don't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Because the truth is, both police horses and carriage horses can live and work and thrive in a city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: But only the carriage horses stand between the real estate people making more money than they even need now anyway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: But this bill was never about what's best for horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: It's about rich people taking something that isn't theirs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: It's a message to the elite of the city they can take whatever they want, just spend enough money and spread enough propaganda.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you please summarize?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: My summary is that I'm asking you to pass Intro 937 instead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: The real estate people have gotten a few decades now lying about the carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: Please give the drivers a little time to show the truth of what they do before you take away their livelihoods and the lives of the horses they love and care for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Grievey
utterances.text: I thank you so much for your time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next person, just identify yourself and then...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Give your testimony.

chapters.summary: Leonor Quadrosi, an equine studies student, testifies opposing the ban, arguing carriage work is biomechanically easier than riding, the industry is heavily regulated, and the bill could lead to discontinuation of working horse breeds.
chapters.title: Leonor Quadrosi (equine studies student) testifies opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: Leonor Quadrosi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: So I am a student of equine studies program soon to graduate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: I have been working with horses for over 10 years and riding for almost 20.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: I am in opposition of this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: This is biomechanically an easier job for a horse to perform than that of being ridden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And this is one of the most regulated industries for horses anywhere in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: In fact, telling some of my professors and classmates, the amount of regulations that horses are made to follow here is one that actually shocks a lot of people because they don't believe that there are so many rules here that horses have to follow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And I strongly believe that the bill presented today is the first step in severing a human-animal bond that has existed for thousands of years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: These are domesticated horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: They are not ones that can easily just go into a pipeline of not working anymore.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And this will lead to the discontinuation of these certain breeds, such as drafts and standard breds, that were created to do this type of work, although it is much easier of the work they were originally created to do back when the industry began.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: This is a very painfully vague bill, and we don't have any guarantee that once these horses leave the city that they will be able to continue the proper care that they have been over the watchful eye of in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And, you know, while a lot of the people sitting here today mean well, once they are out,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: Out of their eyes, there is no way that we will be able to tell what's going on with these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And I have been a part of an independent study for my degree program, talking exactly about the social license to operate urban stables and had the task of laying out

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: exactly how to ensure good welfare for horses that exist inside the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: And it is possible, and not only possible, but the surrounding community can be a part of this as well, where not only do we have respect for the horses in the park, where we don't have e-bikes and other things running all around them, causing spooking accidents that have increased.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: I ask that this is not the bill that is supported, rather the other one that has been...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: Introduced by Councilman Gennaro, so that we do not go further away from the natural world than we already have, and that we remember that we do share this world with other species and not just ourselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: New York City does not have to only be for people, but can coexist with horses as well as the other animals that exist here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leonor Quadrosi
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you both.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So a couple of housekeeping things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is that if people, we have a ton more people to testify, so several hours worth of testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you want to submit testimony, you can.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You have up to 72 hours after the end of this hearing to do so, and you can do it at testimony.nyc, testimony at council, sorry, .nyc.gov, gov.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We look at every single, we've actually had people submit testimony already earlier this week, knowing that there was a hearing today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Every single piece of testimony is going to be looked at and reviewed and put into a report.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I just want to let folks know that's an option.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, also, if there is anybody here that still wants to testify and has not filled out one of these cards, please see one of the sergeants and do so.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So now I'm going to call Joel Bartlett, Drew McCormick, Emily Erodis, Deborah Thomas,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nicolene von Fink.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Three people.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So I'm going to give you three more.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Well, let's see.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Let's see.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, just state your name for the record, please.

chapters.summary: Drew McCormick of Animal Defenders International testifies supporting the ban, citing the horse named Ryder who died from exhaustion, and arguing electric carriages could replace horses.
chapters.title: Drew McCormick (Animal Defenders International) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Drew McCormick.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: May I proceed?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, please.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Drew McCormick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: I represent Animal Defenders International and our New York City supporters to urge you to please pass Bill 0943 to phase out horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: This bill was renamed Ramanche after he was killed while trying to save his mother, who was stuck in a carriage pulled by a horse who had become spooked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: We all are all very incredibly sorry for this family's loss.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: We hope that this bill can prevent such tragic losses from ever happening again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Subjecting horses to the intense working conditions of pulling carriages in the city has proven to be a serious public safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Mistreating animals causes unacceptable pain, fear, and distress to the animals, which also increases the risk of them panicking and trying to escape, which poses a risk to the public as well as to the animals themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: These horses are forced to work even if they are exhausted, fearful, and when their bodies need rest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: That is why this bill was previously named Rider's Law to honor one of the many horses who

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: lapsed and died from exhaustion from pulling carriages in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: The horses work in extreme heat, breathe the exhaust fumes, and navigate traffic that terrifies them and me, risking injuries to both horses and people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Recent accidents have proven the danger is real.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: This is not a tradition worth preserving.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: It is cruelty to animals and a public risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: That we can end today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: This bill previously died in this committee due to concerns about eliminating jobs for the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Although the drivers deserve to make a living, this should not be at the expense of the horses or at the risk of the public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Furthermore, this bill does not immediately ban horse-drawn carriages and therefore provides more time for drivers to find alternative employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: For example, the electronic carriage business could replace horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: We heard comments earlier how these people could work in the government and in agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Therefore, we urge you to pass this bill and ban horse-drawn carriages in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Drew McCormick
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next, identify yourself and then give your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry.

chapters.summary: Nicolene von Fink, a lifelong equestrian, testifies about seeing a carriage collapse onto a horse's legs and hearing the horse scream, and describes another horse with slack ears in a trauma response from sustained distress.
chapters.title: Nicolene von Fink (equestrian) testifies on traumatized horses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Nicolene von Fink, and I'm a resident of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: I was born on a horse farm in Northern Virginia, and I've been riding since age four.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: I strongly support Intro 943, Romance's Law to End Horse-Drawn Carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: I've seen happy, healthy carriage horses growing up in Northern Virginia, and the horses that I've seen in Central Park are not that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Several years ago,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Years ago, I saw with my own eyes a carriage collapse onto a horse's hind legs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: The horse screamed like a child.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: And several men chaotically tried to free him, one man pulling at the reins while another pushed him down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: They were unable to lift the carriage because he was harnessed to it and caught in it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: That this is what happens when you put a half-ton prey animal in city traffic with no way to escape.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Two years ago, I was walking into Central Park and one of the horses caught my attention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: I pet his neck, he didn't react at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: His ears, a horse's primary way of engaging with the world, hung completely slack to either side.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: In 28 years around healthy horses, I had never seen that before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: It is a trauma response, total dissociation from sustained physical and emotional distress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Many people have testified today that they care deeply for their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: So why is Central Park so full of emaciated, exhausted, slack-eared horses with cracked, sore hooves?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: New York is not being asked to do something untested.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: This is one of the most modern cities in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: We should be leading, not trailing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Again, I urge the Committee on Health to vote yes and move Intro 943 into a full council vote.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicolene von Fink
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: State your name and then give your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I am Deborah.

chapters.summary: Deborah Thomas, a 20-year animal advocate, testifies that 19th century carriages don't belong in 21st century traffic, that no amount of hitching posts or training will stop spooking, and that it's cruel to force horses to work in extreme weather.
chapters.title: Deborah Thomas (animal advocate) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: Thomas speaking in support of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: I am one of the many animal-loving, humane New York City animal advocates who've been fighting for a carriage horse ban in this city for the past 20 years

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: because I feel that having 19th century horse-drawn carriages in 21st century New York City traffic is both inhumane to the horses and very dangerous to all of us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: Sadly, both were shown to be true when the horse Denise ate a poisonous plant and died in Central Park,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: and 18-year-old tourist Romesh Mahajan was killed, and my heart and prayers go out to his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: As stated earlier, New York City and Central Park have become busier and noisier in the 21st century.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: Although horse carriages in the city were once a quaint and enjoyable tradition, present-day activities and sounds going on in New York City

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: can easily spook a horse and cause them to bolt and run at the drop of a hat, making it extremely dangerous and irresponsible to continue this industry any longer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: No amount of hitching posts in the park or carriage horse driver training sessions will ever stop this from happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: I also feel that it's downright cruel to force an animal to pull a heavy carriage in the extreme heat and humidity or in the cold snow and ice that they're often expected to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: to work in, and it's questionable as to whether they're given sufficient water in the summer or blankets and shelter from the cold in the winter while they're pulling these carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: Therefore, for the sake of the horses and human beings alike, I respectfully urge the City Council to vote for and pass Intro 943, Romanche's Law, as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: I also want to thank Speaker Menon and Chair Schulman for calling this hearing and all the members who have voted for it, as well as thank you for putting language in the bill

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: to help the carriage drivers transition to new jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deborah Thomas
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you all.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So now we're going to call a new panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and thank you for waiting.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Maddie Johnson, Eve Usadi, I'm pronouncing it wrong, I'm sure.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Patrick Norton.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Christina Tabaka, something like that, Tabakin, Caroline Zonis, and Regina Clark.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so just state your name for the record and then you can testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Who's going first?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Wait, we have to put the button on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Eva, okay, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Eva Yusef-Salaam, an equine-assisted psychotherapist with 40 years of experience, testifies about horses' sensory adaptations for detecting danger, and that Manhattan's constant bombardment creates inherent safety risks no training can eliminate.
chapters.title: Eva Yusef-Salaam (equine-assisted psychotherapist) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Eva Yusef-Salaam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Yes, I got it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you very much for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: I'm speaking in support of 0943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: I am a New Yorker, born and raised in Brooklyn, currently in District 2.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: I've been a trauma therapist for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Twenty of those years, I have been an equine-assisted psychotherapist, meaning that I incorporate horses in psychotherapy with clients.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Primarily, that work has been with combat veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: In this work, I have seen firsthand how horses depend on their senses and their ability to flee perceived danger to stay safe and regulated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: We work with a herd of horses at liberty, meaning no halters, no lead lines, no harnesses, or any other means of control or domination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: And in all these years, we have had no injuries to people, no injuries to horses, none.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Horses survive by using their senses to detect danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Their nearly 360-degree vision, highly sensitive hearing, and acute sense of smell are adaptations that allow them to identify potential threats and to react quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: In Manhattan, those same senses are constantly bombarded by sirens, jackhammers, buses,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Motorcycles, dense crowds, exhaust fumes, flashing lights, and nonstop unpredictable movement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: A horse pulling a carriage cannot move freely away from what it perceives as a threat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: This conflict between instinct and environment creates an inherent safety risk that no amount of training can eliminate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: It's a public safety concern, and protecting the public is one of the City Council's core responsibilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages belonged to a different era of New York City's history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Today, we know far more about equine behavior, welfare, and public safety than we did a century ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: We cannot train away millions of years of evolution, and we should not ask horses to ignore instincts that have kept their species alive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: The question before the city council is not whether some horses can be trained to tolerate parts of this work or whether better regulations can reduce the risk to horses and citizens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: The question is whether New York City is an appropriate place to ask horses to do work their biology is not built to withstand, while also putting pedestrians, drivers, cyclists, joggers, and the horses at risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eva Yusef-Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next, state your name and then give testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is the button on?

chapters.summary: Maddie, an architect and animal welfare advocate, testifies that traditions evolve, compares horse carriages to obsolete practices, and argues the city has better jobs with benefits that can absorb displaced workers.
chapters.title: Maddie (animal welfare advocate) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: I'm Maddie, a longtime animal welfare advocate, a trope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: biracial, multilingual New Yorker, a legal immigrant citizen like our mayor, and professionally an architect, landscape architect, and urban planner who has lived and worked in several countries across the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: For those who say horse carriages are a part of old tradition, well, there was a time when flaying and beheading, drawing and quartering, and several methods of torture were also tradition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: So we keep those and bring the guillotine back too?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: And chamber ports in lieu of indoor plumbing?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Evolve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: There are far more ecological, sustainable, humane alternatives of tourist transport.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: This is for the promising young man who lost his life due to this archaic, outdated industry, which has no place in this century in this busy city when so many other cities in Europe, in the US, in Asia have banned this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: It is beyond cruel to the horses, as several other panelists have pointed out, but if not even for compassion, merely from a safety perspective, this practice should be banned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: For the ones defending this practice, it means the loss of life of a young man does not matter to you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Would you feel the same if the victim was one of your own kin and not a young man from South Asia, Romanch Mahajan, pronounced the name correctly?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Would the proponents feel the same if this was the son of someone who represents your kind of immigrant?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: The water baptisms, comparing the deaths to car accidents, even a poisonous plant, are ridiculous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: I saw a display of gaslighting, lies, full-on projections, Machiavellian manipulation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: And sociopathy, and above all, DARVO, that is, deny, attack, reverse role of victim and offender, from the defenders of this obsolete, vicious practice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Evolve, speak the truth, not lies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: So proponents of horse abuse, please get off your high horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Just like cat and dog holders claim they love animals while abusing them, riding horses to death and then sending them off to slaughter is selfish narcissism, not love for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: The city has several jobs with way better benefits that can absorb those who leave the industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, please, please summarize.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: If Romantia's law does not get passed, I'm still going to hope that someday New York will truly ban this barbaric industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Thank you so much to the caucus and to all the council people who may choose logic, compassion, and common sense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Thank you, Shakriya.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Dhanyawad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Maddie
utterances.text: Mercy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are you Patrick?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Patrick Norton, a long-term Upper West Side resident, testifies about routinely seeing horses in extreme weather, left unattended, and having close calls with runners and bikes, warning another tragedy is inevitable without the ban.
chapters.title: Patrick Norton (UWS resident) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Patrick Norton, and I'm a long-term resident of the Upper West Side.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: I live next to the 72nd Street entrance, where I walk my dog every day, and I'm here to testify about what I've seen all too many times over the past decade.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: I've seen horses out when it's over 90 degrees, horses when it's below freezing, horses left unattended, horses going up and down 72nd Street, horses going up and down Central Park West, including the incredibly busy intersection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: I've also seen runners and bikers almost crashing into horses, as well as dogs barking and lunging at the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Sometimes these dogs are even off-leash.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: And the horses are understandably startled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: I've also witnessed too many close calls where the horses and carriages lose control.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: And if this madness continues, it's only a matter of time until there's another rider or another remanche.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: You have the opportunity to end this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: You have the opportunity to prevent future cruelty, injury, and death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Please vote yes on Romant's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Otherwise, the next tragedy, the next death, the next time blood is spilt, that blood will be on your hands.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Patrick Norton
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, next panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Kathy Nazari.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Here, not here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Danny or Donnie Morris?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Donnie, right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Donnie Morris.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I know he won.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Um.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ris.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: American Museum of Natural History.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I can't read the name.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Laura Leopardo.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Paisley Anna Bailey.

chapters.summary: Donnie Moss, documentary filmmaker of 'Blinders,' testifies that the city knew this would happen, that advocates warned for 20 years a child would die, and asks what the council will do to protect people before the law takes effect.
chapters.title: Donnie Moss (documentary filmmaker) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: My name is Donnie Moss.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: I made the documentary film Blinders, the truth behind the tradition about the horse-drawn carriage controversy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: I'd like to start by saying that when Councilmember Gennaro wasn't busy belittling us for engaging in this civic right that we have in an extremely unprofessional manner, he made our argument for us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: He said that when you see horses outside of the city, they're invariably grazing on a pasture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: New York City has no pasture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: Grazing is a fundamental instinct of any horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: New York City has no pasture where these horses can graze, run, roll, interact physically as herd animals do, move about freely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: And to add insult to injury, these horses see pastures every day that they can't even access.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: That, the lack of a pasture in and of itself, should be a reason why we don't have horse-drawn carriages in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: To Romantia's family, the city knew.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: The city knew that this would happen because for the past 20 years, we've been shouting from the rooftops

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: and from inside these council chambers, and we've said explicitly that a child is going to fly out of a moving carriage, land on the pavement, and die.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: City officials chose politics ahead of public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: Romance is a victim of political expediency.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: And Mrs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: Mahajan, if you're watching, please know that we tried.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: We've been trying for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: On a final note, now that more people understand that no amount of regulation can stop a spooked horse from bolting and killing someone else,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: I'd love to know what the City Council and the Department of Health will do to prevent between now and when this law takes effect, what is the City Council going to do to protect

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: tourists inside of the carriages and pedestrians, runners, and bicyclists who could get trampled by a spooked horse who bolts and runs uncontrollably through Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donnie Moss
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Donnie.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Kathy Nizzari of Lights Out Coalition testifies supporting the ban, compares industry displacement to her own family's taxi medallion loss, notes the MTA is hiring with strong wages, and urges passage without delay.
chapters.title: Kathy Nizzari (Lights Out Coalition) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Afternoon, I'm Kathy Nazari of Lights Out Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Thank you for allowing me to testify in support of Intro 943, Roe-Munch's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: These are the only horse-drawn carriages not obsolete in NYC and are still forced to work in conditions unsuited to their welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Reports of collapses, injuries, illegal working conditions, and deaths on city streets continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: For years, advocates warned it would take a human death before this business finally ended.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Tragically, that prediction came true when a promising young man named Romesh Mahajan lost his life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: his life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: My heart goes out to him and his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: They should never have endured such a devastating loss or been forced to become advocates for change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Change is a reality of life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: How many of us have had to find new employment at some point?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: When rideshare services transformed the taxi industry, my own father's immigrant family's medallion lost its value.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: There was no bailout.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: They adapted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Histories filled with occupations that disappeared and workforce transitions are increasingly common.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: If we cannot manage this one, how will we respond when more than half a million local jobs predicted are lost to AI?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: We cannot justify continuing a dangerous, outdated industry simply because people don't like change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: The carriage drivers have something many displaced workers never had, union representation and a pathway to retraining and new jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Their union is well positioned to help them and should.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: The MTA, also with TWU, is actively hiring with strong wages and benefits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Romant's death must not become another tragedy that is acknowledged and forgotten.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: The fundamental right to life must always take precedence over commercial interests.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Business must never come at the cost of living beings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: I respectfully urge the Council to pass Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: without delay and get these poor horses off the streets now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: And if I may, Chair Shulman, I wanted to respond to a question that came up earlier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: Someone asked about NYPD hiring horse caregivers or caretakers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
utterances.text: And according to NYC.gov, the NYPD frequently hires dedicated civilian staff to care for their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathy Nizzari
