utterances.text: They work directly in the department stables and are responsible for feeding, grooming, cleaning stalls, and assisting the units veterinarians.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next.

chapters.summary: Laura Leopardo, a Brooklyn resident who grew up in Chicago, testifies supporting the ban, citing Chicago's elimination of carriages, and listing numerous documented accidents including runaway horses, collisions, and passenger injuries.
chapters.title: Laura Leopardo (Brooklyn resident) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: My name is Laura Leopardo, and I live in Prospect Heights, Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: I urge the committee to pass Romach's law into 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: I grew up in Chicago, which ended its horse-drawn carriage rides, as many major cities around the world, because they have recognized that this industry is no longer viable in dense urban environments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: I do appreciate that into 943 recognizes the livelihoods of carriage workers by providing workforce transition assistance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: That makes this legislation both compassionate and reasonable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: I had originally planned to present every documented carriage accident from the past two decades to illustrate the ongoing dangers of this industry, but there are simply way too many to fit into two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: But the record does include runaway horses, collisions with taxis, buses, bicycles, pedestrians, parked cars, and other vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: As it's been said, no amount of regulation can eliminate the inherent risk of horses operating in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: As prey animals, as it's been said, horses can panic and bolt unpredictably.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Here are just a few examples from the most recent past.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Horses have bolted into midtown traffic on multiple occasions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Passengers have been forced to jump from runaway carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Multiple times, horses have crashed into signs and parked vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Multiple times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Carriages have turned several times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Drivers have been injured and hospitalized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Then, as we all know, came June 17th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: 18-Year-old Ramach was killed after a horse bolted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: After years of warnings and repeated accidents, the tragedy that so many feared finally occurred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: We cannot say this was unforeseeable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: The warning signs have been there for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: It was a culmination of long pattern of increasingly serious incidents that the city turned a blind eye.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: Passing into 943 is an opportunity for New York City to lead with compassion, responsibility, and common sense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Laura Leopardo
utterances.text: It will protect horses, support the workers through a fair transition, and most importantly, protect the public.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to call a name I called before.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Chris Ramirez?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is that person here?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, you are here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Let me give you more names.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Give yourself a second.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Okay, I know you guys have had a very long day and I see everybody's attention dwindling, people on their phones, people conversing, and I really hope that I get your attention when I say that.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: That I found it very disrespectful and disturbing to hear that in a case about horses, that a councilman that's no longer even here would say, forget about the horse, it's already dead, and he won't hear anything that any of us are having to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Others want to speak about alternate means of transport and compare Amazon trucks, e-bikes,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: trains, planes, cars, to live sentient beings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Not any of the other things forementioned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And that is the pretense of our objection, that so many drivers treat these horses in the same indignant manner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Most of us grew up romanticizing the idea of horse and carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We normalized it as a part of the background of the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: But the reality is, for these horses, it's been decades of cruelty and neglect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: With the introduction of video cameras and camera phones in society, along with undercover investigations, countless videos are now in the forefront of what this industry is really about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We begin with their homes, the coffin-sized stables, with not even enough space to turn around or lie down in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: It's rat-infested and there's no AC or heat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: These horses are not allowed to be in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Horses freeze throughout the winter or swelter in the summer with no relief and limited ventilation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: If anything, contrary to what one of the testimony says, you cannot in fact openly visit one of these stables with open access.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'm sorry I'm losing you guys again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I'll wait.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And just, you know, just for the record, I was pushing back with anybody that was, you know, out of decorum up here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I've been doing that the whole day today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No worries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I wasn't able to finish it because I feel like I lost everyone's attention and I still have 10 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And I want to address the painful metal bars in their mouth and the many videos that have surfaced of the drivers yanking at the attachments of the bars, causing extreme pain for these docile animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, next panel, Lisa Goldberg.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Lisa Goldberg here?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's you?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Tilly the Clown?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Zach.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Baldian, I think it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Zach?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Margaret Lee?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, wait, give me one second, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Mr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Melody Tim or something to that effect?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Marion Koenig?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Koenig?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And Linda Mann.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so from my right, your left.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just state your name for the record and then go into your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Linda Mann.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Chair Shulman and members of the committee that are still here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: I know some of you have already made up your minds, and nothing I can say will change that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: But I was brought up to speak for those who cannot speak for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: And today I will speak for those whose world is shaped by constant noise, crowds, traffic, and stress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: For those who are forced to live in an unnatural environment for their species.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Who never feel the natural ground beneath them, and for those who go back to enclosures that never come close to meeting their needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: This is an industry built on confinement, whether in the park or in their stalls.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Walk by a hack line and see the sad, defeated look in those eyes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Then go to a sanctuary where they feel safe, probably for the first time in their lives, where they are given adequate space and a

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: to live free from exploitation and abuse, where these sensitive, social, herd animals are allowed to live naturally and see the difference in those eyes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: I am a big believer in the concept of once you know, you can no longer say you didn't know when something happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: This is where we are now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: You all know the danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: We saw it in real time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: The world saw it in real time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: And there was a family in India who lived it in real time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: This hearing brings us one step closer to a city safe from that ever happening again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: It brings us one step closer to giving these magnificent beings the freedom they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: For years, they have been waiting for us to step in to end their suffering.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: They have waited far too long.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Linda Mann
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, no clapping.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Oh, hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: My name is Tilly the Clown.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: I am a drag entertainer here in New York City, and I felt that with the very rich history that queer people have in standing up for, you know, human and animal rights, I figured, you know, why not represent my community?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: So I sit before you today as a drag entertainer in the greatest city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: I'm an artist who benefits greatly from the tourism that sustains our nightlife, and I am grateful for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: And there's a glaring archaic contradiction in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: When I left my city of Philadelphia 14 years ago,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: was trying to find a better, the next step up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: I never thought that Philadelphia would end up being better than us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: We pride ourselves on being world-class destination with the best nightlife, museums, food, and we are still clinging to the exploitation of horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: This is not a matter of commerce.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: It is a matter of biology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: A giant prey animal is not built to thrive in New York's overstimulating concrete environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Veterinary experts, many of which we've seen here today, have consistently found that pulling heavy carriages on unforgiving concrete leads to arthritis, hoof deterioration, chronic pain, and these animals are biologically imprisoned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: They are kept in cramped stables where restricted movement causes colic, a painful, often fatal condition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: While we are forcing them into a lifestyle that causes them internal agony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: We are gathered here today after the tragic death of a human about to embark on adulthood, Ramansh.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: My heart goes out to his family and the loved ones for their devastating loss, but we should not have had to wait for human tragedy to act.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: This has been a systemic failure for over a decade.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: We have seen evidence in our streets for years from Tickles back in 2015, who was euthanized after a fracture because he was no longer a profitable product, to a more recent collapse of Ryder, who this bill was prior named after.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Even regarding Dennis, who died in the park, the industry may point to toxic plants as the cost, but the fact remains that these animals are being forced into urban environments where they're exposed to such dangers while working for this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: The current system is clearly broken, marked by an annual turnover rate of over 70 horses with zero transparency regarding where these animals are sent once they are removed from city rolls.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: The union argues that we cannot phase out these horses.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I need you to wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: But we all know, as we've seen today, the network of over four dozen sanctuaries have written a letter of intent to take them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Cities across the globe, from Philadelphia, Chicago, Salt Lake City,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: to London, Beijing, Barcelona, and Montreal have all put this terrible practice behind them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: So my last words to you is thank you so much for your time, and let's make history and keep New York being on the future and the best city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tilly the Clown
utterances.text: Of course, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Lisa Goldberg.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: I'm a New York City resident and an animal advocate and speaking in favor of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: The Central Park carrot horses are denied the freedom to graze, roam, and form the deep bonds with one another that are natural to these highly social herd animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: More than that, we've witnessed too many of them collapse and die on New York City streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: Most recently, Denise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: lady and rider, but many others over the years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: You don't have to be moved by the plight of the horses, though, to recognize how incredibly dangerous it is to have horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: Horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: Their hardwired instinct is to spook and run when they perceive a threat, a loud noise, or a sudden movement, things that are everyday, unavoidable occurrences in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: We've seen so much video footage documenting harrowing near misses where horses have spooked and bolted and passersby have narrowly escaped being trampled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: Now 18-year-old Romesh Mahajan has tragically been killed in a preventable incident that should never have been allowed to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: I hope the City Council will respond to the Mahajan family's tragedy by passing Romesh's law immediately,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lisa Goldberg
utterances.text: because if horses continue to pull carriages in New York City, it's only a matter of time before the next inevitable tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: I'm Margaret Lee, New Yorker, animal defender.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: My thanks to Speaker Menon, Bill's sponsor Chris Marte, and co-sponsors of Intro 943, and all who made this hearing possible, today's hearing possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Many New Yorkers have waited decades for this moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Immense and devastating heartbreak has finally brought us to this hearing on national celebration of the horse day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: I am here in support of Intro 943, Romange's Law, named for 18-year-old Romange Mahogany, who died while riding in a Central Park horse-drawn carriage with his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: I am also here as one of the great majority of New Yorkers who have been long pleading for an end to this dangerous and cruel industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Many of us even voted based on promises to ban horse carriages, only to be disappointed time and time again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: The truth is simple.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Horses do not belong on New York City streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: No amount of legislation, regulation, training, infrastructure changes can make horse carriages safe or ethical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: The tragedies that preceded Romach's death were foreseeable, and until this industry ends, more tragedies

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: will inevitably follow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Four years ago, Ryder's Law, named for the exploited horse who collapsed while pulling a carriage, never even received the hearing it deserved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Despite the support of most New Yorkers,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: It was repeatedly blocked while activists like myself spent years urging this city to choose compassion over tradition and profit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Today you have the opportunity to do what should have been done long ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Pass Intro 943 and move it urgently, speedingly forward now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Before another horse or another person risks being harmed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Let New York finally become the compassionate, forward-thinking city it aspires to be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: Release these long-suffering horses to caring sanctuaries waiting for them, where they can run freely, and show the world that compassion has finally

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Margaret Lee
utterances.text: prevailed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next panel, Lisa Reddy, Steve Batista,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Shiloh Hoffman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Ellen Ruddick-Sunstein.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Howie Voges, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And Ed Schultz.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, starting with you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Make sure your mic is on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, you have to push the button.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And identify yourself and then testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Hello, I'm Skyler Hoffman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: And I want to be respectful of your time, so I'm going to try to actually finish this early.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Sign of respect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Let's start off with...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Poison shrubbery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Poison shrubbery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: It's kind of ridiculous, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Like, the audacity and greed of this horse carriage industry to expect that Central Park change everything to accommodate for their needs kind of seems a little funny to me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: And I really think that this is greed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: I mean, I am an organizer and I'm very interested in unions and labor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: And so when I see this sort of union that they have, but it's all of managers, and it's mainly representing their own greed, I think to myself, this is ridiculous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: We're saving them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Who are we actually saving?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: People that want to force other beings to suffer to make them money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Let's get rid of this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Skyler Hoffman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: My name is Steve Batista.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I've been a dog walker on the west side of Manhattan for

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: 26 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: So the congested gridlocked avenue paths the carriage industry takes to and from Central Park coincide with my own work territory.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I've seen it all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I've seen horses electrocuted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I have seen horses clipped and injured by vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I have seen horses jump curbs and bolt onto sidewalks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I have seen horses frantically pulling empty carriages lost by their drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I have seen terrified, overtaxed animals inhaling exhaust fumes and suffering in extreme weather conditions, suffering year after year in bondage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: In August of 2025, a horse named Lady collapsed and died on 53rd Street in Hell's Kitchen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: A carriage driver admitted to my face that the horse was forced to work with cancer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: I have seen enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: We all have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: The TWU

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: and whatever shameless shills that may remain on the city council are insulting New Yorkers when they falsely claim that any animal welfare reforms are applicable in this situation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: The only answer to this ugly relic of history is a full ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: Mahatma Gandhi famously said,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: that the greatness of a nation and its moral progress can be judged by the way its animals are treated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: As I stand here today with my fellow citizens of conscience, I ask you to realize the greatness

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: of this city and its movement towards progress by supporting the passage of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Steve Batista
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Ed Schultz.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: I'm a New York City public school teacher.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: I'm a proud union member, a son of an immigrant, and I'm a grateful constituent of City Council Member Shahana Hanif.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: Thank you all for being here, by the way, so late.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: I come today to urge you to pass Romancia's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: The carriage horse industry has always claimed that their horses are retired to sanctuary when they can no longer pull.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: This despite the fact that former New York City carriage horse named Bernard was rescued from a notorious kill buyer auction in Pennsylvania in 2024, as reported on by the New York Post.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: I find it telling that today the carriage industry has repeatedly threatened that they will sell their horses to slaughter if this bill passes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: One would expect that every carriage horse should have a retirement plan from the very first day they're connected to a carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: That's the industry's responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: And if they don't, they are literally one serious injury away from being sold to a kill buyer, which they've acknowledged today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: I teach my students that a fundamental part of democracy is that the majority rule.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: A vote for Romancia's law is not just a vote to protect horses and public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: It's a vote for democracy, as an overwhelming majority of New York City residents want this bill to be law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Schultz
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Ellen Sunstein.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I'm a former equestrian turned horse advocate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify in strong support of Romantia's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: When I was a child, I rode a horse-drawn carriage through Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I offered a carrot to the weary horse, and he took it from my hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I didn't realize at the time that that gentle horse was forced to pound the pavement day after day, even when it was swelteringly hot or blisteringly cold.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: At night, he'd go back to a tiny stall, likely aching and exhausted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I have known many horses in the years since, but I never forgot the feeling of that horse's breath in the palm of my hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I've often wondered what became of him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Did he drop dead in the street like Lady and Denise?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Was he whipped like Ryder or kicked while screaming like Luciana?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Did he die after being forced to haul tourists in a heat wave like Billy?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Did he spend his last moments alone and terrified in a 60-square-foot stall?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Such extreme confinement is harrowing for a horse, whose most fundamental needs include social contact, free movement, and access to forage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: When deprived of these needs, they can develop colic, ulcers, or musculoskeletal issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: They may sway endlessly or exhibit other repetitive stress-induced behaviors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: As I've seen myself, some become shut down and unresponsive in a state similar to depression in humans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Intensive confinement is not horsemanship.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I loved New York as a child.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: and I still do, but the carriage trade is a stain on the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: No one can save Lady, Denise, Ryder, or Billy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: No one can save Romant, though you can honor him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: I can't save the gentle, exhausted horse I met all those years ago, whose name I forget, but whose suffering I can't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: But you can save the next horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ellen Sunstein
utterances.text: Please pass Romant's law.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank this panel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, the next panel is Joe Schinkel, Benjamin Larkin, Lackin maybe, Rosemary Espinel,

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Elizabeth Farrell and Nicole Revere.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: My name is Benjamin Konigsberg.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: I'm a native New Yorker and I support Intro 943 prohibiting our city's horse-drawn carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: This isn't about tradition or tourism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: It's about the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Every carriage horse exists because humans intentionally bred them into a life of servitude.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: They didn't choose this work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: We chose it for them.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sorry, sorry, Benjamin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I said Benjamin Lackin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: That's you?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, I thought you said another name.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Horses evolved to roam freely and live in family groups.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Instead, carriage horses spent much of their lives confined to stalls as small as 60 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Research has shown that prolonged stall confinement is associated with chronic stress, gastric ulcers, and other serious physical and psychological welfare problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: They walk on hard concrete and asphalt, breathe exhaust, navigate loud noise and dense crowds despite their natural instinct to flee perceived danger at a moment's notice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: To control them, they wear bits in their mouth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: And veterinary research has documented that these bits can cause pain and injuries to the sensitive tissues of their mouth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: We all remember Ryder, whose collapse shocked New York and raised questions about industry oversight, including allegations that records regarding his age had been falsified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: And of course, Romance, whose tragedy reminds us that this industry is not only dangerous for the horses, but for New Yorkers and tourists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: So the question before us isn't whether carriage horses are treated better than others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: The question is why do we continue exploiting sentient beings for entertainment when humane alternatives already exist?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: And all of these harms stems from one fundamental belief that horses exist for us to use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: They don't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Horses, like all animals, exist for their own purpose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: They value their own lives independent of what they can provide to humans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: They are not property.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: The measure of a city is not how it entertains its tourists, but how it treats the most vulnerable beings in its care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Please vote yes on Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Benjamin Konigsberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Rosemary Espinal testifies that a government failing to protect vulnerable beings including horses is not effective, and tells council members who previously voted to keep carriages that they bear responsibility for Romanch's death.
chapters.title: Rosemary Espinal testifies supporting ban, holds past council responsible

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: My name is Rosemary Espinal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: Honorable council members, a government that fails to protect its most vulnerable, it's not an effective government.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: And that includes horses who are made to work in excruciating and deplorable conditions in the streets of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: Animals need our protection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: These horses suffer pain and depression from the atrocious conditions and hard labor they're made to endure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: New York City should not be participating in this cruel and depraved industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: A human life was lost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: But so have many horses lost their lives, too, in a cruel and senseless activity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: To those council members who voted in the past to keep horse carriages, you bear some responsibility for the death of Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: Romance because you failed to dismantle this industry when you had the power to do so.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: To council members today, I plead with you not to make the same mistakes that other council members made in the past.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rosemary Espinal
utterances.text: Please help put an end to this long and painful chapter for New York City and pass Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Nicole Rivard of Friends of Animals testifies supporting the ban, proposes adding language for a fund to buy out horses from willing owners, and questions how the industry can claim retirement is safe now but a death sentence if the bill passes.
chapters.title: Nicole Rivard (Friends of Animals) testifies supporting ban with buyout fund proposal

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Nicole Rovard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: It's an honor to be here on behalf of Friends of Animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: We were founded in New York in 1957.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: You can stop the carriage horse industry from claiming any more lives, and we urge you to pass 943 quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: But we realize you can't legislate that the owners will willingly relinquish their horses, so we would also support adding language that creates a fund to enable the city to facilitate

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: a buyout of the horses from the owners who want to sell and help with placement fees at the sanctuaries who need them, which of course Friends of Animals would contribute to, and we know other groups would as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: We can even accommodate five or six of the carriage horses at our own sanctuary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: Because of Intro 943, the industry is trying to get a leg up, pun intended.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: It's pivoted from talking only about so-called safety measures like posts to spinning a narrative that all the horses are going to slaughter if this bill becomes law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: But how come when they retire their own horses now from service, that's not a death sentence?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: How can both things be true?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: Being deadly is not the only reason the trade needs to be banned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: It also robs equines of their basic needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: People have talked about turnout for roaming free and socialization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: But there is actual research that shows these things lower stress-related behaviors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: And not only does the carriage horse industry take away the very...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: things these animals need to be calm, then they have to pull carriages, along with unassuming tourists, into the unpredictable New York City environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: As an equestrian of 42 years, I know firsthand that even in the safest, calmest environments, horses may still freeze,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: bolt or react defensively to sudden changes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: The industry made drivers undergo safety training after the recent tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nicole Rivard
utterances.text: They wanted the public and tourists to feel safer, but they aren't, and neither are the horses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you all for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The next panel is Fiki Ryder.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Marilyn Galfin, Kathleen Rabb, Nicolette Hoffman, Fiorella Valdivia, Marley,

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Del Mar Leon, Diane Pretuzelli.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Y'all may begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just identify yourself for the record.

chapters.summary: Marilyn Galfin of Voices for Shelter Animals testifies supporting the ban, citing eight incidents in 13 months, majority public support, and arguing tradition is not an excuse for an outdated, inhumane industry.
chapters.title: Marilyn Galfin (Voices for Shelter Animals) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Marilyn Galfin voices for shelter animals in support of Intro 943, Ramach's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: The tragic loss of Ramach Mahajan is a devastating reminder that the carriage horse industry is not only inhumane, but is inherently dangerous to the public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: The city can no longer look away as horses collapse and die in our streets, traumatizing and horrifying the public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: As spooked horses run wild and as drivers continue to violate laws, including operating in brutal weather conditions,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: My attempts to get help to remove a carriage from the streets during a blizzard were met with total inaction, leaving me feeling completely helpless as I couldn't protect this horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: There were eight reported horse-related incidents in 13 months, and a recent poll shows the majority of New Yorkers support a ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Allowing this industry, this cruel industry, to continue

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: is playing Russian roulette with the lives of New Yorkers, tourists, carriage horses, carriage drivers,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: and would be negligent, especially in today's city's chaotic, unsafe environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: We are way past the time for band-aids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: No amount of regulation can make this so-called iconic practice acceptable anymore.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Tradition should no longer be an excuse for an outdated, inhumane industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: It has already been banned in so many places across the world, and it's long overdue in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: These majestic horses deserve retirement and a peaceful life flourishing in sanctuaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Not another day in traffic living a miserable life they are forced into.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Furthermore, this bill provides a structured transition for workers, setting a modern standard for navigating change, and addressing supporting the livelihoods of the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Not every industry gets that help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: Now, while the carriage horse trade is in plain sight, I cannot ignore the fact that we have a hidden tragedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: It's the adoptable, treatable animals being needlessly killed in our city shelters, which is long overdue and needs to be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: I just want to finish this off.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: That shelter animals have no rights and they receive lack of humane treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: And what's happening now is neither acceptable nor a solution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: I just ask that it's 2026, we pass from Ansh's law, and show our compassion and end this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marilyn Galfin
utterances.text: but also realize that all life is sacred and everything possible must be done by the city government to protect it, including our shelter animals.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Kathleen Reb of SPCA International testifies supporting the ban, citing recent tragedies including Denise's death and Romanch's killing, and noting Philadelphia's recent vote to end carriage rides.
chapters.title: Kathleen Reb (SPCA International) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Kathleen Reb, members of the council, thank you for the opportunity to speak today on behalf of SPCA International about an issue that directly impacts animal welfare and the safety of all people in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: We are here today because it is time that New York City move beyond horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: This is an outdated practice that puts both horses and people at risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Our cities are busier than ever, filled with traffic, construction, noise, and countless hazards that were never designed for large animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Yet horses are still expected to navigate in these conditions every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Recent tragedies have underscored these concerns within a matter of weeks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Another carriage horse collapsed and died.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: A man lost his life in an incident involving a horse-drawn carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: These heartbreaking events remind us that the status quo is not without consequences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Cities are beginning to recognize that there is a better way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Just last month, Philadelphia voted to end horse-drawn carriage rides, demonstrating that communities can prevent their history while embracing safer and more humane alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: New York has long been a leader in advancing animal welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: We have the opportunity to once again lead with compassion by ending an outdated practice that no longer reflects the values of our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: We urge the council to support Ramont's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: that phases out horse-drawn carriages and prioritizes the safety and well-being of both New Yorkers and the horses who deserve a safer life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Let's be on the right side of history for our communities and for the animals who depend on us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Reb
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and consideration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Diane Petruzzelli, a former SPCA volunteer, testifies about horses being forced to pull tourists through the busiest city in the world, asks council members to imagine if this were their own pet, and urges passage.
chapters.title: Diane Petruzzelli (SPCA volunteer) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: My name is Diane Petruzzelli.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: I've worked with the SPCA for many years in animal adoption and humane education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: For years, I've signed petitions, written letters, hoped someone would finally end the suffering of New York City's carriage horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: But after witnessing tragedy after tragedy, I realized these horses need more than our sympathy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They need our voices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Imagine spending every day surrounded by blaring horns, sirens, traffic, exhaust fumes, scorching heat, freezing temperatures, and miles of unforgiving pavement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Imagine never having a choice, never being able to say, I'm tired, I'm hurt, I'm afraid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: That is the life of a New York City carriage horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: These beautiful animals were never meant to spend their lives pulling tourists through the busiest city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Horses are meant to run through open fields, graze beside other horses, and simply be horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Instead, they spend their entire lives walking on pavement, surrounded by noise and traffic, living a life

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They never chose nor would ever choose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Horse-drawn carriages belong to another century.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They were created before automobiles existed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: New York has always...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: He's been a city that embraces progress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Today you have the opportunity to let our compassion evolve as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: And this isn't only about animal welfare, it's also about public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Recent tragedies have reminded us of the risk to both horses and people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: We should not have to wait for another tragedy before deciding that this outdated practice has to come to an end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: If this were your horse, your dog, or the pet you love the most, would you want this life for them?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Would you want them breathing exhaust surrounded by blaring sirens and traffic, walking mile after mile on hard pavement in the heat of summer and the cold of winter?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: These horses have spent their lives serving us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They never chose this life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They cannot stand before you today and ask for a different one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Only you can give them that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: I respectfully ask that you vote yes on this legislation and help give these horses the future they've always deserved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Years from now, people will not ask why New York City ended horse-drawn carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: They will ask why it took so long.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Diane Petruzzelli
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This side of the table.

chapters.summary: Delmar Leon of Canine Rescue NYC testifies supporting the ban, listing decades of specific carriage incidents from 1982 to 2016, and argues the pattern demonstrates an industry with little oversight showing neglect.
chapters.title: Delmar Leon (Canine Rescue NYC) testifies with incident timeline

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairman and Council Members, for prioritizing this matter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: My name is Delmar Leon, founder of Canine Rescue NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: I believe the compounded numbers of accidents that have occurred over three decades on an ongoing basis only points to an industry who has been overlooked with little oversight while consistently showing neglect based on the numbers of accidents we have seen

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: for decades, which culminated into the tragedy that called for its suspension after the death of Ramond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: A precedent needs to be made.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Be sad with the death of Ramant, whom I take this opportunity to offer my condolences to the Mahajan's family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: This bill is not about removing the passion for riding your horse, as was heard by one panelist's testimony, but removing them from riding on the streets of New York City and Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: We also heard earlier from their main veterinary, there is no standard policy in place to ensure their animal's well-being is being maintained.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Assessment of the horse health is left to be determined by the horse rider or owner, and the horse owner or rider also decides when to call the vet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: How are we ensuring the driver that horse that day will be looking out for the animal's welfare?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: If it may mean no income for that day or week.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: We also heard earlier how council members had yet to be called back to schedule a meeting and only did so after weeks, which allows time to correct and or cover up any deficiencies before the time comes for visit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: The horse carriage drivers mentioned horses are a creature of habits, yet there is a pattern of accidents showing that in 30 years, horses have yet to get used and accustomed to riding on the streets of New York City and Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: 1982, three horses collapse and die on the streets in July, including Malachi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: November 1986, riding horse taking off and crashing into building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: May 1988, a horse failing into manhole.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: May 1990, a horse being fatally pinned between a bus and a car.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: January 2006, a five-year-old gelding named Spotty spooks bolt into traffic and wraps himself over the top of a station wagon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: The horse breaks in leg and dies, becoming a major catalyst for organized campaigns to ban New York City horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: April 2014, a carriage horse is pinned to the ground in an overturned carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: after being spooked by a bus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Two years later, in January 2016, Spartacus spooks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: I would like to conclude.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: It is time to ban carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Horses out of the streets of New York City and Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: This incident only demonstrates an industry that has been overlooked with a little oversight showing a pattern that has been overlooked.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: To conclude, just one little thing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Your time is up.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: May I just finalize this last thing?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: I would like to conclude by stating that I'm in favor of Romance Riders Bill 1943, bringing attention to a similar matter happening within the animal shelter system and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: NYC ACC, also left with no oversight and accountability, leaving an inhumane confinement, highly medicated, and ensure that they can cope with the inhumane shelter environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: This couple of weeks alone, they have doubled up in killings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Schedule oversight hearing on NYC ACC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Delmar Leon
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Fiorella Valdivia, a District 5 resident near Central Park, testifies supporting the ban, shares her own experience losing a sibling, and asks council members who haven't supported the bill if they can afford to let this continue.
chapters.title: Fiorella Valdivia (District 5 resident) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: My name is Fiorella Valdivia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: I live in District 5.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Talk closer to the microphone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: My name is Fiorella Valdivia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: I live in District 5, a few blocks from Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: So I see this every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: I would like to thank everyone who came here today, including all council members supporting romance law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: And especially Speaker Julie-Menin for her leadership.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: As someone who already lost a sibling in a tragedy, I know what a romance family is going through.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: They have to go through this pain for the rest of their life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Fiorella Valdivia
utterances.text: And when his little brother sees another brother together, he will be in profoundly pain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This issue is far from ill-conceived and silly, as was suggested by Councilmember James Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: What a shame.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: I have a question for all the council members who have not supported Ramange Law Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Do you really have the luxury to allow this to continue?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Do you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We can see why carriage horses do not belong in dense populated settings like Central Park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: We all know how heavily trafficked and loud New York is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The carriage industry is a 19th century industry trying to fit in the 21st century.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: No occupation should take precedence over human life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: The citizens of New York deserve a transformative change and not want to be compromised.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: This is an urgent matter, and the truth is that there is no regulation

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: that can prevent this from happening again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: And if we don't address it with urgency, many more will follow Ramesh and his family destiny.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: Let's go.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The next panel is John Rosen.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Rose and green, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes, no?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Lauren Koenig?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Israel Melman.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yalda.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Mosley, okay, come on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Roxanne Delgado.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Nellie McKay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Nope.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, Justina Adorno.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Joanne Sulaco.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Are you just in it?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, you're Nellie.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, Valentine.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Joanne Sulaco.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yalda Moslenian?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, that's you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: It's late.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Donna Stein and Lorraine Fleischman.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, Lorraine's not here.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Donna's here.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So we'll start with you guys.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just state your name for the record, and then we'll give you a testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Yalda Musleon, a Manhattan resident, testifies about seeing depressed, malnourished horses every time she enters Central Park, cites eight incidents in 13 months as a pattern, and references LinkedIn's coaching program for transitioning workers.
chapters.title: Yalda Musleon (Manhattan resident) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Hi, good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Is it evening?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Wow, I've been here since the morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Oh, I don't need to hold it, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: My name is Yalda Musleon, and I'm an animal advocate based in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: So I'm just here as a human being and a resident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: I work in tech.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And I'm also an actor, and I'm very much involved in New York City activities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And one of my favorite things to do, I live in the West Village, but I, on a monthly basis, will visit Central Park because it's just a nice escape, get the greenery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: But every time I enter Central Park, I just see the horses, and they look depressed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: I mean, we see it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And it's like, it's so blatant in our faces.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Every single time we enter the park, we see the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: They're malnourished.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: You can see like the bony ribs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: It's very disturbing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And I'm like, how is this still happening?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: New York, where is the need?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: What is the need for horse carriages?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: We have modern alternatives, pedicabs, eco-friendly transit, we can walk, that don't require living animals to labor in traffic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Under New York law, animal cruelty is defined as every act, omission, or neglect whereby unjustifiable physical pain or suffering is caused or permitted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: No carriage ride is worth a horse's suffering.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: These are not isolated incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: There have been eight documented horse-related incidents in Central Park in the 13 months leading up to Romach Mahajan's death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
