utterances.text: Eight in one of the most visited public spaces in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: That is not a safety record.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: That is a pattern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: It was mentioned earlier, veterinarians describe their basic needs, and it's the three Fs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Friends, they are social creatures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Forage, they need to, oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Well, I am in favor of the law, and I appreciate you taking this into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And horses can't speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: So we are the voice for them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And let's ban this animal cruelty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Yalda Musleon
utterances.text: And I just want to say LinkedIn has an excellent coaching program to support the transferable skills that these carriage drivers have.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Roxanne Delgado, a Bronx resident and Latina daughter of immigrants, testifies about horses being double-shifted and overworked, compares NYC to other cities that have banned carriages, and criticizes Councilmember Gennaro's behavior.
chapters.title: Roxanne Delgado (Bronx resident) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Good evening, my name is Roxanne Delgado.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I reside in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: If it matters, I'm Latina.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: My parents are immigrants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And I'm here because I support this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I just left work, and I work a very physical job, and my back is killing me, and I feel tired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: But after this, I'm gonna go home, rest, take some medication,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And if I don't feel better, I'll call out tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: But the horses don't have that luxury.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: They work to death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And I have seen, first,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: how these horses are double shifted, overboarded, left to bake in the sun despite the fact that the driver is underneath a tree, refuge from the heat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And during those holiday season, they work to death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: They work around with no rest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And there's no enforcement of the minimal humane laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Like I said, I was here almost 15 years ago with the Coalition to Ban Horse-Drawn Carriages with Intro 89, and things have not improved for the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: It's gotten just worse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Things that we can't even address, like global warming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I'd like to mention that the drivers have unions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Those unions have many jobs on the website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: They could help employ many of their workers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: But the horses, it's not right what we're doing to these horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: These horses are worked to death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And I feel like the city council in general, the city has been the forefront of many issues that other cities follow along.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: But with horse-drawn carriage, we've been behind the issue of many cities throughout the country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And the city banned horse-drawn carriage for the same issues why we're here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Because it's abusive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And yes, of course we want people to be employed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: But there's not jobs on an excuse to abuse animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: We are better than that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And I voted for term limits because of Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And he hasn't evolved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: He's still with that same nonsense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I just, after 15 years, he's still back in that mentality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: We as humans have to evolve every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: And I'm so disappointed in his behavior.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I'd like to thank you for your time and hopefully to see you do the right thing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I want to tell you that, for the record, all of us that are here now are on the bench.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Well, thank you for doing the right thing, and thank you for having the courage for those who don't have a voice in the most marginalized community like myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: We're marginalized, and we need people to advocate for us, and so do those horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: That's why I'm here, because they're marginalized just like me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: You have a great evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Roxanne Delgado
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, put your mic on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You have to push the button.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Donna Stein, a lifelong New Yorker, testifies about 20 years of advocacy, cites 128 accidents since 1982 and 29 work-related horse deaths, and argues hitching posts invite disaster by inviting drivers to leave horses.
chapters.title: Donna Stein (lifelong New Yorker) testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Now my time starts.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: My name is Donna Stein.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: I am speaking for myself and for C.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: White, lifelong New Yorkers and lifelong horse lovers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: 20 years ago, Newsweek wrote, New York has the highest carriage horse accident rate in the country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: The industry called for hitching posts, driver training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Newsweek feared only a human death could end it now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Now the eyes of the world are on us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Safe, humane, horse-drawn carriages are not possible in New York because of the nature of the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Dangerous urban traffic, crowded parks, all horses spook, prey animals, wary of danger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: They bolt and run for their lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: 128 accidents since 1982, 29 work-related horse deaths, now remorse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Plus, lives shortened by overwork and slaughter, no turnout to pasture, which horses, working horses require for their health and well-being to be horses with other horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Climate extremes have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Busy seasons when they collapse and die in our streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Summer heat, hotter on asphalt, humidity hard on horses, then the cold of the punishing winter holiday season.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Concussive leg and hoof injuries, pounding pavement with respiratory harm coming from exhaust can end their working lives and lead to slaughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Inadequate housing, even upstairs in stalls too small for draft horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: At the end, brutal slaughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: The history wants hitching posts in Central Park, but this invites disaster.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Horses can spook tied to hitching horses, posts, sorry, breaking their necks, rearing, collapsing, backward runways, dragging the posts, injuries and death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Hitching posts do not make it safe for drivers to leave their horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: By law and good horse sense, horses must be attended at all times at the horse's head, including at a hitching post where the public can stress the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: Negligence caused the death of Romange and many horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: It's human to think, I'll only be a moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: What could happen?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: But in that moment, horses spooked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: The unthinkable occurred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: More driver training a few years ago did nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Donna Stein
utterances.text: It's no surprise to me that everyone who is against this law had a profit motive.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Nellie, right?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just state your name.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: For the record, sorry.

chapters.summary: Nellie Mackay, a longtime animal rights activist, testifies briefly in support of the ban, urging retirement of horses to sanctuaries.
chapters.title: Nellie Mackay testifies supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: Oh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: My name is Nellie Mackay, and I've been going to protests against the carriage horses since I was a little girl, so it's been a very long time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: And so I am here in support of Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: As you have heard from so many people, these horses work in the sweltering heat and the freezing cold.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: They're easily spooked.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: They work next to loud, unpredictable vehicles and machinery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: This exploitative and dangerous industry has gone on long enough, so please retire the horses to a caring sanctuary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nellie Mackay
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Really appreciate you staying.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Appreciate your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: It's very important to us, so thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, Karen Kapnick.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes, no?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Farratt is the last name on this, F-I-R-A-T.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Christopher Leon Johnson and Raul Rivera.

chapters.summary: Christopher Leon Johnson testifies supporting the ban, calling it a 'great land grab' that saves lives, criticizing TWU as corrupt, and arguing the union fears losing members to other unions if drivers transition to other industries.
chapters.title: Christopher Leon Johnson testifies supporting ban, calls it 'great land grab'

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Ready?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Yeah, hello, my name is Christopher Leon Johnson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I support Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I wanna say this right now, there's a special place in hell for Jim Gennaro because he is one of the biggest adversaries when it comes to this bill, and many others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I know I can't, I'm trying to be under quorum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I'll say this right now that look, let's keep it 100% that this bill

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: is going to target two properties, the 52nd Street location and 30th Street location.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Now, I understand that a lot of people say it's not a land grab.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: It's a land grab, but it's a great land grab because if that means that this type of land grab will save lives, I'm for it 100%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I'll say this right now that TWU is a powerful union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: They are a corrupt union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I got them this morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: They're a real corrupt union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: They have a lot of elected officials under their thumb, including the spy ones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I know a lot of you guys turn limited.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: And they're going to do whatever they got to do to make sure this bill dies in the city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I hope that you guys have the balls to fight against them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Reynoso lost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: The borough president lost against Claire Valdez.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: If she could defeat him, you could defeat John Samuelson and John Chicharillo in the city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I'll say this right now that when it comes to amendments, you have to make sure that the people that do the horse carriage drivers are protected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: And the only reason why he's pissed off, John Chicharillo is angry about, he's saying about casinos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Everybody in the industry knows he gambles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: He gambles a lot in Atlantic City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: It's because those casinos and the cleaners, they're under 32BJ and HTC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: And he knows if they go to become a cleaner or a host at a casino like Resource World,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: he's going to lose his members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: So won't we just say, like, won't he push for making people bus drivers and bus drivers and conductors?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Why is he not doing that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Oh, he don't care because he knows that once that happens, he's scared of getting voted out of office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: So I'll say this right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I support the bill 100%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Like I said, I know this is targeting 38th Street Hub, 38th Street by Javits Center, and on 52nd Street by the West Side Highway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: But this is the great land grab.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: I support this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Leon Johnson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Raul?

chapters.summary: Raul Rivera, a TLC driver advocate, testifies opposing the ban, calling it a real estate land grab by developers, criticizing Speaker Menin's husband as a real estate developer, and arguing the council weaponizes safety against working people.
chapters.title: Raul Rivera (TLC driver advocate) testifies opposing ban as land grab

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Good evening, Chair and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: My name is Raul Rivera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: I'm a native New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: I have been advocating in this city for almost nine years, fighting for the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: If this council could not help the New York City TLC drivers, how do you expect to help horses?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: How do you expect to make things safe when you couldn't help the drivers?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Let's call this what it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Intro 0943 is a land grab by the real estate companies, a land grab by the developers, and both Councilmember Speaker Julie-Menin and Mayor Mamdani knows it's a land grab.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: They know this has nothing to do with safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Speaker Menon's husband is a real estate developer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: That's why this land grab is happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: And when advocates like myself speak up, the committee is quick to cut off my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: What you're doing is weaponizing public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: This has nothing to do with safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: This is about real estate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: The most profitable business in New York City is real estate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: This isn't about horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: It's about the stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: You're taking the homes away from the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: You're gentrifying New York City even for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Case in point, the vessel at the Hudson Yards, several suicides there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: No outrage from this council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: No hearings on safety at Hudson Yards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: You weaponize safety against working people while ignoring real safety failures in your pet projects.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: And let's be honest about Speaker Julie Menon's record.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: She's never supported the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: We asked for help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: We asked for a meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: We even got an email from her office that said, not interested in meeting with the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: And where was this council for Priscilla's Law?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Priscilla Loki was killed by an e-bike.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Her family begged for Priscilla's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: No one asked City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: No one at city council cared enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: I'll submit the rest of the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can summarize if you want.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: It's okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: It's all right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Raul Rivera
utterances.text: But I do want to say that Curtis Sliwa sold out New York, and he is officially now, I anoint him king of the scumbags.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you both.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And, yeah, please make sure you submit the testimony, okay?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Is there anybody here who has not testified that hasn't filled out a card yet?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Then what we're going to do is we will now move to virtual testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please wait for your name to be called to testify and please select unmute when prompted.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Caitlin Gamble.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Caitlin Gamble, an Upper West Side resident, testifies remotely about seeing horses working in 90+ degree heat and calling it a quality of life issue for residents who must witness animal cruelty.
chapters.title: Caitlin Gamble (UWS resident) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Caitlin Gamble.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: I'm a resident of the Upper West Side.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: I used to live near the entrance of 72nd Street and Central Park, where a few horses, maybe three to five, would line up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: I would pass them several times a day while walking my dog, and it was distressing to see the carriage horses, particularly on hot days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: I walked by at least once when a carriage horse was starting out on a ride when the temperature was over 90 degrees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: As a New Yorker, I do not want to witness animal cruelty as I just try to go about my day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: In addition to the horse carriages being a horror for horses, it is a quality of life issue for residents who must witness the cruelty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Caitlin Gamble
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next is Valvoa Manson.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Sorry, Manson Dubin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Are you on?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you want to testify?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Valvoa?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right, let's move on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Clara Tav Akoli-Gosh.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Clara Tavakoli-Gosha, a multi-species ethicist with a Harvard master's, testifies remotely that lack of oversight is devastating, that no one truly enjoys the carriage experience, and proposes museum-like immersive historical experiences as cruelty-free alternatives.
chapters.title: Clara Tavakoli-Gosha testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Clara Tavakoli-Gosha, and I am a multi-species ethicist with a master's from Harvard University.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: My work involves addressing the dominance of the anthropocentric worldview with respect for all species.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: If these horses were truly cared for and respected, they would not be in this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Lack of oversight and transparency for the well-being of horses in the carriage industry is not surprising, but it has been devastating to hear the extent of it in this council meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: When we see a horse attached to a carriage collapsing in the street, there are countless devastated passersby.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: New Yorkers stop to try and help the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Some are crying, some are filming, and time and again we see the driver looking on with an expression of annoyance rather than concern for the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Every sad passerby is not an animal rights activist, as those who exploit horses suggest, to minimize how many people care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Yes, New Yorkers care and are saddened by the continual displays of cruelty to horses being forced to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Tourists expect New York City attractions to be fun and safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Who truly enjoys this?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Not the horses, not the residents, not the tourists, not even the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Wasn't this supposed to be a pleasant experience?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: The way nostalgia works is that we recall something from a bygone time in our lives, often from childhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: No one alive today remembers the 1800s.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: What could be fun is real nostalgia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Instead, imagine unique museum-like spaces designed for immersion and photographs, featuring styles and music from different eras of New York City, providing a historical experience that is actually fun and cruelty-free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: The horses must finally be freed to live at sanctuaries and have a chance to feel relief, no longer forced to work until they collapse and die from pain and exhaustion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: I very much look forward to this band finally being made official.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Clara Tavakoli-Gosha
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Victoria Moran.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Victoria Moran, a 76-year-old New Yorker and author, testifies remotely about decades of advocacy since the 1970s, losing a 16-year-old stepson herself, and urges the council to prevent more carnage.
chapters.title: Victoria Moran (author) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: Hello, I'm Victoria Moran, a New Yorker by choice and an author so grateful to be here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: And it breaks my heart that I'm not there in person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: It's doctor's orders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: As a young woman in Kansas City in the 1970s, I learned about the work of Gretchen Weiler and Pigeon Fitzgerald and this movement to get the carriage horses off the streets of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: New York City, a place that seemed to me like the magical land of Oz.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: I love the idea that even then these people were working to be sure that New York was this inspirational, aspirational, flagship American city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: So I watched through the 80s and the 90s and finally managed to move here to Oz in 2000 and joined the cause myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: I supported candidates who said that they would see that this ended and they didn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: And after the deaths of so many horses and now this precious young human being, it's time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: It's time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: I lost a 16-year-old stepson, and I can tell you, you don't want to know what that feels like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: This could have been prevented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: When I was in my 20s, I'm 76 now, and I'm still waiting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: What you do on this...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: to bring Romacha's law to be, will not bring back lost lives, but you have in your hands the ability to prevent more carnage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: I see there's a class of cultures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: Sometimes there's an argument between loving animals and hating cruelty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: I think we need to bring ourselves to the point where we can do both and make the city indeed livable for all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Victoria Moran
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next is...

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Una Rose.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Una Rose, an animal rights activist from Toronto, testifies remotely about boycotting NYC for 15 years until carriages are banned, proposes electric carriages as replacements, and references ASPCA founder Henry Bergh's legacy.
chapters.title: Una Rose (Toronto activist) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Hi, can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Una.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: I'm from Toronto and I'm a long time animal rights activist and I'm speaking today on behalf of carriage horses and to give my support to the passing of Roma Lux Law and to ban the use of carriage horses in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: It is very important that this ban be passed and all these beautiful horses be retired to reputable sanctuaries and other non-working, loving, forever homes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: They all deserve a nice retirement and that could easily be arranged by the city working together with aligned activists and welfare groups for all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: For the drivers, the city could purchase electric carriages, which other cities around the world have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: They're really popular.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: They look like a lot of fun, and they're green, so they're electric.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: So they would be green as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Historically, passing this bill would be furthering the proud history of New York City as a leader in animal welfare.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: ASPCA was founded by a New Yorker, Henry Berg, in early 1800s, who would walk the streets of New York City berating drivers who abused, neglected, or forced sick animals to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: This decision, passing this bill, would confirm New York City is not only a great city for people, but also a great progressive one for animals as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: And finally, as a tourist, I long to visit New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: I live in Toronto, so it's not much of a journey, but I can.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: I made a pledge to myself about 15 years ago I would not visit New York City until the carriage horse industry was banned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: So it's been 15 years and four mayors, and I'm still waiting for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: It's just, it would be too depressing to go into beautiful Central Park and see these horses that all look sad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: They look bored.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: They look like just full of, just miserable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: That would ruin my trip.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: So I said, no, I'm not going to do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: I'm not going to support a city that supports this kind of animal abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: So crew of kindness to animals is deep.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just to summarize your end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Okay, so let's not let the heroism and tragedy of the young man who died be ignored and denied, and let's not let this bill be delayed and postponed any further.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Let's get this bill passed and let's ban this cruelty because it's the right, smart, and kind thing to do for animals, for horses, for New Yorkers, for tourists, and for New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Una Rose
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sherry Ramsey.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sherry Ramsey.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Sherry Ramsey, co-chair of the NYC Bar Association Animal Cruelty Committee, testifies remotely about 20 years of urging a ban, citing a 2007 Comptroller audit finding inadequate protections, and arguing no legislation can make the city safe for horses.
chapters.title: Sherry Ramsey (NYC Bar Association) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Ah, good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: My name is Sherry Ramsey.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: I'm co-chair of the New York City Bar Association Animal Cruelty Committee, and we're very happy to be here to speak on this topic and be in support of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: intro number 0943 in relation to prohibiting the operation of horse-drawn cabs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: For approximately 20 years or more, the city bar has urged the council to enact legislation to not only ameliorate the extremely harsh conditions under which our city carriage horses work and live, but at the same time, the city bar has argued that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: all of these legislations previously would not create a fully humane condition and that the ban of carriage horse rides was the only humane solution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Despite broad...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Public vocal support for reforms, including the ASPCA and the Maine Society, no legislation can ameliorate some of the conditions which make the city a deadly and inappropriate

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: environment for horses or eliminate the threats to the public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: And as such, this has hurt the horses and served as a betrayal of the public interest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: As indicated by a landmark 2007 audit report by the New York City Comptroller, there's been no at all adequate protection provisions under the current laws that have turned out to be failures so far.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: The administrative code, particularly the audit, found that horses were not provided enough drinking water, risk overheating on hot asphalt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: He also discovered that the horses lacked proper veterinary care and were forced to live in their own waste due to inadequate draining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: He also cited the New York City Department of Mental Health and Hygiene and the New York City Department of Consumer Affairs for their legal noncompliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Moreover, the presence of horses on the city's congested streets have always posed a danger to the public, as is evidenced by the numerous and unceasing instances of spooked horses bolting, putting both horses and people at risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: In a report on the previous time expired, this bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: We identified dozens of...

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just give a summary, because we have to move on.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We have a lot of...

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, thanks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: So there are lots of, although the agriculture and markets laws have prohibited things that should have been addressed through our laws, they haven't been.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: And as a result, the horses get very little good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Treatment and are not being turned out appropriately as is essential for horse care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: So in addition to addressing the problems, there are things that we think would be helpful in getting rid of the care of horses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We need to wrap it up like a couple of sentences.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just tell us where you're at and we have to move on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: So the ban would reduce the city's exposure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: They would protect

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: the public from risk, and it would also be the most meaningful thing we can do for the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: So we thank you so much, and in sum, we'd like to support the passage of 0943, which we believe is law.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, and I also want to encourage you, if you have longer testimony, to submit it at testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: I did, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, you did, great, because we want to see the whole thing, and we want to also thank the Bar Association for being helpful to us in this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sherry Ramsey
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Pauline Burns.

chapters.summary: Pauline Burns, president of the NY State Horse Council, testifies remotely that the organization is abstaining on Intro 943, recommends considering Intro 937 instead, and seeks clarification on horse disposition language.
chapters.title: Pauline Burns (NY State Horse Council) testifies remotely, abstains on bill

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: This is Pauline Burns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Can you hear me okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Very good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: I want to thank you for the opportunity to give some testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: I'm president of the New York State Horse Council and would like to address the esteemed members of the New York City Council Health Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: The New York Horse Council was founded in 1966

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: as an umbrella organization for the equine community residing in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Our mission is to create a strong unified voice for all horse interests toward the preservation of a future for horses in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: The New York State Horse Council Board of Directors and its membership is composed of a diverse group of industry professionals

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: making our organization uniquely qualified to assist in all matters pertaining to horses in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: At this time, the New York State Horse Council is abstaining from a direct position on Introduction 943,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: a bill to amend the Administrative Code of the City of New York in relation to prohibiting the operation of horse-drawn cabs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We do recommend consideration of introduction 937.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: New York State Horse Council would like clarification on the intent of language contained in New York City Introduction 943 pertaining to the disposition of licensed horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We agree horses should not be sold for slaughter, and it is in fact illegal in New York State per Agriculture and Markets Law Section 385.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: However, we also recognize that the carriage horses in New York City are privately owned, and as such, their owners are responsible for their humane treatment and disposition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We recognize New York City has evolved since the inception of the carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If you could just summarize the end part, because we...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: This is the end paragraph.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We recognize New York City has evolved since the inception of the carriage industry and that Central Park is now host to millions of tourists and residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We all have a vision for the use of the park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Your supportive enforcement of current regulations pertaining to suspensions and revocations of licenses within the New York City carriage industry will improve the safety of the horses, drivers, passengers, and general public.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We welcome the opportunity for thoughtful discussion so we can work together to find fair and humane paths forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: For the city, the residents, the visitors, the carriage industry, and the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We also recognize that if this- We have to, we can't, we can't, we have to move on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So just tell you a couple of sentences and we have to move on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: This is the end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: We also recognize that there is a proposed phase-out period that will require responsible transition, and we would welcome an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: I also have a list of recommendations that are set forth with the testimony, but thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Nora Marino.

chapters.summary: Nora Marino, an attorney specializing in animal rights litigation, testifies remotely that the carriage industry is entertainment not transportation, that no industry has received government coddling like this bill offers, and that most bolting incidents occur with the driver present.
chapters.title: Nora Marino (attorney) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nora Marino?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Yes, hi, couldn't unmute myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Hi, thank you for staying so late.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: My name is Nora Marino, I'm an attorney.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I've done a lot of animal rights litigation over the years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: And I have two not-for-profit organizations for animals as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: One of them, the Legal Action Network for Animals and the Animal Pools Exposure Fund.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I don't want to repeat what's been going on here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: There's been a lot of great testimony in support of the bill, which I am in support of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I just want to mention a couple of things really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: First of all, some people have referred to the horse-drawn carriage industry as a transportation industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: It's not transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: It's entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: People don't take horse-drawn carriages to get to work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: They don't take horse-drawn carriages to meet their friend or go to dinner or go to an appointment or go to a job interview.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: People take horse-drawn carriages for one reason and one reason only, and it's entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: It is not transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: So that needs to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Also, no industry has ever received this government hand-holding or coddling that this bill is offering the members of the horse-drawn carriage industry who will be seeking other employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Many industries fall by the wayside over the year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: over the years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I mean, think about typewriter repairmen or print news or, I mean, the yellow taxi industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I mean, so many industries become obsolete due to progress.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: This is an industry that actually has the government willing to lend them a hand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I mean, that's extraordinary and very unheard of if you think of the grand scheme of things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I also just want to mention that the horse is bolting, which has been talked about a lot today, so I don't want to get too involved in that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: But most of the incidents where a horse has bolted has occurred with the driver in the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: With Ramage, it happened to have occurred when the driver was not in the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: But I think almost every other bolting incident that I can think of was with the driver in the carriage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: The driver could not control this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You have to summarize.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Everybody has two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have a lot of people that are waiting to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Anyway, so I'm just saying that in or out of the carriage, post or no post, the bolting is a risk to the citizens of the city, the visitors to the park, and the horse's safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: And I'm not going to get into the cruelty to the animals because, number one, I don't have time, but number two, you've heard.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You can submit testimony online, full testimony online.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: I absolutely will.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nora Marino
utterances.text: Please do that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Bye.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nina Marie Perino is next.

chapters.summary: Nina Marie Perino testifies remotely from Florida about signing petitions for years, every mayor promising to ban carriages, and compares the horses' lack of autonomy to being in a concrete prison.
chapters.title: Nina Marie Perino testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nina Marie Perino.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Yes, I just unmuted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I think everything has been covered that I wanted to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I support the bill fully.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Live in Florida, but I'm originally from Long Island, and going into the city, depressing to see the horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I see them here in Florida, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And I've been signing and sharing petitions, calling council members, calling the mayor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: De Blasio promised it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Every mayor that came into office promised to ban horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We're still waiting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I don't know why it has to exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: It's completely unnecessary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And thank you for saying that they are not used for transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: It's purely entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And think about the autonomy that you have that the horses don't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: They don't have a choice of what they're doing, when they eat, if they're cold or if they're hot, if they want to walk around.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Just think about when you're waiting in line somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Imagine that's your whole life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And somebody controls when you go to the bathroom, you go to the bathroom right where you are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: They live in a concrete prison upstairs in a concrete prison.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: That's their life, for carting people's fat asses around.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Have a heart.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Have compassion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Don't just think about your own needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We can have fun without harming animals and other people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I cried through the whole testimony of Raj's family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I was heartbroken.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: It was just so unnecessary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Tried to get this passed for so long.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And knew this would happen, and now it did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And I hate that it has to come to a human life being lost, to people to care about all beings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We're all the same in all the important ways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We all want to be free from pain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We want to be loved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: We want kindness and care and companionship and community and support and freedom.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: And I want to get up, or I want to sit down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I want to lay down now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: I don't want to be told what to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can you please summarize?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nina Marie Perino
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Nathan Semmel.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Nathan Semmel, a District 7 resident, testifies remotely about texting with Majority Leader Abreu who said 'took me some time' on this issue, and warns that 'took me some time' will never be an acceptable response in the future.
chapters.title: Nathan Semmel testifies remotely on need for courage to act

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Good evening, my name is Nathan Semmel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: My wife, Meredith, and I are District 7 residents in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Our representative is Majority Leader Shaun-Abreu.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: When we saw the video that Leader-Abreu posted of his goddaughter, Jada, asking the council to ban horse carriages, we were thrilled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: I immediately texted him to thank him, and he responded right away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: He wrote, my man, took me some time, exclamation point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: That phrase took me some time, got me thinking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Councilmember O'Brie was a good man and a wonderful council member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: And I suspect from our conversations over the years that he's never been comfortable with horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: However, the conviction just wasn't quite there to stick his neck out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: And I suspect there are many members of this committee and council who have felt the same way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Good people, uncomfortable with horse carriages in a modern urban environment, but for various reasons not quite uncomfortable enough to do something about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: To do the right thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: And I responded to the council member and I said, that's okay, the arc of progress bends towards justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: And he immediately wrote back, yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: I'm not here to litigate the past or ask anyone what took them so long or sit in judgment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: I'm here to litigate the past.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Here to celebrate being on the precipice of justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: But I do so with a message to those members who still find themselves not ready or unwilling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: There are now flashing red lights with conspicuous warning signs screaming two things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Horses in New York City parks and streets are unpredictable, and horse carriages kill people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: Members, took me some time will never be an acceptable response or explanation in the future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: To those members who for years have been willing to protect the horses, I thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: To those who it took some time.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just summarize.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: I'm equally grateful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: And one more sentence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: To the gentleman who earlier said that advocates are exploiting Romach's death, you don't get to tell people who have warned for years of this tragedy happening that they are exploiting

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nathan Semmel
utterances.text: when it does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Nancy DeJesus, an 80-year-old Manhattan resident, testifies remotely supporting the ban, citing 60% polling support, listing dead horses, and arguing the bill protects both horses and workers through transition programs.
chapters.title: Nancy DeJesus (80-year-old New Yorker) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nancy DeJesus.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nancy DeJesus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: I was trying to unmute.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: That's okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: My name is Nancy DeJesus, and I am an 80-year-old New Yorker who currently lives in Manhattan, and I'm here to support Intro 943, Romanticist Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Tales of old have romanticized the role of horse and carriage in battles, love stories, and adventures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: While these images are reflected upon with a nostalgic wistfulness, they do not reflect the reality of our present lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: We are in a modern world with different challenges, dangers, and an evolved sense and understanding of what safety for all creatures looks like today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: This is evidenced in polls such as one taken just this month by the Central Park Conservancy, which reported that 60% of New Yorkers support a ban on horse carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: It's also reflected in the numerous and unavoidable tragedies that have occurred year after year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Horses like Ryder and Denise have lived lives of endless brutality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: They're only respite to retire to a concrete soul with minimal ventilation and no room to move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: And they are gone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: And just last month, an 18-year-old visitor to our city, Romach, died during a family vacation on a carriage ride trying to save his mother when the horse carriage then became frightened as a direct result of the dangers and realities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: of our present time, honking horns, relentless traffic, and concrete paths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: And folks will rightfully ask what will happen to the drivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: The provisions in Intro 943 not only ensure safety for the horses and transition out of this cruel and outdated practice, but will also utilize and oversee a workforce development program that will assist these drivers to securely transition to other employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Horses will no longer be victimized and used to create a false sense of nostalgia in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: No more innocent lives will be lost and drivers will not lose their jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Please, endless, don't let the loss of a young man's life and so many horses be without consequence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Those who seek to romanticize this practice can find it in the works of literature from long ago where it should remain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: I implore the members of the City Council to vote in favor of Romach's law and take direct action to prevent additional suffering and loss of life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nancy DeJesus
utterances.text: Please be compassionate and do the right thing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, next is Monica Sharma.

chapters.summary: Monica Sharma testifies remotely supporting the ban, arguing human life cannot be compared to horse life but all lives matter, and that if your livelihood causes someone's death, change it.
chapters.title: Monica Sharma testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Thank you for staying so late.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I've been online since 10 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: And I appreciate what's going on over here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I just want, like everything has almost been covered by everybody who's been talking and I totally am in support of this bill, but I'm so amazed, you know, that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I understand this was originally for the welfare and the well-being of horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I am a pet lover myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I have two rescue dogs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I have two dogs in the past that we've lost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: But I cannot compare the life of my son to a life of my pet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: And I love my pets as much as you can think.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: It's just come down to a human life being in the part of a horse, you know, well-being.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I don't know how we're comparing the two.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: It's very unfortunate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I just hope no mother ever has to think, you know, is my son the same importance as a...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Horse on a street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Horse lives are very important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Dog lives, extremely important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Any pet, any life, extremely important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Everyone has a right to live.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: But don't bring human life down to, you know, thinking with the horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: It's really, really not comparable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: It's sad that, you know, it happened the way it did happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
