utterances.text: Everyone has a right to get a...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Fair living, you know,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: They have a right to livelihood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: They should completely be able to achieve whatever dream they have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: And it's wrong of them to say that they cannot adapt to a new lifestyle or adapt to a thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Everybody adapts to everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: We've been through three different businesses in a lifetime, three different careers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: After my children, I started something else.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Before my life, I had something else.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Everyone changes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Everyone evolves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Everyone can continue to grow into something else.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: If your livelihood is a reason for somebody's death, change it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: It's not worth it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: We are all going to die one day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: But, you know, I would be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: Devastated to know that because of me or my business.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time is up and if you want to just summarize in a couple of sentences, that's fine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I'm just in support of the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: I'm a lot in support of the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: This bill should definitely be passed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: And I appreciate all the councilmen over here and the councilwomen and everybody here who's been so respectful and patient.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Monica Sharma
utterances.text: But I must say that Councilman Gennaro was very rude to the people who did not support him.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I hear you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have to move on.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Melissa Pellicer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Melissa Pellis.

chapters.summary: Melissa Pellicera of One Kind World Foundation testifies remotely supporting the ban, arguing horses' freedom has been stolen, that longevity doesn't confer moral legitimacy, and that human alternatives exist.
chapters.title: Melissa Pellicera (One Kind World) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Yes, good evening, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Good evening, Chair Shulman, Speaker Manin, Council Member Murtay, members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: I'm sorry that I could not attend in person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: My name is Melissa Pellisera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: I'm the founder of One Kind World Foundation, and I respectfully urge you to pass Intro 0943, Romach Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: It has been mentioned multiple times today that horses are prey animals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: They have survived by perceiving danger and fleeing from it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: No regulation or reinforcement mechanism can override millions of years of evolution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: They are meant to traverse vast landscapes, forage freely, and live within enduring family bands.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: It is their inviolable right to be born and to live free, and we have stolen this right from them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Every human enterprise built upon the use of horses begins with the same assumption, that another living being's freedom may be subordinated to our desires.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: There is a reason the traditional expression is to break a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Before a horse can serve our purpose, its own will must first be overcome.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Throughout this hearing, it was suggested that those of us who defend animals seek to remove them from human lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: That misunderstands our position entirely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: We do not seek to remove them from human lives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: remove horses from the human story.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: We seek to remove oppression from theirs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Let us call this what it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: It is animal exploitation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: These are magnificent sentient beings, and they were never ours to use.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Their lives belong to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: It was said today that horse-drawn carriages have been part of New York City since 1858.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: History is not only a record of traditions we preserve, it is also a record of practices humanity eventually found the courage to abandon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Longevity has never conferred moral legitimacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Human alternatives exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: We have the ingenuity and compassion.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You have to summarize because we've got to move on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Thank you, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: I just wanted to thank you for your time today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: And that's it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Melissa Pellicera
utterances.text: Thank you so very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You're very welcome.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Jennifer Garrett.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Jennifer Garrett, an attorney and Catskill Animal Sanctuary board member, testifies remotely that the industry is beyond regulation, the ban needs to happen faster, and thanks the council for its courage.
chapters.title: Jennifer Garrett (attorney/sanctuary board member) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Jennifer Garrett.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: It's an honor to speak with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: Thank you for staying so late.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: I'm an attorney, a volunteer for voters for animal rights, and I serve on the board of Catskill Animal Sanctuary in Saugerties, New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: I'm testifying today in my personal capacity as a New Yorker in strong support of Intro 943, Romant's Law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: I moved to New York 16 years ago, and I've been walking past the horses in the park ever since.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: Without a doubt, these horses are neglected and abused, and they are forced to work in dangerous conditions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: As we have seen, way too many have died on the street, and those that haven't, they often disappear or are sold to auction or slaughter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: We should all be embarrassed and outraged that with their one life, we have treated horses in New York City in this horrendous way for so long.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: I've spent my career in law and risk management, and I wish to make the point clear that this industry is beyond regulation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: This city has issued additional regulations for decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: The safe, responsible, necessary path forward is a wind down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: If anything, this ban needs to happen faster.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: I wish to thank Speaker Menon, Chair Shulman, Councilmember Marte, the Animal Welfare Caucus, and every co-sponsor of Intro 943, including my council member, Julie-Won, for their leadership and compassion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: History will remember this council for its courage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: truly believe that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: You certainly are my heroes already.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: As a premier global city, New York City, we owe it to the millions of people who come from around the world to visit and to our residents, human and animal, to put an end to this cruel, outdated, unsafe industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: Our city is better than this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: We have a duty to prevent future tragedies, and other cities around the world will look to us to set the example.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: With the passage of Romach's law, you will save lives and you will honor Romach in the strongest way possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: We need to act now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Garrett
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm a big fan of V4.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Fiona Walsh is next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Fiona Walsh.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Faith Rashid.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Faith Rashid, a lifelong New Yorker with background in occupational therapy, testifies remotely about hippotherapy as a potential retirement path for carriage horses and urges passage of the ban.
chapters.title: Faith Rashid testifies remotely on hippotherapy as retirement option

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: I'm Faith Rashid, concerns private citizen, lifelong New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: It's time to pass Raw Matches Law, Intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: I care deeply about this issue because I feel horses should not be used purely for entertainment and profit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Horse carriage rides are a threat to the health and safety of humans and horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Too many horses have suffered and died in this industry, and unfortunately now 18-year-old Ramat Mahajang, who had his whole life ahead of him, was tragically killed on June 16, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: My deepest condolence go to his family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: My background in education and occupational therapy has shown me that the benefits of hippotherapy and equine-assisted therapy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Hippotherapy is a medical treatment strategy utilizing horses'natural rhythmic movement to engage and improve a patient's sensory, neuromotor, and cognitive systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: It is widely used as a modality treatment for physically and developmentally disabled children and teens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: This therapy is also used by veterans who are healing from psychological and emotional trauma.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Carriage horses can successfully...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: transition to hippotherapy or equine assisted programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Hippotherapy requires a calm, docile horse with a low flight response.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Retired horses, of course, are evaluated to see if they do have the right temperament.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: They will have the opportunity to be well taken care of and loved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: These centers are mostly located at equine centers and farm-like facilities often run by licensed therapists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: My daughter and myself have volunteered at two different centers here on Long Island, so I've seen the conditions at these facilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: They truly are very wonderful programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: The horse carriage industry is an archaic tradition that several major U.S.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Cities have already banned or are actively phasing out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: To protect animal welfare and public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: It's time for New York to pass Romach's law and transition and explore animal-free electric alternatives so carriage drivers can transition to new employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: There was a time and place for the horse carriage rise, but that was in the past.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: So please pass this legislation now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faith Rashid
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and giving me the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We appreciate your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Endo Yuki.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Pauline Burns
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Endo Yuki.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Emily Harding, H-A-R-T-I-N-G.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Emily Harding, who worked at a Clydesdale farm, testifies remotely about preparing horses for parades through desensitization, and contrasts that with NYC carriage horses being abused, kept in tenement-like stalls, and forced to work in brutal conditions.
chapters.title: Emily Harding (former Clydesdale farm worker) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: When I was a kid, until I was in my mid-20s, I worked at a Clydesdale farm in Massachusetts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: These one-ton animals were my best friends.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: They were sensitive, loving, and intelligent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: I was so lucky to get a chance to work with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: One of the biggest things we looked forward to every year was driving our horses with the wagon in the town's 4th of July parade.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We would prepare for weeks to desensitize the horses to things like fun snaps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We would play loud music.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We showed them balloons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We threw hard candies at their feet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Anything we could think of that they might encounter, we showed them so they wouldn't be afraid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: When we prepared for the parade, we would bathe and shine them up until they were glowing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We braided their manes and tails in red, white, and blue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We would walk the parade route as proud as could be with our friends who were looking their best and behaving admirably.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: We were a crowd favorite and something people looked forward to seeing every year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: I say this to underscore that I have no problem with horses being used to pull wagons, carts, or plows.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: No problem with riding or otherwise working with and using horses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Partnership that develops between a horse and their human is beautiful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: I know I've been a part of one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: I have a problem with the way that carriage horses in the city are treated, the conditions in which they are kept, and the environment in which they must work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: They're abused, forced to work when they're lame, sore, old, injured, or sick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: They are kept in tenement buildings with...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: No room to lie down or to rest their weary legs, tenements that we long ago deemed unfit for human life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: They are driven through traffic in midtown Manhattan, a place where I, as a person who learned to drive in Boston, is still nervous to drive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: In all types of weather, including brutal heat and humidity, when we're told by newscasters to avoid going outdoors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Pounding on the pavement, expected to plod along no matter what, only to return to a hot, dark, airless stall where they can't lay down and rest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: This is antiquated, abusive, and unethical.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just can you please summarize and then give us your name also at the end?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: I just want to also say that I'm so heart sick for the Romach family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: What a living nightmare that they are experiencing and I'm holding them in my prayers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: It is beyond high time that we outlawed the practice of horse-drawn carriages in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Please pass Romach's law.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And your name for the record?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Emily Harding, H-A-R-T-I-N-G.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much, and thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Emily Harding
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Lauren Lind.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Dr. Lauren Lindner, a clinical psychologist and sanctuary owner, testifies remotely about the cost of sanctuary care (~$6,100/year per horse), her willingness to take carriage horses, and her childhood trauma from seeing horses in Central Park.
chapters.title: Dr. Lauren Lindner (psychologist/sanctuary owner) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Checking to see if you can hear me or not.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, we can.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And please keep it to two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We have a lot of people waiting to testify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I shall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: This is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Lauren Lindner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I'm in support of the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: And I'm a native New Yorker, grew up riding horses in Central Park from the age of seven, Claremont Stables, Dixie Do in Queens, been riding and caring for horses all my life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I moved to California to complete my graduate school education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: And I'm going to turn my video on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: And also because I wanted to start a horse sanctuary based on the love that I developed for horses as a child.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I want to provide two different perspectives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: One is that as a sanctuary owner and as an ethologist, an animal behaviorist, I know what it takes to provide an ideal life for a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I have studied equine behavior, equine ecology for the past 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Horses may be bred to pull carriages, but that doesn't mean that's what they want to do day after day in an environment that is simply not conducive for horses, albeit their ability to adapt, reluctantly or not, with blinders and curb bits and whips,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: to many of the things humans ask them to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Once you have the land, however, and the pasture and barn and fencing and all the other appropriate infrastructure, it does not cost more than $6.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: $1,000 a year for a reliable and reputable sanctuary to provide a home for a horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: My sanctuary is willing to provide homes for several of the horses from the carriage industry, and their former owners are welcome to continue to visit with them should they so desire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: The other perspective that I want to offer is that I'm not only an animal behaviorist, but I'm also a human behaviorist, a licensed clinical psychologist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: And as a child, I was personally traumatized by seeing the horses in Central Park pulling carriages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I didn't understand how they were allowed to be in the sun, in the rain, on concrete, with feed bags.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I never saw them getting water.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I was in Central Park several times a week.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: I asked my parents repeatedly why this was allowed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: And I've found since that others, children and adults, are also distressed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Please summarize that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Summarize the end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: My summary of that, yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Traditions are often changed once we recognize the cruelties behind them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lauren Lindner
utterances.text: Let's go ahead and change this one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Next is Cynthia Bathurst.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Cynthia Bathurst.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Cindy Anthony Favia.

chapters.summary: Cindy Anthony Favia testifies remotely about her two children being animal rights activists since ages 7 and 9, her daughter questioning why horses are in the city, and 10+ years of protesting carriage horses.
chapters.title: Cindy Anthony Favia testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: I'll be right back.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are you?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Hello?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Wait.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Hello?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: I'm just getting my paper here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Well, my name is Cindy Anthony Fabian.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And I'm...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: So the reason why I'm speaking here is because I have two children that have been involved in the animal rights community since they're seven and nine years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: They are now 20 and 22.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And they have stood on the line as animal rights activists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: When my daughter was a little girl, she saw the carriage horses in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And she had said, why are those horses here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Aren't they supposed to be where there's grass and there's green fields and everything like that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Why are they breathing up all that garbage from the buses and everything?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And she used to always question that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And then.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: We started standing there and asking, why are they still there?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And so the reason they've stood there for all those years, they have gone and protested and everything through the blase on everybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: So they've been involved in this for 10 plus years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: The carriage horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: has to be out of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And it needs to end now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: And it's not a form of transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: My daughter told me that when she was nine years old, that that is not transportation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: We have buses clearly that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Everybody could see trains, cabs, pedicabs, everything like that, their own feet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: The horses are not here to be used by anybody like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: So that's the reason why I stood here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: We don't need any more lost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Humans or animals and you need to pass please please for my children's sake

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Please pass intro 943.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Let the horses, let them live out happy lives now, please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: I thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: Thank you for allowing me to talk and appreciate everything.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Car Pelletieri.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Corpelatory.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Allison Soul.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Allison Soule, an Upper West Side resident, testifies remotely about witnessing malnourished, lame horses with drivers staring at phones, the Amish country pipeline, and over 500 horses leaving the registry in eight years with no verification of retirement.
chapters.title: Allison Soule (UWS resident) testifies remotely on horse pipeline and turnover

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Good evening, members of the Committee on Health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: My name is Allison Soule.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: I'm a native New Yorker, and I live on the Upper West Side.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Menon, for your historic leadership.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: By backing Roe v.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Wanch's law last night, you proved this council will no longer look the other way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: For too long, the horse carriage industry has scapegoated its tragedies, from decades of horse deaths, including Ryder, to the devastating loss of Ramansh Mahajan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Unbiased polls have consistently shown 70% of New Yorkers want the exploitation to end.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: As a daily Central Park dog walker who tries to avoid the West 72nd Street entrance to spare my own heartbreak, I routinely witness the reality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Many drivers sit staring into their phones, ignoring horses that appear malnourished and lame.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Exhibiting the classic head jerk and leg raise of an animal in pain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: The industry in part defends this with mandated five-week furloughs to the country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: But let's be honest about the pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Many are originally purchased from Pennsylvania.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Pennsylvania Amish country after years of exhausting farm labor, where whipping is well documented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Even if they are allegedly resting during furlough, that environment follows them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: One driver admitted to me that his horse returned with a fresh whip gash on its nose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: After logging long days on concrete, these horses walk congested city streets back to cramped, poorly ventilated, fire-hazardous stables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: DOH data shows a massive turnover rate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Records compiled by animal welfare groups showed over 500 horses left the registry over an eight-year span.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Horses are routinely dumped back into auctions, facing farm labor or slaughter, with no verification of true retirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Urban carriage horses are purely a tourist attraction, not a city's necessity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Please pass Intro 943, mandate proof of safe retirement, and transition drivers into alternative employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: A revanche and brighter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Allison Soule
utterances.text: I thank you for your consideration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Audra Gibson is next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Anthony Favia
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Audra Gibson, who worked in Charleston's carriage industry for 4.5 years, testifies remotely opposing the ban, describing rigorous horse care protocols including temperature monitoring between tours, and arguing the industry can be regulated for safety.
chapters.title: Audra Gibson (former Charleston carriage worker) testifies remotely opposing ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: Hello, good evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: My name for the record is Audra Gibson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: I am very much in the minority in comparison to those who have testified that I am here to oppose the March's law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: I think what happened to that young man is devastating.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: But was an accident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: Background-wise, I work in Midtown Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: Prior to that, I lived in Charleston, South Carolina for 20 years, and for approximately four and a half of those years, worked in the carriage industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: It was my job to make sure that the horses got enough rest between tours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: Constantly in front of water.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: I measured their breathing respirations between tours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: I made sure that their temperatures were taken between every single tour.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: If a temperature was too high, we immediately pulled those horses off of tour rotation and cooled them down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: We were responsible for vet care,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: farrier care, dental equine care, and more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: There's absolutely zero percent of the industry that I was involved with that was anything close to animal cruelty or abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: And I find it offensive that so many people

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: people have just blatantly accused people in the industry of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: We loved those horses and animals like members of our family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: I saw many carriage owners spend thousands of dollars taking care of animals that they knew would never go back on tour, never be back in the industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: To make sure that they were well cared for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: There is an unfair standard that is being held here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: Over 100 people a year are killed crossing the street in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: There are people that fall from

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson

utterances.text: To conclude, I would say that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: We're the most highly regulated industry in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: And so you can regulate around this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Audra Gibson
utterances.text: You can regulate for safety and do so successfully.

utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: D. Shroff, a union member, testifies remotely supporting the ban, arguing horses can't consent or call in sick, that 70% of New Yorkers support the ban, and criticizing TWU's comparison of horses to subway conductors.
chapters.title: D. Shroff (union member) testifies remotely supporting ban

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: My name is D.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: Shroff, and I'm a part of the vast majority of New Yorkers who are sick and tired of witnessing these horses being exploited in our streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: And as a proud union member, I also understand how vital it is to ensure that those working in this industry are supported as they transition into other employment once horse carriages are finally phased out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: I encourage the remaining past leaders who have put their name in support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: To put their name in support of this bill and listen to the 70% of New Yorkers that support the end of this industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: Find the courage to stand up against the transit workers'union.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: And to the TWU representatives comparing horse carriage rides to other forms of transit, there is an obvious difference here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: A subway conductor or a bus driver who isn't feeling well stays home from work until they feel better.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: A horse can't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: They have zero consent in the matter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: These horses are forced to work their bodies into the ground whether they like it or not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: It's no surprise when you see them collapse and die on our streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: You can say you love horses all you want, but at the end of the day, the fact is you are exploiting these animals for profit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: New Yorkers go to our city parks to escape the chaos of the big city, to clear our minds, breathe in fresh air, and surround ourselves in nature.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: And we are sick and tired of being forced to witness this cruelty in our streets and in our park.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: Enough is enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: And lastly,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: To Representative Gennaro, thank you for perfectly embodying the dying death rattle of this outdated horse carriage industry today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - D. Shroff
utterances.text: Peace to all.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Really appreciate the testimony.

chapters.summary: Daniel Chapuis, son of a Swiss farmer, testifies remotely opposing the ban, sharing family history with horses, threatening to sue the mayor and council for $25 billion, and arguing tourists deserve real horses not electric vehicles.
chapters.title: Daniel Chapuis testifies remotely opposing ban, threatens lawsuit

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Hey, yo.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is that Danny?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Daniel, stop me.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You have two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Okay, my testimony is in one minute, 50 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Please don't interrupt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: I'm the son of a Brazilian Swiss farmer from people where the corn is used to make the gruyere and the ashram that compose the famous, world famous cheese fondue as well as the Nescafe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: My father farmed on the horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: After World War II, his father got hit by a truck backing up and he didn't wish to die.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: It was too much for my grandma and five kids.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: They set the farm and moved to my hometown, Geneva.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: They had a front job in construction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: The only daughter, even my grandmother, took care of grandma and cooked for the four boys.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: During World War II, my father was on the horse at the Swiss-German border.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: While she helped the peasants in the ashtray, and in return, the farmer gave my lovely dad a banana.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: In Fribourg, they knew only the oranges for Christmas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: So he gave the banana to his horse and he didn't have any way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: While visiting me, we took the horse carriage's ride in Central Park, also featuring a picture together with the police mounted horse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: In 1986, I was very excited to see the horse carriages in front of the Irish restaurant, Hosea O'Grady, at 32nd and 7th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Tourists to visit all ages, including...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Silver blood them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: Everywhere now we have these company dogs in the supermarket, the MTA, buses and subways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: They bark most of the time and I get aggravated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: We have to preserve the hostage charges because of the human values.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Daniel Chapuis
utterances.text: The foreigner spent a billion of dollars to get something better than an AI-driven motor vehicle.

chapters.summary: Chair Schulman closes the hearing, noting written testimony can be submitted within 72 hours, thanking council members, staff, and Speaker Menin for her leadership through the long day of testimony.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman closes the hearing

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you to everyone who has testified.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If there's anyone present in the room or on Zoom that has not had the opportunity to testify, please raise your hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Use the Zoom raise hand function.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Use the Zoom raise hand function.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Seeing no one else, I would like to note that written testimony, which will be reviewed in full by committee staff, may be submitted to the record up to 72 hours after the close of this hearing by emailing it to testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I also want to thank Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank Chris Marte.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank Councilmember Phil Wong, who came and left and came back.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These are the administrative staff.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank the council administrative staff, Michelle Otis, Raquel Faria, Mark...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Bermudez.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I also want to thank the committee staff again, Christopher Pepe, Elizabeth Arts, Josh Newman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's been a very long day and I want to thank my team, Jonathan Boucher,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Kevin McAleer, Sammy New, who's here, went and came back.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It was very grueling testimony.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We got a lot done today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank everyone for all of their patience.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I particularly want to thank the speaker, Julie-Menin, for her leadership on this and for helping get us through all of this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And with that, I am concluding this hearing.
